### Post by lkrory21 on 2010-05-10
Hi all!

It seems that my Ubuntu 10.04 (clean install) to randomly freeze / hang (mouse/keyboard unresponsive, only a hard reboot is available as option) but with no garbled screens/windows/etc. There's no pattern why or any application that could be responsible. It could happen ten minutes after it is started or after 2 days but at some point and suddenly the system will become totally unresponsive, freeze and hang up. I used the same PC with Karmic Koala and releases before that (sometimes on for days) with no such problem so it shouldn't be a hardware problem.

I thoroughly check system logs after the hard reboot (*System > Administation > Log File Viewer*) and there's no entry around the time of each freeze / hang to pinpoint me to something.

The only thing that is certain is that the following errors / warnings always appear in .xsession-errors file:

1.
*Window manager warning: Failed to read saved session file /home/lkrory21/.config/metacity/sessions/1080c45259ed06f712734738133612800000012480026.ms: Failed to open file '/home/lkrory21/.config/metacity/sessions/1080c45259ed06f712734738133612800000012480026.ms': No such file or directory*

2.
[I](polkit-gnome-authentication-agent-1:1321): GLib-GObject-WARNING **: cannot register existing type `_PolkitError'

(polkit-gnome-authentication-agent-1:1321): GLib-CRITICAL **: g_once_init_leave: assertion `initialization_value != 0' failed[/I]

3.
[I]** (update-notifier:1457): DEBUG: Skipping reboot required
[/I]
4. (If I use Chrome)
([I]exe:1524): Gdk-WARNING **: XID collision, trouble ahead
[/I]
5. (If I use Firefox, in this case the file is literally flooded [I]with this warning)
(firefox-bin:1579): Gdk-WARNING **: XID collision, trouble ahead[/I]

Here is also some info about my system:
M/B: ASUS P4R800-V DELUXE
CPU: Intel(R) Pentium(R) 4 CPU 3.00GHz
RAM: 1GB
Graphics: ATI  Radeon 9100 IGP

Any suggestions what else I might check? 

Thanx a lot in advance!

### Post by dino99 on 2010-05-10
first check: system admin hardware driver, is yours activated ?
then try without: flash, compiz, java if installed

you have some of the not yet fixed bugs :(

metacity --replace

sudo dpkg --configure -a

install gconf-cleaner and use it (yes to all)
install bleachbit and use it "as admin" but take time to read and understand every setting (in doubt dont check the selected setting)

---

### Post by ld0d on 2010-05-11
I was able to get my PS/2 mouse and keyboard working again, at least once, by simply reloading the psmouse module remotely.

```
sudo rmmod psmouse
sudo modprobe psmouse

```I found this out just some 10 minutes ago, so I'm not sure if it will work again next time.

I haven't found any error messages in any logs, but suddenly the mouse and keyboard just stop working.

---

### Post by frncz on 2010-05-12
> **lkrory21 said:**
> 
@frncz  How can I check if it is running and how can I remove it completely?

Cheers!

If you go to System -> Administration -> System Monitor, click on Processes and scroll down to ubuntu, you may see one or two processes with the word ubuntuone in them. 
I you highlight these and right click, you will have several options, including, Stop, End or Kill the processes. I expect any of these will do, but not doubt Kill will be the most radical.

However, this may not be the answer as reported by rueben

My own freezes are quite rare, and usually happen when the children are using the computer, so I may have to do some more detective work. 

Regards

Mike

### Post by aphatak on 2010-05-13
Been having the PC freeze on me like all the other folks.  Some pointers -
1) This is a clean install, updated as of this morning.
2) If I stay in the console (ctl-alt-F2), the PC does not freeze.  I can install packages (nfs, ntfsprogs and a variety of others), and do all kinds of stuff from the command line, no problems, no freeze.
3) If I do not log into the Gnome-session, the PC does not freeze.  The log in screen can be active for half hour or longer.  The mouse works, the keyboard works.
4) If I do log in, and do nothing, the PC does not freeze.  Within minutes (usually less than 5) of starting any application - Firefox, System Monitor, you name it - bang, the keyboard and mouse go into a coma.
5) Even when the keyboard and mouse are frozen, the PC responds to pings - and pretty quickly, too.
6) If I start an X-session from another PC in the local network,  that session does not freeze - at least for the length of time I have been checking.  The only weird thing is the window frame - it shows random patterns instead of a nice solid color.
What I do know is, it's not hardware.  Windows XP Pro and Ubuntu 8.04 LTS keep working for hours.
If an expert reads this, will she (or he, I don't care!) please tell me what I should dump and send?  Or at least pray for my PC?

---

### Post by aphatak on 2010-05-13
An update -

1) When the keyboard and mouse are frozen, I can no longer start a Gnome session from another computer on the network.  However, I *can* start an SSH session remotely.  Works exactly as an SSH session should work.

2) I installed the base KDE environment.  No freeze, no lock-up.

I have two other computers running Ubuntu 10.04 64-bit, and I have had absolutely no problem with those two.  Neither of these computers are ever switched off, and they get re-booted only when a new kernel version is installed.

I would conclude from this experience that the Gnome Desktop Environment trips over some hardware feature and falls flat on its face.  The hardware is fine, the kernel works, and X is healthy too.  Any comments?

### Post by Neo2 on 2010-05-14
It's not just Internet, although it is quicker using FF than without. Happened once just by moving the mouse! The 'Xfce4 packages' sounds interesting, how to please! Sorry for being a newb, the more I ask the more I learn!

---

### Post by chantrelle on 2010-05-14
> **Neo2 said:**
> It's not just Internet, although it is quicker using FF than without. Happened once just by moving the mouse! The 'Xfce4 packages' sounds interesting, how to please! Sorry for being a newb, the more I ask the more I learn!

Xfce is a window manager just like Gnome and KDE, but light weight in comparison.  Xubuntu is ubuntu with Xfce as the default WM rather than Gnome which is the usual default for the standard Ubuntu distro. I would recommend giving it a try, in that it isn't all that different or exotic from the other WM, just stripped down. Select it from synaptics and do an install, and when you boot your machine and reach the login window, select Xfce as your window manager.

While the freezing of the screen and hard reboots have been reduced, they are still there.  My original optimism is now gone, but at least I can get some work done where before under Gnome the machine was unusable. 

Give Xfce a try and see if it helps. The suspicion is that this is on the kernel level, and it will only be a matter of time before this is reconciled.

Best,

William

---

### Post by melendyk on 2010-05-14
Not sure where the freezing is happening w/ 10.04, but I can tell you this, I've installed on three different machines from the same CD (Ubuntu 10.04 Desktop 64 bit) and all are using Gnome and all using Nvidia drivers.  Only one of the machines freezes... that one acutally froze twice during the installation process random places (during selections) and had to restart it.  Seems to loose keyboard / mouse awareness...

Machine 1)  Desktop - Biostar Mobo w/ integrated Nvidia video, AMD Athlon X2 BE2400, 4 gig gskill memory.  This machine is my file server, however I use this machine all day as well.  Surf the net, virtual windows installs to test other software, you name it...  some weird things happen with firefox from a url/goole search typing auto highlight which require me to close it and reopen it, but then it works fine for a while before I need to close and re-open it.  It never does freeze though.

Machine 2)  HP Laptop - Intel Core 2 Duo T5550, Nvidia Gforce 8400M video card, 4 gig ram.  Don't use this as much, but hasn't ever froze up.

Machine 3)  ... The problem child, freezes all the time... ASUS P7P55D LGA 1156 Intel P55 ATX Intel Motherboard, Intel Core i5 750 Lynnfield, 4 Gig GSkill Trident memory, Nvidia GeForce 8800 GT video card.  This machine us unusable with Ubuntu 10.04.  Freezes all the time.  Doesn't matter if I am surfing or not.  Not ctl + alt + whatever, ctl + shift + whatever works.  If I hit the power button, the window pops up for reboot, shutdown, logoff options, but cant do any keyboard or mouse navigation...  beyond that seems like it might fine other than ignoring keyboard and mouse...  Don't know, but very upsetting...

All these machines ran 9.10 prior.  1 and 2 ran 8.10 and 8.04LTS as well

---

### Post by melendyk on 2010-05-14
OK, so this is interesting... my "problem child"  just "froze up"  Both the keyboard and mouse are PS/2,  I plugged a usb mouse and keyboard into the machine and I got control back.  This maybe a different issue, not sure.  If I unplug the usb devices and go back to the PS/2... nothing...  Another weird thing that just stated happening, upon boot up, it says network is unplugged.  If I uncheck enable networking and then recheck it, all works fine until a reboot... what the!

OK... my ps/2 keyboard mouse set is usb w/ ps/2 adapter... switch to using just usb and no more problems so far (except the network thing)... lets keep fingers crossed.  Was thinking maybe I'll take a look in the bios to see what is there about usb/ps/2 preferences...

---

### Post by winecurmudgeon on 2010-05-14
I am beginning to wonder if it is a Gnome desktop problem as well. My keyboard freeze only seems to happen when I am typing in Pidgin or Thunderbird, but not in Open Office. The mouse freeze, these days, is less common. But the last time it happened, it came while I was playing Tali, the Gnome desktop dice game.

Of course, my expertise is limited, and it may well be a kernel problem. And at one time thought it was an Nvidia problem.

### Post by aphatak on 2010-05-14
> **Neo2 said:**
> Interesting. For my benefit, what is the KDE desktop? What do you mean when you 'were in KDE'? I have done software updates already using Synaptic but hasn't cured anything. Haven't tried to update desktop though. FYI, I did look at what proprietary drivers I had installed and nothing appeared in the box, so nothing to update. Could that be a problem?

KDE is an alternative desktop environment (Ubuntu comes with Gnome by default).  You can install it in addition to (not necessarily instead of) Gnome, or install Kubuntu which comes with the KDE environment as standard (without Gnome).  Both are good, it's merely a matter of what you are used to.  There are other desktop environments, for example xfce.

To install KDE along with Gnome, here's what you do.  If you use the command line, enter -
  sudo apt-get install kubuntu-desktop
If you prefer the Synaptic GUI, find 'kubuntu-desktop' and then install it.  When asked for, retain the GDM as your display manager.

When Ubuntu presents you with a log-in screen, select your ID; a drop-down box lets you select the environment (KDE or Gnome).  Select KDE, enter your password and hit 'login'.

When I said I was in KDE, I meant I was using the KDE desktop environment.  Gnome used to freeze on me, but KDE didn't, which was the reason I did that in the first place.

I am not sure what you meant by updating the desktop.  What I did was (a) invoked Synaptic, (b) found 'ubuntu-desktop', (c) marked it for re-installation and (d) hit 'apply'.  This removes the Gnome desktop, and installs it again.

I wouldn't worry about not having proprietary drivers (in fact I would be relieved!).  I don't have any, either.  These are a necessary evil - hardware manufacturers that want to keep their interface a secret, may sometimes publish a binary-only driver package for their hardware;  this allows the hardware to be used under Linux (at least the distribution which can use the package).  I find them more pain than purity.  If the manufacturer does not update the driver, publish the version you can use (such as 32-bit and 64-bit), or does not fix bugs, you - the user - are stuck.

Let me know if I am being obscure - it's one of my many failings.

### Post by ntucker on 2010-05-14
Similar problems for me.  Not scientific at all, but I think there may be a correlation between lockups and network usage.  So far all the lockups have occurred while I was doing something like constantly pinging a bunch of hosts, or I was logged into a remote machine and doing something with constant output like running 'top' or a 'tail -f' on a lively log file.  Of course, these are things I do most of the time, so my mind could just be desperately grasping for associations.  But back on hardy with this same hardware I used to be able to pretty consistently lock up the whole machine by trying to copy a couple GB of data to another host on my wireless network, so I've been suspicious of the ath5k driver for a while.

The symptom for me is that my X graphics on my primary monitor suddenly go away and instead, I see a TTY with some old console messages on it (e.g. the same thing I'd see if I hit ctrl-alt-F1), but the machine is unresponsive, cannot be pinged, etc.  The secondary monitor keeps displaying X graphics but is obviously frozen.  Hard reboot is necessary, and after a reboot, there are generally no recent messages from before the crash in any of the logs (syslog, debug, daemon, auth, xorg, etc), and if there are, they're standard irrelevant stuff (e.g. a cron job ran).

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-14
> **ntucker said:**
> Not scientific at all, but I think there may be a correlation between lockups and network usage.

There does seem to be a correlation, but I suspect other I/O in addition to the network - if I mount Samba shares, then my machine is guaranteed to freeze after the next resume, whether I umount the shares before suspension or leave them open. The same applies if I insert an SD-memory card or a USB memory card, or mount a foreign filesystem partition - and again, it does not prevent a freeze to umount or remove these devices before resuming.

Incidentally, I see no hardware in common with the listings above, although hardware seems the logical problem if most people do not have this problem and it occurs on an unmodified fresh install for those who have it.

---

### Post by smspiff1 on 2010-05-14
Been reading the messages, but haven't jumped in till now 'cause I thought my problem may have been unique. Now I think it may be the same/related issue, and I hope my descriptions below can help in narrowing down a solution.

**Keyboard**: I too have a keyboard problem, but I get a system beep every time I hit a key, i.e., not no response, just a beep per keystroke.  It was working fine, then it went into this system beep mode on/around Tues 5/11. BTW, the keyboard _does_ work to login, but then it just beeps thereafter.

**SSH**: Since the keyboard doesn't work, I can, and do, consistently ssh into my logged into machine.  I do this from an external terminal (on a Mac Host), and this is the way I've been "using" my machine since the keyboard went offline.

**Power Mgmt**: I've changed my power management preferences to "never", but, like others have reported, that doesn't seem to help.  However, recently, after re-booting over and over again trying to get the keyboard to work, I get a message (from my virtualization host) that the "poweron" script has changed. I haven't changed anything on the Host or Ubuntu (client), but something has changed on the Ubuntu side.

**Setup**: I've using been Ubuntu as a virtual client since April of 2008 with no problems. (The host is MacBook Pro).  I upgraded to 10.04 on April 30th, and had no problems 'till approx last Tuesday (5/11). Until then, it had been working fine. I do power down on occasion, but "luckily" I had been running Ubuntu for around 4 days straight when the keyboard went awry and started beeping.

I know with virtualization that I'm coming at it from a different way. But, and as others have discussed, I think this helps present a strong case that it may not be a hardware problem.

I hope this helps, and I, too, need a solution soon -- or I will have to resort to my backed up 8.04!

### Post by lkrory21 on 2010-05-15
Hi everybody and thanx for your valuable input!

> Incidentally, I see no hardware in common with the listings above, although hardware seems the logical problem if most people do not have this problem and it occurs on an unmodified fresh install for those who have it.

**@StuartN** Indeed this is the weirdest part of all! That's why I believe it is something in the kernel (this is also backed by the fact that the random lockups happen in Gnome and in KDE) but since nothing is recorded in system logs around the time of the crash...

I agree with **Ntucker** about the correlation with network usage based on my personal observations on the .xsession-errors file (see several posts of mine above) and also it's not browser specific.

Let me recap the symptoms just to be sure that everybody here is talking about the same bug:

1.System freezes / lockups at random. It could be 10mins after boot or several hours later. Screen freezes as it is or goes black. Mouse / keyboard unresponsive (i.e. caps lock/num lock lights not blinking, etc)

2.Only Hard reboot is available. Several users reported though that system is available to connect remotely via SSH.

3.Absolutely nothing relevant is recorded in system logs around the time of the crash (System > Administration > Log File Viewer)

4.Xsession-errors file is flooded with messages about "XID Collision,XID collision, trouble ahead" if you use the internet (Chrome or Firefox) and tends to grow big in size if the system manages to stay up for hours.

5.This didn't happened with previous Ubuntu versions or other distros (no hardware failure)

Cheers!

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-15
> **lkrory21 said:**
> 2.Only Hard reboot is available. Several users reported though that system is available to connect remotely via SSH.

4.Xsession-errors file is flooded with messages about "XID Collision,XID collision, trouble ahead" if you use the internet (Chrome or Firefox) and tends to grow big in size if the system manages to stay up for hours.

2. Some people can use AltSysReq-REISUB instead of hard reboot, even though the keyboard seems unresponsive and the CapsLock etc do not change the indicator LEDs, which gives a cleaner shutdown. (On the other hand, I can't SSH in or ping my wireless machine, others can).

4. The ~/.xsession-errors message "(firefox-bin:3097): Gdk-WARNING **: XID collision, trouble ahead" and some other messages can be eliminated by changing your screensaver - I changed my screensaver to "Pictures folder", but other settings should work.

I would add that a) the wireless networking is sometimes disabled on reboot and b) there is always a message "INFO: recovery required on readonly filesystem" with several orphan cleanups, but never any recovered files in /lost+found.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-15
This is possibly not related, but what happens if you select "Switch From [username]" in the shutdown menu? This option should save your running session, allow another user to log in, and then restore your session when you next log in. On my machine it is doing a fair impression of the Ubuntu freeze routine.

In .xsession-errors I have the error message *"Window manager warning: Failed to read saved session file /home/stuart/.config/metacity/sessions/10ce4223c261411605127350520720563800000012500026.ms: Failed to open file '/home/stuart/.config/metacity/sessions/10ce4223c261411605127350520720563800000012500026.ms': No such file or directory"* and the directory ~/.config/metacity does not exist. This has been reported as Bug #249373 [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-session/+bug/249373](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnome-session/+bug/249373)

Possibly there is some element of the Gnome session-saving that is causing freezes because part of the session-saving is incomplete - but the big question would be why this would affect some users and not others.

Does user-switching fail on your freeze-affected machine?

### Post by Andrew.Stevenson on 2010-05-15
Hello all,

I'm trying as well to find out what's going on in the realm of frozen land. I'm running debian testing (x86 32bits) with kernel 2.6.32-3-686-bigmem. I'm using a "wired PS/2" keyboard (i did check that it was plugged into the "green" socket), and an usb mouse. I'm experiencing random freezing as well, but *only* my keyboard get frozen, my usb mouse keeps working properly. 
I already posted yesterday a message at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1468620&page=2#15](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1468620&page=2#15) (obsoleted by this post).

i thought "i8042.nopnp" kernel parameter fixed it, but keyboard froze while i was typing this post (haaaaa, sweet irony :).

I will try to summarize the potential leads about this problem. It's really hard for me to test since the
freeze happens really *randomly*, so it is therefore tricky to reproduce and test with new/different kernel parameters, or by blacklisting modules.

I first thought it was a problem related to a p7p55d asus motherboard and kernel 2.6.32, but according to other posts it seems people are experiencing this freeze with different motherboards. A wild guess is that my 2.6.32 kernel is surely not innocent about this problem :)

1/ hpet (UNTESTED):
---------------------------

a possible completely unrelated matter but before freezing, i had this message in dmesg. 

[ 8069.165815] CE: hpet increasing min_delta_ns to 15000 nsec

[   12.169669]  [<c1036b4d>] ? warn_slowpath_common+0x5e/0x8a
[   12.169675]  [<c1036b83>] ? warn_slowpath_null+0xa/0xc
[   12.169680]  [<c101ec51>] ? hpet_next_event+0x49/0x6b
[   12.169685]  [<c10542ce>] ? clockevents_program_event+0xbb/0xc9
[   12.169687]  [<c10550b6>] ? tick_dev_program_event+0x1e/0x81
[   12.169690]  [<c1054c49>] ? tick_broadcast_oneshot_control+0x8f/0xe2
[   12.169693]  [<c10546c3>] ? tick_notify+0x1d6/0x2f7
[   12.169695]  [<c104de42>] ? notifier_call_chain+0x2a/0x47
[   12.169697]  [<c104decb>] ? raw_notifier_call_chain+0x9/0xc
[   12.169700]  [<c1054132>] ? clockevents_notify+0x22/0xd4
[   12.169704]  [<f8740fec>] ? acpi_idle_enter_bm+0x154/0x281 [processor]
[   12.169707]  [<c11c7b89>] ? cpuidle_idle_call+0x68/0xbb
[   12.169710]  [<c10071e8>] ? cpu_idle+0x89/0xa5
[   12.169712] ---[ end trace 8e54e267104ccde8 ]---


i read that using "nohpet" (or "hpet=disable"?) on kernel command line can disable it. 
but i haven't tested it yet to see if it could fix keyboard/mouse freezing.


2/ i8042 parameters (TESTED - failed):
--------------------------------------------------

try playing with these parameters on kernel command line.

i8042.reset
i8042.nomux
i8042.nopnp
i8042.noaux

it seems that for me the "i8042.nopnp" kernel parameter did not solve the freezing problem in the end..

if you need debugging, you can use "i8042.debug" option too, or set it after boot using:

echo 1 > /sys/module/i8042/parameters/debug

after enabling the debug flag, and right after a freeze, i had this kernel dmesg error:

[...] drivers/input/serio/i8042.c: interrupt 1, without any data

in a terminal, i was running 'watch "cat /proc/interrupts | grep i8042"' and could see that the keyboard interrupt #1 wasn't ticking anymore while pressing keys on the keyboard. it is usually a bad sign i guess :)


3/ asus_atk0110 (UNTESTED):
-----------------------------------------

this module is loaded at boot for my p7p55d asus motherboard. detection in dmesg is:

[   12.192796] ATK0110 ATK0110:00: EC enabled

if i remember correctly, i think i read on a forum somewhere that someone blacklisted this module and he wouldn't have the keyboard/mouse freezing anymore.

i didn't try to "rmmod asus_atk0110" or blacklist it so i don't know if it's relevant. besides,
as i said before, it seems that people with different motherboards are having the same problem.


4/ psmouse module (TESTED - worked):
-----------------------------------------------------

after a freeze, i did a:

rmmod psmouse
modprobe psmouse

and it unfroze my keyboard.

as a side note, unpluging/repluging the keyboard doesn't do anything (capslock/numlock/scroll leds all light on though).

5/ ps2 mouse socket (UNTESTED):
-----------------------------------------------

I read somewhere that linux can detect when a ps2 keyboard is plugged into the "purple" mouse socket and that linux 
supports it(?). I think i read somewhere to test this. I didn't try it, especially that i'm not fond of voodoo ritual thingies.

6/ acpi/apm (UNTESTED):
-----------------------------------

maybe acpi/apm/cpu throttling mechanism/PNP trigger the freezing, but I haven't tried yet BIOS options to figure it out.


hoping this might help people not to go crazy..

### Post by kokonobs on 2010-05-15
I've read through the whole topic and I also have exactly the same problem. It started on day one since i installed 10.04 of my shinny new dell. Today the freezing has happened more times.
I ruled out the following as the freezing happened with or without them:
- connected usb devices
- Firefox or Chrome (Though i should add that in many cases i had them opened; i should also add that since its random, and from reading all the posts it will most likely happen when you are doing what you do most on ur pc. Its easy to try to blame the browser since many people use it often)
-Docks (AWN, Docky, Dockbarx)
-Ubuntu Tweak
etc etc etc

I'm sure many people are facing this. If only they'll just log in and post something so it gets looked at by those who can actually help. Or maybe an admin can make this a sticky. As far as i can tell because we cant pin point the exact cause or reproduce the problem its hard to report it as a bug.

I have some important work to do and i'd hate to have to go over to Win 7 for this. This will not do my cred any good as i've been advocating Ubuntu to anyone who will care to listen.

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1209386](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1209386)

Thanks to tad1073 for taking the time to post this tip, and to StuartN for reminding me to give this work-around a shot :)

### Post by aphatak on 2010-05-15
Did some digging, in the hope that it will help pin the keyboard / mouse freeze bug down.
First, the hardware: -
        Intel(R) Pentium(R) D  CPU 2.66GHz
        MemTotal:        2049400 kB
        Disk /dev/sda: 160.0 GB, 160041885696 bytes
            /dev/sda1   *           1       10100    26270074+   7 HPFS/NTFS
            /dev/sda2           10101       18172    20995272   83  Linux
            /dev/sda3           18173       26224    20941824   83  Linux
            /dev/sda4           26225       60089    88081399+   f  W95 Ext'd (LBA)
            /dev/sda5           26225       27836     4192786+   c  W95 FAT32 (LBA)
            /dev/sda6           27837       29374     3999854   82  Linux swap / Solaris
            /dev/sda7           29375       30912     3998720   82  Linux swap / Solaris
            /dev/sda8           30913       52025    54914887+   7  HPFS/NTFS
            /dev/sda9           52026       60089    20972544   83  Linux

        1 PS/2 Keyboard, 1 PS2 mouse, 1 USB keyboard, 1 USB mouse
        RTL-8139/8139C/8139C+ 100Mbps Ethernet card
        RTL-8185 IEEE 802.11a/b/g Wireless card
        Intel N10/ICH 7 audio (on motherboard)
        Intel 82945G/GZ Integrated Graphics (on motherboard)

Current OS: Linux anirudha-desktop 2.6.32-21-generic #32-Ubuntu SMP Fri Apr 16 08:09:38 UTC 2010 x86_64 GNU/Linux, running from /dev/sda9.

Other OS's: Windows XP (32-bit), Ubuntu 8.04 (64-bit), Ubuntu 10.04 (64-bit) on /dev/sda3.

Boot manager: Grub-2

When I say the keyboard and mouse freeze, both (PS/2 and USB) freeze.

I did the following tests.  Didn't pin the bug down, but at least eliminated some possibilities.
1) Ran the live CD.  Froze in less than 10 minutes.  Did that three times, once browsed  the Web, then did absolutely nothing, and third time with the network disabled.  Conclusions: (a) the version on the Live CD has the problem, the installation process did not introduce any, and (b) networking is probably not the culprit.
2) Ran the live CD, immediately switched to console (ctl-alt-F2). Gave random commands for 10 minutes.  Left the PC alone till the screen shut down.  No freeze, the PC sprang back to life when I hit the spacebar.  Conclusion: the internal physiology of the PC is fine, the problem must be in the graphical interface - either X or Gnome.
3) Rebooted with live CD, no freeze after 10 minutes at install/try screen.  Selected install, no freeze during installation.
4) After installation, opened Synaptic (did not install ant package).  Keyboard / mouse froze within minutes.
5) After hard re-start, switched to console.  Removed ubuntu-desktop, then installed it.  Switched to Gnome desktop (ctl-alt-F7).  Keyboard / mouse froze within minutes.  Did a hard re-start, keyboard / mouse froze within minutes.
6) Installed OS again.  Applied all updates from the console.  Keyboard / mouse froze within minutes of switching to Gnome desktop.
7) Re-started, installed KDE from the console.  Switched to Gnome desktop.  Keyboard / mouse froze again.  Conclusion: the mere presence of KDE makes no difference.
8) Re-started, went into KDE.  No freeze.  Logged out, then logged in again to the Gnome desktop.  Keyboard / mouse froze within minutes.  Conclusion: KDE works fine, the fault must be in the Gnome desktop.
9) Re-started, logged into Gnome.  Guess what - frozen again.
10) Re-started, logged into KDE.  Applied available (bug-fix) updates - this lot included the current kernel.
11) Re-started with he new kernel version.  Since then the keyboard / mouse have not frozen in either KDE or Gnome, even after extended (couple of hours) use.
I am tempted to conclude that the updated kernel (2.6.32-21) has fixed the problem.  However, I am not willing to testify to that effect under oath yet.  I would rather run a test scenario to confirm it before I swear by it.
Well, I guess this is not conclusive yet.  Any comments or suggestions, anyone?

---

### Post by Doki on 2010-05-16
Hello, 

I had a flash last night... One word appeared in my head: "acpi" ...
So before I went to sleep, I restarted the box with "noacpi" option and this morning: Tadaaam! My computer was still awake and responding !

For those who may may be interested in this solution:

```
sudo gedit /boot/grub/grub.cfg
```

Then find a block looking like this: 

```
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, avec Linux 2.6.32-22-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 29cfded9-471e-4a5c-82a5-ee7e7dd36c74
	linux	/vmlinuz-2.6.32-22-generic root=UUID=d62c5cde-26f6-486d-8efc-f9fcd7520493 ro    quiet splash
	initrd	/initrd.img-2.6.32-22-generic
```

And add "noacpi" like this : 

```
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, avec Linux 2.6.32-22-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 29cfded9-471e-4a5c-82a5-ee7e7dd36c74
	linux	/vmlinuz-2.6.32-22-generic root=UUID=d62c5cde-26f6-486d-8efc-f9fcd7520493 ro **[COLOR="Red"]noacpi[/COLOR]**   quiet splash
	initrd	/initrd.img-2.6.32-22-generic
```

save close, and restart.

There is a way to enable the option so it will persist at next kernel update, but since I never had any troubles with acpi before on this machine, I guess this is just bug linked to this kernel version (2.6.32-22-generic) so it should be fixed with next one...

I hope it will help some of you around here to fix this freezing issue.

Bye :)

---

### Post by squareman418 on 2010-05-17
I have the keyboard/mouse freeze problem too!   But not random, it's pretty much instant. 

As a newcomer to Linux I added a comment to bug report number 577671 as it matches my problem right down to the mobo and how quickly it happens.  

I have since added another comment, the gist being that booting with the noapic parameter gets rid of the instant freezes for me.  Not great, but at least it then let me install and gave stability to try and debug things.

My further researches led me to you, and I am glad to have found such a lively discussion. 

If I boot without noapic, then my freeze happens as the login invitation appears, or if I get past that then as soon as I touch the keyboard to enter my password I get what looks like a "stuck key" and then it all freezes.  Only rarely do I get to the desktop before it then freezes at first use of keyboard.  The only way out is the power off button on my system.

The details are in 577671.  I can run live off an old 8.04 CD on the same system, and Windows XP I am dual booting with is just fine.   Have switched keyboards and mice, fiddled with BIOS  settings, all to no avail.

This is maddening for a beginner.  I've trawled the system logs looking for anything untoward but my inexperienced eye has found nothing of much interest.

### Post by evil-nick on 2010-05-17
I've had this happen a couple times to me, the latest time apparently scrambling superblock or something on the boot drive..

I dont know what mobo I'm using, or which graphics card (machine given to me, but it's an AthlonXP 1800+ with some ATI Radeon card using stock drivers),but it's setup with 1.7G of RAM, and an old 8G western digital hard drive (/home accessed through NFS).

When my machine gets super-laggy the hard drive is grinding like mad, as though the swap is being hit very hard.  The screen saver is set to blank, and the activates after 10min.  The monitor also shuts off after 10min.  Every time the machine has locked, it has been when left alone for several hours (4 to 8 hours).  Apps running are generally Firefox, Chrome, Empathy, maybe Gwibber and VLC.  Maybe a memory leak somewhere, causing it to hit the swap?

My other 2 machines running 10.04 have NOT done this.  One is a 1ghz Celeron machine with 512M of RAM and an Intel video card.  It boots off the livecd (no hard drive) and will run fine forever so far.  The same with my server, running 10.04 server edition.  AMD64 system w/1G RAM, and no video card installed.

Now to reinstall, since fsck won't even touch the filesystem :(

### Post by eGelor on 2010-05-18

I fix this today. thank you.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-19
This from Bugzilla looks very interesting - a change in LibATA behaviour that should fix ATA flush failure reporting errors, committed to kernel 2.6.32:

[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006)

> Comment #4 From  Tejun Heo   2010-05-19 14:40:31  -------

FLUSH_EXT timed out which shouldn't happen but can.  libata as of 2.6.32
doesn't retry after any FLUSH failure and just returns the error to upper layer
leading to fs ro remounting the device.  The reason for the behavior is that
FLUSH failure often indicates (abort by device always does) data loss so
continuing RW operation is likely to cause massive filesystem corruption.  As
the behavior caused some spurious failures like this, EH was updated to
distinguish between various FLUSH failure modes and retry unless it's certain
that the device aborted it.  So, in short, please upgrade to newer kernel or
tell your distro to backport the update.

Thanks.

See also patch description [http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f](http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f)

This patch adds a function **ata_eh_maybe_retry_flush** that is not present in /linux-source-2.6.32/drivers/ata/libata-eh.c in the Ubuntu package linux-source-2.6.32, so I assume that this commit (6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f) has not been applied in Ubuntu kernels yet?

### Post by paul.drover on 2010-05-19

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-19
> **paul.drover said:**
> Is it me, or are we seeing multiple indications that point towards power management and ACPI? I've not crashed since setting noacpi in GRUB. Indeed KwukDuck was able to capture logs. Are they the smoking gun? (I'm no expert)
Paul.

The patch described at [http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f](http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f) and the symptoms described in the Bugzilla Bug #14543 [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543) (and later in Bug #16006 [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006)) sound plausibly the cause of the diverse symptoms.

This patch has not been applied to the Ubuntu kernel yet. This is the strongest candidate for a fix that I have seen so far.

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-19
Reading through [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543), it would seem that if the flush error is the cause, then hardware systems most likely to experience it have a slow physical hard drive (or controller) that is formatted EXT4 for the system partition.

A power-management status change (from min_power to max_performance) at the same moment as a journal update causes an update failure and a readonly remount. This is essentially a random event, but some hardware and software combinations would make it more likely.

My system has an EXT4 / partition and EXT3 /home on an old 80 GB Western Digital drive with an SB600 controller.

---

### Post by fx69 on 2010-05-20
> **StuartN said:**
> This from Bugzilla looks very interesting - a change in LibATA behaviour that should fix ATA flush failure reporting errors, committed to kernel 2.6.32:

[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006)



See also patch description [http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f](http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f)

This patch adds a function **ata_eh_maybe_retry_flush** that is not present in /linux-source-2.6.32/drivers/ata/libata-eh.c in the Ubuntu package linux-source-2.6.32, so I assume that this commit (6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f) has not been applied in Ubuntu kernels yet?

I'm almost sure that this caused me freezing.. When system got it, disk seemed to hang. I have done many useless actions to get rid of this problem (cpu scaling, updating xorg, disabling screensaver..) but the one was (I don't know for sure) disconnecting sATA devices (in my case CD burner) and ATA devices one the same cable as disk.

No freezes since than :)

---

### Post by rlogan on 2010-05-20
My wife's laptop does random slows/freeezes, but I can ALT F2 into terminal and after a short time I get a chance to type again. Albeit this is still an unstable system at this point.

This machine is using the current 195 Nvidia driver and Compiz. If I disable COmpiz and Nvidia drivers it run's fine.

I have left the NVidia drivers enabled and disabled Compiz and all seems to work OK at this stage. 

As other people have said is seems to happen when Firefox is running but I have noticed it seems to happen quicker with both Firefox and OpenOffice running.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-20
> **KwukDuck said:**
> Updating the kernel worked though!

I added **kernel 2.6.34-020634-generic** but I now have no wireless connection, which will seriously impede any attempts to test the kernel (which is running fine so far) because I have to work too. Trying to get a USB wireless dongle (which is supported by an installed and free driver) to run now.

EDIT: I had a choice of wireless drivers, and the "free" (actually uses a nonfree firmware file) driver works fine - so I am writing from kernel 2.6.34 with functional wireless - let's see if this one runs without a freeze.

To test this kernel's stability, does anyone have an action that repeatably and reliably freezes their system? The bug report [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543) had a script that did not freeze my affected system, so it would be great if anyone could improve on that and develop a "kill" script.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-20
> **breek said:**
> compiled by yourself or found on some ubuntu repo?

sorry about your question but my ubuntu freezes totally randomly

The kernel is from the Ubuntu mainline at [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/) and installed using the instructions from [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds) (which are very straightforward). It should be added to your grub2 menu automatically, but that did not work for me on my legacy grub.

(I am keeping notes, including these instructions, at [http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html](http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html)).

As regards the freezing, yes it does appear totally random, but if someone can write a script that reliably causes a freeze on affected systems, then it would help diagnose this problem. The script on [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543) is not freezing my affected (kernel 2.6.32) system, but might be a starting point.

### Post by IIVQ on 2010-05-20
Hello,

Let me also jump in. I have the random freezes as well.
This is a new machine with a fresh kubuntu 10.04 install on it. I didn't install any other than "end user" packages (so no drivers), did no tinkering in any setting other than trough system settings etc.

What happens is that I can still move my mouse cursor, but other than that, no response at all - (edit): SysRq+b keeps working (and I hope / assume S and U as well)

My freezes:
- mostly random
- 1 case: left the pc on overnight to copy 110 gb from an external hard drive to internal. No crash - afterward, within 10 minutes of use: freeze.
- one day later, I was doing the same (different data) and it froze. However, I noticed that the hard drive light was still occasionally flashing. But I left it for a while and the copy wasn't finished.
- Today, I was watching the tv news via a webstream, via VLC. My box froze, but the audio kept playing, for some 10 minutes (then I rebooted) - that is way longer than the buffered amount can be IMHO, so appearently my pc kept receiving network data and decoding it.
- CPU temp usually is around 37-40 C (allways, both when freezing and not)

Hardware specs (related to things being said on this thread)
-Intel core i7 860
-Asus p7h55d pro motherboard
-MSI VN210 MDh12H (gforce 210) graphics card (where can I see what driver that uses?)
-1 hard drive (1 tb Samsung hd), with the / formatted as ext4
-optical sata drive on same sata channel as hard drive
-the mobo supports 4 DIMMS, I have 3 2 GB DIMMS (in one kit), 2 on channel 1, 1 on channel 2.

I have just after the latest freeze tried to:
- remove 1 DIMM so I have only 4GB, one on each channel
- remove the optical drive's SATA. Now, only my hard drive is connected (no IDE devices as well)

Let's see what this brings. If freezes, I need to read a manual and turn off acpi, though I really want that.

Tijmen
Update - nope, removing the ram and unplugging the optical's SATA didn't work - same freeze, again playing media which' audio kept playing. Next will be disabling ACPI - but not now, need to sleep.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-21
As far as I can tell, installing a recent mainline kernel, following the straightforward instructions at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds), solves the freeze issue on my machine.

The mainline kernel does not support all proprietary drivers and, of course, may introduce new bugs.

I am assuming the freeze issue is bug [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006) and I have put my notes at [http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html](http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html)

---

### Post by Andrew.Stevenson on 2010-05-21
Re,
> **IIVQ said:**
> Hello,
What happens is that I can still move my mouse cursor, but other than that, no response at all - (edit): SysRq+b keeps working (and I hope / assume S and U as well)

Requoting something I said in a previous post. Only my keyboard freezes, but mouse is still working. Checking Interrupt #1 (keyboard) in /proc/interrupts shows that the keyboard interrupt doesn't receive any keypress anymore.. *however* alt+sysrq+R-E-I-S-U-B *does* work. This is weirder than I thought.
> **IIVQ said:**
> 
My freezes:
- mostly random
 
Same here. Happens completely randomly. Never saw a specific pattern. Sometimes it can take 2minutes, sometimes 6hours or more.

This will surely work for you  (I use a script that i cut/paste to a term using mouse only), it's possible to unfreeze your kernel by doing:

rmmod psmouse
modprobe psmouse

> **IIVQ said:**
> 
Hardware specs (related to things being said on this thread)
-Intel core i7 860
-Asus p7h55d pro motherboard
 
I have the same specs. I saw many people having the p7p55 motherboard and experiencing this exact same freeze problem.
> **IIVQ said:**
> 
Let's see what this brings. If freezes, I need to read a manual and turn off acpi, though I really want that.
 
All these failed for me:
- turning off acpi in bios (and using "noacpi" on grub kernel command line)
- disabling Plug&Play OS in bios.
- using "nolapic" on grub kernel command line.
- using misc "i8042" parameters on grub kernel command line.

Good luck

### Post by edantes on 2010-05-22
So far, I set all power saving features to "never", added "nolapic" to grub and uninstalled ubuntuone.

System freezes went "down" to 3-4/day.  Usually black screens after ...

:mad::mad::mad: Oh well, it just happened as I was writing the sentence above :mad::mad::mad: 

I am back after Firefox restored this session.

Where do we stand?  Is this a Ubuntu only problem or are other distros running the same kernel version subject to frequent crashes?

I hate to admit it but this exposes very serious problems with the Linux distribution model.  The reason I always upgrade to the latest release of Ubuntu is not the core system itself, but  access to the latest versions of certain applications and drivers in a ready-to-install fashion.  Binaries of applications and drivers in Linux are rarely backward compatible.  In practice,  you are forced to upgrade.

Another problem is that Linux lost one of its main advantages compared to "other" OS's.   Now, a GUI crash brings down the whole system!!!  Just like running you know what.

---

### Post by Dave J on 2010-05-22
Hello folks - 
I'm a noob here but have been reading threads and following advice since 8.10 - thanks for all your help! and time to give back (I hope).

I've been investigating hangs (i.e, sudden mouse freeze, mostly whille moving between windows, with nothing to do but shut down or Alt-SysRq USREIB  to restart) in a clean install of 10.04 on a Gateway NV79u15. I had been using 9.10 on a Lenovo T61 - no problems. I reverted back to 9.10 on the Gateway, but the problem remained.  That told me it was  machine-related.

I read another thread about compiz being the cause, so I stopped using compiz by doing the following:
System > Preferences > Appearance > Visual Effects > None.  (Normal had been active).

I then opened a blizzard of programs in as many windows, moved around among them across three desktops.   It would have already hung up before, but all is fine as of now.

I hope this simple fix works for others - it seems others have been correct in that compiz is not Arrandale-ready.

---

check the simple things in life first

like is the power supply fan ceased?
cpu fan ceased?
video card fan ceased?
case fan ceased?

rip the side cover off the pc and blow it out with an air compressor

stick a pen thru the fan blades so the fans dont turn when u clean them or they will spin so fast the blades will fly off them....take it from someone who experienced this years ago


also remove the cpu fan, clean up the heatsink compound and apply a very small amount of fresh compound and try again


also check your hard drives for physical defects as well

### Post by wknight8111 on 2010-05-23
> **edantes said:**
> Let me join the chorus of crash and freeze victims.

I am running 10.04 AMD64 with all updates.  This is an desktop AMD dual core system with ATI onboard X1200 graphics through the X.org radeon driver (the only choice, ATI "legacied" this gpu a year ago).   

My crashes usually come after the system is left unattended as if had gone into hibernation and something went wrong.    Also suffered other nondescript random reboots while using the system.

This situation developed after a clean install of 10.04.

Can anyone suggest a way to diagnose the problem and generate a meaningful but report?   Nothing shows in logs I looked at.

This mirrors my experience almost exactly. It was not a clean install of 10.04, however. As a bit of background, I tried a clean install of 10.04, but the CD had a problem and the install failed. I installed 9.10 from a working CD and then immediately upgraded to 10.04. So it's not quite a "clean install", but relatively close.

My system is a Lenovo Y530. Intel Core 2 Duo (T5800). lspci only reports my graphics card as "Intel Corporation Mobile 4 Series Chipset Integrated Graphics Controller", I don't know the exact make of it. I also don't know offhand whether my keyboard/mouse are PS2 or USB internally. I'm using the built-in keyboard and trackpad, so I suspect they are PS2.

The freeze has happened for me twice now: Once yesterday after the install. I went to dinner and when I came back the computer was HOT, the screen was black, and there was no response to keyboard/mouse and I had to hard reboot. The second time was this morning, I was away for a few minutes, when I came back the computer was cool as normal, the screen was black, and it was unresponsive to keyboard/mouse.

I did install the 64-bit flash plugin for firefox yesterday, so that may have something to do with the heat issue. I've had plenty of problems in the past with 64-bit flash eating up CPU and RAM, so I am content to ignore the heat issue and write it up as "Flash was going crazy while the keyboard/mouse were locked".

Any help or information about this issue would be much appreciated.

### Post by PerfectReign on 2010-05-23
> **Dave J said:**
> Hello folks - 
I'm a noob here but have been reading threads and following advice since 8.10 - thanks for all your help! and time to give back (I hope).

I've been investigating hangs (i.e, sudden mouse freeze, mostly whille moving between windows, with nothing to do but shut down or Alt-SysRq USREIB  to restart) in a clean install of 10.04 on a Gateway NV79u15. I had been using 9.10 on a Lenovo T61 - no problems. I reverted back to 9.10 on the Gateway, but the problem remained.  That told me it was  machine-related.

I read another thread about compiz being the cause, so I stopped using compiz by doing the following:
System > Preferences > Appearance > Visual Effects > None.  (Normal had been active).

I then opened a blizzard of programs in as many windows, moved around among them across three desktops.   It would have already hung up before, but all is fine as of now.

I hope this simple fix works for others - it seems others have been correct in that compiz is not Arrandale-ready.

Excellent suggestion, Dave. 

I've never had Compiz enabled, though. I always have had it turned off.

### Post by thecityofgold2006 on 2010-05-23
More random freezes for me over the weekend. Turning off acpi, diasbling power management, nothing seems to work.

Lucid is a very poor release. I've tried installing it on three systems, one with Nvidia graphics, one with ATI, one with Intel, and am still to see anything like a correct boot-up sequence. On two of those systems (Intel 915 EEE and an ATI 3000) the system has been so unstable that it is unusable.

I am very surprised that the change to Plymouth (which I blame for all of this, it's never worked properly and has led to panicked changes close to release imho) happened for an LTS release. I was one of the people advocating Plymouth last summer before 9.10. If implemented at that stage there would have been time to fix it. 

As it is, Lucid goes down as the worst Ubuntu release in my 5 year experience of Linux. The fact that a major and unwanted change to button layout was foisted onto us in spite of the mess being made of the core distro just compounds the problem!

I love Ubuntu and will stick with it to a degree, but I need a stable system to work on. You'd think an LTS would deliver it, but no. It's temporary (possibly) Fedora for me.

### Post by IIVQ on 2010-05-24
Hi all. I had the freezes as well (see my earlier posts) but I don't have them anymore for 3 days (knock on wood)

There are 2 things I changed from my previous configuration:

[LIST=1]
[*]I meddled a little trough the appearance and desktop settings - unfortunatly I don't know exactly what I did. How can I see whether I have compiz enabled (on kubuntu 10.04)? - I now hear from chat that kubuntu doesn't have compiz, but anyway, I have "System settings -> desktop effects -> desktop effects -> enable desktop effects" set to Disabled
[*]I got myself an xorg.conf, so I didn't need to set the display settings as they reset every time. This solved the "set display settings each time after reboot" problem and introduced a host of other, but lesser problems for me. However, I want to present you my xorg.conf: 
[http://pastebin.com/HMCyWTtY](http://pastebin.com/HMCyWTtY)
[/LIST]

> **PerfectReign said:**
> 
As it was, I noticed a bunch of pixilazation on my screen just before it happened. Here's a photo I took with my blackberry...

[IMG]http://www.perfectreign.com/stuff/2010/20100523_ubuntu_desktop.jpg[/IMG]

My programmer brain is wondering why this isn't seemingly more obvious. You just look at what in X changed between 9.1 and 10.04.

I did notice the same kind of pixelation (even when the system was still live), though not in scroll bars but in the icons in the "task bar" (both of active windows and of the system notification area - mostly the kbluetooth icon which I've since disabled - this box had no bluetooth - oh and I did get freezes after disabling kbluetooth). It's over now, but I don't know since when.

Knock on wood,

Tijmen Stam

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-25
> **thecityofgold2006 said:**

Try kernel 2.6.34 using the instructions at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds)

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2010-05-25
> **StuartN said:**
> Try kernel 2.6.34 using the instructions at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds)

"Although the mainline kernels are built using the Ubuntu configuration files they do not include any Ubuntu specific drivers, if you use any of these drivers these kernels are likely to fail for you. There is also no restricted modules for these kernels. Finally they are also not modified for a specific Ubuntu release. The further away from your base kernel release you are the more likely that there will be an incompatible userspace interaction which will prevent them working for you. Each kernel is configured using the configuration from the release with which it is suffixed, for example 2.6.34-rc4-lucid is built using the Lucid configurations and most applicable to testing there.

The kernel team does not support these kernels, use them at your own risk. "

Sounds like trouble for many noobs.  This would just add to the level of frustration.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-25
> **edantes said:**
> Is the problem traceable to the kernel itself or to some feature added by ubuntu?

The bug that seems to fit the symptoms that I have is the incorrect detection of slow disk access as disk failure - Bugzilla #14543 [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543) and 16006 [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006) describe the fix.

I used the Ubuntu build of kernel 2.6.34 at [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/)

My notes: [http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html](http://www.iol.ie/~stuartneilson/Bootup_fsck.html)

---

### Post by fram84 on 2010-05-25
I had the same problem with total freeze in lucid. 

This command fixed it

nvidia-settings -a InitialPixmapPlacement=2 -a GlyphCache=1

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2010-05-25

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-26
> **florianr said:**
> Is there allready a bugreport for this issue?

There is probably a solution too, for most people, reading back through this thread.

In case these are not listed above, here are some probably relevant bug reports. There is no point in adding "me too" comments to them, but do click "Affects me" and subscribe to whichever resembles your problem.

[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006) Bug 16006 -  System remounted read-only at heavy HDD usage (watching movie, torrent, copying files)

[Karmic] Frequent freeze on Dell D810 [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/399054](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/399054)
Ubuntu 9.04, 32-bit ext4 file system suddenly becomes readonly [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/400531](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/400531)
[MICRO-STAR INTERNATIONAL CO., LTD MS-7100] suspend/resume failure [non-free: nvidia] [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/427947](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/427947)
ext4 journal error, remounted read-only after resume [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/438379](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/438379)
[Vostro 1000] Resume fails after suspend/hibernate [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/468850](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+bug/468850)
Filesystems end up remounted read-only [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/515937](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/515937)
Repetitive massive filesystem corruption [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981)
resume from suspend does not work [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/568711](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/568711)

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-26
> **cottfcfan said:**
> Could be an Ext4 problem.

Bug [https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006) and resolution [http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f](http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f) suggest that ext4 journalling is a trigger.

So yes, if the / partition is formatted ext3 then the bug is present, but (far) less likely to trigger a freeze.

---

### Post by bdcalvert on 2010-05-26
Here's what I did. Booted to the grub, choose the .32 generic  kernel in recovery mode. When I got to the recovery menu, I choose "failsafeX - Run in a failsafe graphic mode" - hit return. after that a message popped up "Ubuntu is running in low graphics mode. The following error was encountered. You may need to update your configuration to solve this 
(EE Microsoft Comfort curve keyboard 2000 failed to initialize for relative axes". 

Hit OK and got "What would you like to do? options:
1. Run Ubuntu in low graphics for just one session
2. Reconfigure graphics
3. troubleshoot the error
4. exit to console login
5. restart x

So I choose 1 - she booted wicked fast (just like I read). and, well, things look sort of normal as best I can tell. I fired up "countdown" on line - which I watch everynight - the full screen version was very jerky, so I think there is a graphics thing going on maybe. Ran a movie dvd - that ran OK. Graphics appears to be degraded. My 32b machine freezes in just a few seconds. 
But this fixes it. before grub starts, hold esc. 
Choose 2-36-32 generic recovery kernel.
on the next menu choose 
So - it does indeed look like me graphics stuff is the problem.

dave c

### Post by k508 on 2010-05-27

Buggy kernel is buggy. I'll wait before confirmation that it's been fixed before upgrading again. Very happy with my Ubuntu experience again now :)

### Post by databubble on 2010-05-27
I've also have been experiencing occasional (2 or 3 times a day) system hangs under 10.04 for at least a couple of weeks.   Keyboard/mouse unresponsive (can't toggle keyboard LEDs), can't ssh/ping... requiring hard reboot... no messages in the logs.    Dual core AMD64, with integrated nvidia chipset, and I use KDE.  However, lockups still occur even with the desktop and X server shut down, so I'm sure it's not desktop or video driver related..  The same hardware was rock-solid under jaunty and karmic. 

Only pattern to the lock-ups I've noticed is that it's usually during times of heavy CPU load... which usually also has heavy disk load: a large compile, ffmpeg video conversion, running virtualbox, dvd ripping, etc.

Reading the whole thread....  it sounds to me like the most likely cause is bug 16006, the FLUSH_EXT issue.   And while I don't have slow drives (3 x 1.5 GB SATA II drives), I do boot using a software-mirrored (md) EXT4 filesystem, and I'm wouldn't be surprised if the overhead of the software mirroring makes the drives "look" slow.

I've installed the mainstream 2.6.34 kernel, and will test whether that resolves the lock-ups and report back.

### Post by daboom on 2010-05-28
I too have encountered this problem, not to mention the I/O error after installation from both DVD and CD, but (I think) then I solved in this way.

Download the minimal cd to install Ubuntu and all updates directly from the net
Select Manual partitioning and check EXT 3 for root
After installation is complete use the nvidia proprietary driver (version 173)

After some hours my system seems to be stable, no freeze and hang up..

I've installed some DVB apps and scan channels, installed MythTV, enable compiz with the maximum effect, try log out and Log in.. all seems Ok...(for now)

My nvidia card is 7500 LE.

Hpe this helps you all, please let me know if someone decided to try.

---

### Post by daboom on 2010-05-28
I guess that the procedure works fine with ATI cards too, just install the oldiest proprietary drivers and try to add at the boot line modeset=0

These problems for sure are derived from the KMS reverted from the new kernel 2.33.x inside the 2.32.x that uses very Unstable Nouveau drivers even if you do not use Nvidia cards also create modules that can make conflicts

### Post by drakesg on 2010-05-28
My two cents..

Here, from Italy, is the same. Trying to find here a way to solve it but we are in the same bad situation.

I read in this topic about someone that experience a particular thing as i do.

My first 2 or 3 freezes stopped everything, as described from almost anyone here (keyboard+mouse blocked=no solution, everything completely frozen=manual power  off), but after waiting  for 2 or 3 minutes without moving the mouse or doing anything (with keyboard) it restarted to work again normally (as if it was "paused"). Maybe you can try to do the same to understand if it's only an event or something interesting.
In the other recent times a tried to press some key shortcuts but after 30 minutes of frozen screen (no hard disk activity during this period) i turned everything off. After forced shutdown in restarting twice happened to me that during the loading screen (after the bios first check-up and the grub choice) it froze again and it needed another manual restart.

I hope this could help the experts to understand something..

Thank you everybody and especially Ikrory21 (that started the topic with great accuracy) for your help.

Goodnight

PS: please forgive my bad english!:(:(

### Post by pvantoorn on 2010-05-29

### Post by pvantoorn on 2010-05-30
In a previous post on this thread I mentioned I had on my multi/linux bootable machine only problems with Ubuntu and gentoo. That is not true (also Fedora). Further test showed the following (USB mouse and keyboard):


[LIST]
[*]The freeze occurs also when X is not running. Keyboard hangs after some time.
[*]I have indications it freezes after some idle time. No keyboard input (when X is not running or not does not matter) (No real timing yet, but at least a couple of minutes)
[*]Whether or not Nvidia driver is used or not does not matter when X is running
[/LIST]

An interesting post is an parallel thread:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9313340&postcount=14](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9313340&postcount=14)

Googling the internet I get more indications that the problem occur after introduction of  2.6.32 kernel or later kernels

see e.g:
[http://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?pid=703024](http://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?pid=703024)

It seems we may have a serious problem with mouse keyboard input after introduction of 2.6.32 kernel !!??

<<<< snip nip >>>>>

I upgraded from 9.10 to 10.04 LTS last week (32 bit, gnome desktop, no desktop effects). The system was quite stable before the upgrade (it's usually on 24/7 with uptimes in the dozens of days), but ever since updating to 10.04, the system loses all mouse and keyboard input at random times. 

I can still ssh into the system fine and reboot it in an orderly fashion from a laptop, but I'd rather that it wouldn't freeze like this in the first place  

I've tried service gdm start / stop from an ssh session to see if it's an xorg-related problem, but sadly that did not help in recovering mouse / keyboard input. 

I also tried unplugging and re-plugging the ps/2 keyboard and the usb mouse, but sadly that also didn't help.

I don't see any weird messages in dmesg, so it's not a kernel oops either it seems. 

I'm using the proprietary nvidia driver if that matters. 

Thanks in advance for any pointers to where to start debugging,

### Post by StuartN on 2010-05-31
> **Esau said:**
> UPDATE: Unfortunately updating the kernel the 2.6.34 has not solved the problem entirely.

You should note that on a relevant Launchpad bug report, because some are being marked as fixed.

You could try these non-invasive changes:

Disable Compiz (System -> Preferences, Visual Effects = None).

If your ~/.xsession-errors has some *"(firefox-bin:3097): Gdk-WARNING **: XID collision, trouble ahead"* then set your screensaver to something other than None or Blank.

If your ~/.xsession-errors has *"Nautilus-Share-Message: Called "net usershare info" but it failed: 'net usershare' returned error 255: net usershare: cannot open usershare directory /var/lib/samba/usershares. Error No such file or directory Please ask your system administrator to enable user sharing."* then install the Samba components by sharing any directory (you can remove the share once it is installed).

### Post by ReyBrujo on 2010-05-31
Well, I verified both disks and neither had errors, then tested RAM all night long and zero glitches. So, I tried an old Ubuntu 5.04 Live CD and, while checking the hard disks with fdisk, system froze again. Then tried the Live CD for 10.04 and crashed with a kernel panic while booting. So, I disconnected all the disks, booted live CD again, and made it freeze while using torrents and uncompressing a big archive several times in a row. The computer would beep while uncompressing for the fifth or sixth time before freezing, which led me to a big clue: overheating.

I have been using my computer at 60C for years now, and while the computer beeps when trying to play a heavy video, it would keep playing. When the kernel log changed and logged every time the temperature was too high, I discovered that I was not alone. I cleaned the micro cooler, the power supply, added another cooler and now the computer runs at 40C. Not a single freeze still. So, my question would be, was something modified related to temperature? Under Windows computers reboot when overheating, but Ubuntu never did for us. Was something done to bring the approach closer to Windows?

If anyone else experimenting these freezes was also affected by the huge log problem some time ago, he could try cleaning the computer to keep the temperature lower to see if his computer continue to crash.

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-01
> **ReyBrujo said:**
> So, my question would be, was something modified related to temperature?

You are almost certainly correct that these are multiple, completely separate problems that all have the same symptoms. My system (which runs cool) was exhibiting perfect hardware failure symptoms, although it passed all diagnostic tests, but it was cured completely with kernel 2.6.34.

My 2 cents' worth is that the mad pressure to reduce Ubuntu boot time has removed all the wait states that used to deal gracefully with differing hardware latencies. Anything you can do to remove an IO request or speed it up will help - EXT3 root partition, all fancy effects off, disable unwanted processes, etc.

---

### Post by Dave Otter on 2010-06-01
I was getting frequent freezes/hang ups in the above,now almost completely
 eliminated by following these instructions:-

     Download linux-headers-2.6.34-020634_2.6.34-020634_all.deb
Download your kernel headers package;
I386:  linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb
AMD64: linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb
Download your kernel compile;
I386:  linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb
AMD64: linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb
Install the files in the same order (else it won`t work!)
In the terminal run:
sudo update-grub

### Post by Dave J on 2010-06-03
> **Dave J said:**
> Hello folks - 
I'm a noob here but have been reading threads and following advice since 8.10 - thanks for all your help! and time to give back (I hope).

I've been investigating hangs (i.e, sudden mouse freeze, mostly whille moving between windows, with nothing to do but shut down or Alt-SysRq USREIB  to restart) in a clean install of 10.04 on a Gateway NV79u15. I had been using 9.10 on a Lenovo T61 - no problems. I reverted back to 9.10 on the Gateway, but the problem remained.  That told me it was  machine-related.

I read another thread about compiz being the cause, so I stopped using compiz by doing the following:
System > Preferences > Appearance > Visual Effects > None.  (Normal had been active).

I then opened a blizzard of programs in as many windows, moved around among them across three desktops.   It would have already hung up before, but all is fine as of now.

I hope this simple fix works for others - it seems others have been correct in that compiz is not Arrandale-ready.
Hello again - 

I've been running my machine about daily, multiple hours per day ever since turning off compiz [System > Preferences > Appearance > Visual Effects > None]. Not a single hiccup in that week or so, and life is good again.  I know that this simple fix cannot work for all, but I recommend trying it if you have not already, if you have Arrandale graphics.  

For what its worth,
Dave J

### Post by joelpedersen on 2010-06-04
IGNORE THIS...  IMMEDIATELY AFTER WRITING IT FROZE...


I have been having the same problem with random hangs and think I found a possible solution (see below) that I found at [http://www.insidesocal.com/click/2010/04/ubuntu-1004-swap-update-its-no.html](http://www.insidesocal.com/click/2010/04/ubuntu-1004-swap-update-its-no.html) 

Once I implemented a permanent change I have had the system running now for several hours with no locking...

Reduce Swappiness
If your computer has 1GB+ RAM, you would be hardly needing your swap space in most cases. It is evident that the RAM is much faster than your hard drive (A good 677MHz DDR2 can give 3000+ MiB/s while a standard hard drive can give around 50MiB/s). So it's better to let the RAM handle most of the processes. The tendency to use swap is called swappiness.Swap space is a cached area on the HDD that the OS utilizes as a memory overflow/dump area. To reduce swappiness, at a terminal, type in:
Code:

sudo sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10

If you wish to reduce the swappiness permanently, do:
Code:

sudo nano /etc/sysctl.conf

And change the vm.swappiness value to 10. If the line doesnot exist, add the line vm.swappiness=x

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-04
> **garth.d said:**
> I am aware that the default fs for buntu is ext4, however, has anyone had better luck with others filesystems?

EXT3 is without any doubt less prone to freeze errors. The condition where the volume is remounted readonly is assumed ([https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006)) to occur during a journal update.

Also Bug 528981 ([https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981)) has just been promoted to High Importance and has a test kernel attached.

I am sure it would be appreciated if you could try the test kernels and report the results.

---

Also Bug 528981 ([https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/528981)) has just been promoted to High Importance and has a test kernel attached.

### Post by garth.d on 2010-06-04
Just had another "freeze" about 2 minutes ago.

This time around I decided to take another approach altogether.  Instead of endlessly trying to click away on an "unresponsive" Firefox window with my external usb mouse, I simply unplugged it and resorted to using the still responsive touchpad on the laptop.  I closed the "stalled" browser window, and all was peachy again.  Now why on earth didn't I try that sooner...?

Oh well, at least I didn't have to hard reset the system (ie: take out the battery), as that almost always leads to some sort of data corruption. :mad:

### Post by daboom on 2010-06-04

NOTE: ext3 or ext4 do not influece the esit.

### Post by clarissa.w on 2010-06-05
Hi there

I'm having random freezes as well of the mouse and keyboard variety - I have to do a hard reboot holding down the power button. My laptop often freezes when I am about to save a file off the internet. It just froze before when I was about to read a saved .pdf. I noticed in one of the launchpad bugs - some people notice on their computers which do freeze, Firefox sometimes becomes grayed out on their computers but there is no immediate relationship between freezing and Firefox. 

When my laptop has frozen, on the network, it cannot be accessed and becomes inactive on the wireless network - therefore, I assume it could not be turned off using ssh methods.

I'm using the current kernel (.22) and I'm afraid of updating to the mainline kernel because of my limited experience using Ubuntu - also because I think the wireless will become unavailable using this kernel. Previously, I have had to install the driver for my RTL8192se card and this has involved installing a modified kernel and/or installing proprietary drivers. Only in the past few days, has the current kernel included this wireless driver.

Just in case this is relevant, I am using a Nvidia proprietary driver; other people in the forum have expressed it might be a factor in the freezing.

On the older kernel .20, Ubuntu often starts up in low graphics mode and restarting X-server allows the graphics to be normal but I cannot view the settings under System -> Administration -> NVIDIA X-server settings

I am using power management but do not feel inclined to change the settings.

Also, I have no swap partition because of unknown 15.69MB partition on my hard drive which I was reluctant to remove in case it rendered Windows 7 in dual boot unbootable.

Finally, how can I record meaningful information on the freezing - logs from Xsession etc so I can post the relevant information on the forum and on launchpad?

Thanks very much

### Post by olesek on 2010-06-05
I am an Ubuntu user for over 5 years. I run my business using Ubuntu. I have migrated many of my clients to Ubuntu. I have made the decision this evening that Ubuntu is no longer a valid platform to run a business on. 3 of 10 of my personal machines and dozens of my client's machines are experiencing the same issues as described in this thread.

At this point I am going to recommend to my clients that Ubuntu linux is not a real world alternative to Microsoft windows. They should continue to use OpenOffice & Firefox but on the Windows platform.

Ubuntu is a great platform for those hobby seekers who have a lot of time on their hands, but for those whose time=money, Ubuntu is no longer worth it. 300 dollars for an MS license makes so much more sense compared to the hours we have spent trying to get around these current issues.

This greatly saddens me, but Ubuntu has failed me and it is time to move on. I will check back on 11.04 to see if things have improved. It was NEVER about the $$$ -it was always about the OpenSource philosophy. I am a huge proponent of Gimp, OpenOffice, Firefox, Joomla, Apache, PHP, and MySQL. Ubuntu just can't compete anymore.

I DREAD making the move back to Windows but their are no alternatives.

Thank you for many good years!!!
