### Post by mudBuntu on 2010-06-06
I have Lucid installed on three PCs here are the devices and status:

1.  ThinkPad T41p
ATI Mobility Radeon
Version: Lucid 32 bit upgraded from Karmic via Update Manager
Status: stable

2. ThinkPad T42
ATI Mobility Radeon 7500
Version: Lucid 32 bit; fresh install
Status: random freezing; always freezing at blank screensaver; was stable with Karmic and openSUSE 11.2

3. Custom Desktop (Intel Pentium D 805)
ATI Radeon graphics
Version: Lucid 64 bit; fresh install
Status: stable

I have scoured various blogs and forums and tried every suggestion I could find to stabilize the T42, including:
a. Custom xorg.conf
b. radeon.modeset=0 in GRUB (also tried nomodeset)
c. Set screensaver to other than blank screen (the laptop would still freeze at some point after the screensaver kicked in)

Initially, I thought that some changes had made a difference.  However, the randomness of this issue can make a one hour freeze-free session seem like an improvement.  Soon enough, though, I would find the system locking every two minutes, five minutes, ten minutes, etc.  

However, I think I have finally found a solution without downgrading to Karmic: I had seen where some users with Intel graphics cards had experienced success downgrading the kernel to linux-image-rt (2.6.31.10.12). While I certainly don't have a need for a real time kernel, this was the only kernel in the default Ubuntu Lucid repositories that was based on 2.6.31 and not 2.6.32.  I didn't want to go with a nightly build or PPA.  I wanted to stick with the stock repo, if possible.  Sure enough, I've been operating a day and a half without a lockup.  The screensaver kicks in appropriately and comes out of screensaver mode (which the laptop previously had not done).  In aggregate, I've had hours of use without any issue whatsoever.  Previously, I would have expected anywhere from 4-12 freezes.

Now, this laptop still has all the various changes that I've made, so I can't tell whether it is the kernel alone or if it is the downgraded (plus real time) kernel in conjunction with one of the other changes I have made.  So, I will shortly rebuild this laptop with a custom remaster (using Remastersys) with the linux-image-rt kernel as the sole change.  I expect this will work without any special change to xorg.conf or GRUB, but will report back if this fails.

---

### Post by BF79 on 2010-06-06
ok after many hours 48 to be exact  i got my 10.4 amd 64 working perfectly 

to 2 reinstalls  but i found were i personally was having   the freezing problem  it got to the point were i couldn't even get past the time server set up in the install process
  
ill give as much info as i can 

ok  i run a dual boot  with ubuntu 10.4 and win 7 both 64 bit 
after  using  partition magic  off of hierons boot cd 10.4 to total clean the partition i keep ubuntu on  [SIZE=5]make sure no usb flash drives are plugged in ,no sd cards in there readers  nothing extra popped in  any open holes on your tower 
[SIZE=3] just mouse,keyboard,monitor(s),power cable and speakers 
boot from you 10.4 cd  pick you settings for time server ,keyboard, were you would like to put it  make sure the partition ,hdd,were ever you put is clean and wiped 
 now i think the issue was somewhere in the update package like in the last update set that was sent out about 2 weeks ago  i ran the update manager  and got hung at grub-pc  so forced a restart  
on login  tried again same thing so not knowing crap i ran the command 
sudo aptitude purge grub-pc
 got a error that i need to configure --a
so i ran
  sudo aptitude configure --a
got a list of commands  next ran sudo aptitude configure --f 
that forced grub-pc to finish installing  which then finished the upgrade  
there is also a command which i had to run for the
 nvidia-xconfig  because that wasn't working 
now i would imagine that that command is going to change depending on which graphic hardware you are using  but  
try    sudo aptitude configure update "hardware drive you need here" 
 and ive been going strong typing like a madman know for 4 hours with out indecent (have a paper due last week)
it is something with the xconfig file not saving properly so when ever you save your xconfig file make sure it parses properly which it should do automatically
 if you get the error that "xconfig did not parse correctly " after you save it then try a different driver maybe  im not to sure !!!  but i know that after the clean install and the issues with grub config not saving correctly and  that caused xconfig not to save correctly and to not load correctly  
if the grub-pc dont work proper than  it doesn't load the rest of the os properly especially the xconfig file which btw took me 4 times to  save without problems 
[/SIZE][/SIZE]

Ubuntu is a great platform for those hobby seekers who have a lot of time on their hands, but for those whose time=money, Ubuntu is no longer worth it. 300 dollars for an MS license makes so much more sense compared to the hours we have spent trying to get around these current issues.

@olesek - 

I wouldn't consider Ubuntu - especially a new release - as a business-level system. There's no way of comparing it to Wintendo (note the derisive label).  I also use Windows (Vista and XP) at work as well as SUSE Linux Enterprise Desktop, in addition to Ubuntu.

The only reason I treid 10.04 over 9.1 was because it was on  a non-production critical laptop.  You'll notice I don't have it loaded on my systems at work and those work systems don't have even Windows 7 loaded yet.

If you don't want to downgrade to Wintendo from Linux, then may I suggest openSUSE or SUSE Linux Enterprise Desktp or even RedHat as alternatives to Ubuntu.  The SLED version from Novell has not - as far as I know - been hindered by the same kernel issues as 10.04.  Or you can do what I did and stick with 9.1 until the service update period runs out.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-09
> **thecityofgold2006 said:**
> Interestingly it looks like some work is being done to backport the Maverick kernel into Lucid.

I have installed mainline kernel 2.6.34 into identical computers suffering from freezing / failure to suspend and it has been a complete cure (other than failing to suspend if an SD Card is mounted) for my machines, as well as for others following some Launchpad bugs.

The fact that the same bug, or something very similar to it, still exists on other machines implies that the actual cause has not been fixed, but worked around for some hardware. It is important to find the cause.

It would be good to have some criteria for identifying affected machines, because this thread seems to have multiple problems. This is a suggestion, perhaps it could be attached to a bug report after a few amendments:

INCLUDED
Computer freezes during normal operation
Freeze is not reliably repeatable, occurs with heavy I/O
No obvious mouse or keyboard response
Caps lock, WiFi key do not change LED status
- But Alt SysReq REISUB may work
SSH from a wired connection may work
On reboot dmesg shows fsck of readonly filesystem
On reboot wireless may be disabled
Possible "end_request: I/O error, dev sda, sector 75769660" type kernel error, but it is not written to log file on disk
No obvious errors written to logs

EXCLUDED
Memory use continuously increasing (look for a memory leak bug)
Freeze is reliably repeated by a specific application and action (look for bug associated with that application)
Computer continuously freezes, then continues (use system monitor)
Top shows 100% CPU use by rogue process (look for process bug report)
Freeze is caused by a USB device, mouse etc (remove device)

---

### Post by ken.sallings on 2010-06-10
> **StuartN said:**
> I have installed mainline kernel 2.6.34 into identical computers suffering from freezing / failure to suspend and it has been a complete cure (other than failing to suspend if an SD Card is mounted) for my machines, as well as for others following some Launchpad bugs.

The fact that the same bug, or something very similar to it, still exists on other machines implies that the actual cause has not been fixed, but worked around for some hardware. It is important to find the cause.

It would be good to have some criteria for identifying affected machines, because this thread seems to have multiple problems. This is a suggestion, perhaps it could be attached to a bug report after a few amendments:

INCLUDED
Computer freezes during normal operation
Freeze is not reliably repeatable, occurs with heavy I/O

- But Alt SysReq REISUB may work

I believe the freeze is a USB kernel problem.  I have a USB mouse and a USB keyboard.  On my system, the mouse always freezes first.  It happens if I play a simple game like minesweeper, and it happens when I am browsing the web.  

When the mouse freezes, I issue the command "lsusb"; it hangs forever.  I have a feeling that the USB subsystem in the kernel is deadlocked somewhere.  If my wife would give me the time, I would have already fixed this myself.  Hopefully, this info can get us down the right path to resolving this issue.

Ken

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-10
> **ken.sallings said:**
> I believe the freeze is a USB kernel problem.

There are nearly 400-odd posts so far here (and probably he same again in related threads), with a huge range of hardware, most of which does not include USB mouse / keyboard triggered freezes.

It may be that the USB device triggers the freeze, but it is almost certainly not the cause. Removal of the problem USB device cured the freeze issue for posters earlier in this thread.

It would be very useful to have one or more definitive "must have" conditions - such as, for instance, dmesg reporting "recovery required on readonly filesystem" upon reboot after a freeze, a message that appears in the majority of dmesg reports on related Launchpad bugs.

Otherwise there is a danger of including all kinds of overheating, memory failure and wifi driver issues that are definitely unrelated.

### Post by oomar on 2010-06-10
I'll be honest I havent read through all 41 pages here but I've skimmed throuh various pages.


I have been experiencing this problem as well. I have an HP Pavillion dv5 with 4 GB RAM, Dual core processor at 2 GHz, 320GB HD, and an Nvideo video card running the latest proprietory drivers. I'm running, of course, a clean install of Ubuntu 10.04.

Now it occurs sporadically, regardless of whether I'm doing anything resource intensive. I could be playing Tetris, listening to music, or simply typing up notes in summer school. OR I could be playing Starcraft through Wine while torrenting and listening to music over wireless. And it randomly freezes. I've actually had it freeze when EVERYTHING was closed and I tried to open a folder on my desktop... Now mine starts with the keyboard being unresponsive. Then only left click works, and even then when I click on the panel or anything, it highlights the button I clicked but the drop down (applications or places... etc.) doesnt appear. The only Keyboard input that works regardless of the freezing is Ctrl + Alt + Delete which brings up my shutdown options. I have yet to try Ctrl + Alt + F2 but I wouldnt be surprised if it worked. I'm starting to think that this may indeed be a problem with whatever part of Ubuntu deals with input (gnome? or does it just handle windows...? ). Perhaps its the processing of this input that is getting frozen. My screen never freezes, per say, merely i lose the ability to effect any change over my system besides moving the mouse. 

I have not looked into the log files but I don't think I could add anythign that someone else hasn't posted. 

Does anybody know if the Dev Team has been looking into this problem with any success?

I'm trying to think of anything else to say but I'm at a loss. This is the first problem I've ever had with Ubuntu that I haven't caused myself (when i just began using ubuntu, i once deleted gnome... that was a bit scary lol). 

has anyone found a work around besides downgrading?

-- Logan

[[COLOR="Blue"]Here's  a link to how I fixed it for the following system:[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9436990&postcount=24")
Ubuntu 10.04 Lucid Lynx 64 bit kernel 2.6.34-020634
AMD Phenom II x4 925
Asus M4A785D-M Pro with on-board Radeon HD4200
4 GB (2X2GB) Corsair PC6400 DDR2 Ram

TP-Link TL_WN350GD Atheros based WiFi Card
sda 160 GB SATA Seagate
sdb 1.5TB SATA Samsung

Basically I needed to install kernel 2.6.34-020634, add the x-swat ppa & install the AMD drivers there to get stability, HW 3D graphic acceleration & wi-fi working. But newbies, please have a look at the link for some extra info.

I hope this helps some of you.

### Post by oomar on 2010-06-10
> **StuartN said:**
> ****

That statement is just so fitting that I think it should be adopted as the forum motto.



StuartN, I couldn't agree more. lol

Now, sorry if this has been said and I just didn't catch on, but if I install the 2.6.34 kernel it seems I'll lose some support for drivers... now what drivers would I lose? I know I use the Nvidia proprietory driver but the rest Ubuntu automatically configured. So does this mean I would need to install my own wireless driver etc? ### Post by viordiasko on 2010-06-10
> **oomar said:**
> 
Now, sorry if this has been said and I just didn't catch on, but if I install the 2.6.34 kernel it seems I'll lose some support for drivers... now what drivers would I lose? I know I use the Nvidia proprietory driver but ...<snip>...How many drivers do you think id have to reconfigure/reinstall?
If you have a look at System>Administration>Hardware Drivers you can see how many _restricted_ drivers you are currently using. These are the ones which will fail with a simple install of the [Mainline Kernel]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/KernelTeam/MainlineBuilds") (just like my ATI Radeon HD4200 did). My ath5k wifi driver is included in the kernel luckily. If your wifi worked 'out of the box' you should be OK.

I got my restricted ATI driver by adding the [[COLOR="Blue"]x swat repo[/COLOR]]("https://launchpad.net/~ubuntu-x-swat/+archive/x-updates?field.series_filter=lucid") & there are two entries there for 
```
nvidia-graphics-drivers  	 256.29-0ubuntu1~xup~lucid  (2010-05-30)
nvidia-settings 	256.29-0ubuntu0sarvatt~lucid  (2010-05-30) 
```
but whether these are the genuine Nvidia drivers or the open source versions I can't tell you, I'm afraid.

### Post by Cyrex056 on 2010-06-14
I have upgraded from 9.10 to 10.04 - Newer computer 64-bit, 8GB RAM, one HD for OS, 8 HDs for RAID 6 (mdadm), all updates installed.

It will randomly freeze while opening nautilus, copying files, surfing the web (firefox), attaching a digital camera (USB).  There does not seem to be a set pattern.  The only option is a hard reboot, since the mouse and keyboard are non-responsive.  Also REISUB does not work.  Never had this happen before since first using Ubuntu (Hardy).

After the hard reboot, the BIOS is all screwed up, and I need to unplug all of the data cables from the RAID array as it will only boot from the OS drive (usually showing some type of message about attempting to fix errors found during the boot screen).  Then I have to shut down, replug in all of the RAID devices, reboot, and allow mdadm to take 6+ hours to resync the array.  This is a huge pain.

Unfortunately, I don't know where the error logs are, or I would post them.  :confused:  Any word on a fix?  For an LTS release, this is pretty unacceptable.

### Post by NTHQ on 2010-06-14
So this is how it all went down for me...
I was checking out some source files and since it was a long process, I went to the gym while my computer was running. When I got back, it said in the terminal that everything was done. Problem was that everything was frozen. Even the green LED on my wireless mouse adapter was off; keyboard was unresponsive. So I had no choice but to hold the power button on my laptop until it shut down.
When Ubuntu booted up again, I opened a terminal using a quick launch icon I put in the top panel and Firefox to continue my work. I noticed that that the cursor in the terminal wasn't blinking. I tried typing and it didn't work. So I closed it by clicking on the X and reopened another terminal; same thing... Then I tried opening a terminal again by going through Applications -> Accessories but the menu wouldn't opened. Then again I was forced to reboot as I did before.
Back into Ubuntu again, everything is normal. I honestly didn't understand it. I thought maybe if I waited it would happen but it didn't. I thought I was just lucky that time. While I was doing the check out I mentioned earlier, I installed ccsm-simple and compizconfig-settingsmanager. Being the last changes I made to my computer and because of the fact that they affect the GUI, I suspected that they may have caused this, so I uninstalled them both.
The next day it happened again... I noticed that not everything freezes at the same time. It starts at the top panel and then moves to the bottom one. The current window that is open will remain operational however. For example, I can navigate through Firefox using the mouse but I can't type anything since the keyboard is unresponsive.
Did a reinstall of Lucid Lynx. This time, I tried not activating the nVidia drivers and just let it run without doing much. Yet it happens again...
I had to do a kernel update though or else my wireless card wouldn't work.

---

### Post by paul.drover on 2010-06-14
With my machine I'm thinking that it's a gdm (or related) issue.

When it happens to me, it's fairly consistent:
1) after some period or keyboard and mouse idleness, the screen-saver kicks in (i think I have the one called video-feedback - lots of neat swirling colours).
2) occasionally (but not always)disturbing the mouse makes the screen saver go away, and the password dialog appears. I can enter my password and then continue to operate normally. This is the expected behaviour!
3) occasionally (but not always) disturbing the mouse will make the screen saver go away, and the password dialog appears. I can enter my password but then I'm shown a freeze-frame of the screensaver. It's like the last image captured, or that which is left in that page of video RAM. From that point on, the machine is locked and the power button is the only recourse.
4) occasionally (but not always) exactly as above, but if I do an Alt-F1 I get a drop down panel menu which I can navigate with my keyboard and/or mouse. There is no desktop, just the residual image of the screensaver, and as I go left or right on the system menues, the background is redrawn as black. I can select applications (such as the terminal application, for example) but they never appear on the screen. Additionally, when in such a failure mode, I can do Ctrl-Alt-F1 to get a text console. Through here I can log in, do a "sudo services gdm stop" followed by a "sudo services gdm start" which immediately presents a new desktop login screen. I can then log in and use the machine normally.

I have not experienced any spontaneous lock-up while actively using my machine, it's always associated with being awaken from suspension.

I tried messing with all sorts of APIC setting, none of which seems to make a difference.

I'm figuring that my experience is different enough and consistent enough to warrant presentation to the group here.

Are we seeing different "classes" of these failures? I must mention that I'm using an nvidia card with the proprietary video drivers. Other stuff: AMD 1.2Mhz, 2Gb ram. older machine. 9.2 was fine, 10.4 and now 10.10 crash as above.

Cheers,
Paul.

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-16
Does the freeze issue only affect machines that have an EXT4 journalling filesystem for the root partition? There are posts above from people who decided to re-install using EXT3 and have not contributed further - was that a complete cure?

Now I am only talking about machines that freeze mouse / keyboard / display randomly, without excessive CPU use and without memory leaks. When booting after a freeze **dmesg | grep "readonly"** will always produce a message like **"EXT4-fs (sda4): INFO: recovery required on readonly filesystem"**, and the wireless adapter probably needs re-enabling.

If every affected machine has an EXT4 journalling filesystem, has anyone experimented with the mount option **nodelalloc** to disable delayed allocation (see the developer's suggestion at [http://thunk.org/tytso/blog/2009/03/12/delayed-allocation-and-the-zero-length-file-problem/?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed:+ThoughtsByTed+%28Thoughts+by+Ted%29](http://thunk.org/tytso/blog/2009/03/12/delayed-allocation-and-the-zero-length-file-problem/?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed:+ThoughtsByTed+%28Thoughts+by+Ted%29)).

Has anyone tried to make the problem more frequent by decreasing the EXT4 synchronisation schedule, or less frequent by increasing the synchronisation schedule using commit=nrsec (*) - the default is 5 seconds (see [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1096467](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1096467)).

These seem to be entirely safe experiments, which I am off to try.

**Passed Step 1** - I have switched back to the freezing kernel 2.6.32-22 (from 2.6.34, which has never frozen) with mount option nodelalloc and the first thing I notice on my first resume from suspend is that the display is dimmed, which is not a good sign, but it has booted, suspended and resumed.

**Failed Step 2** - Failed with a freeze, but an interesting freeze. The machine became sluggish and would not list /var/log (ls /var/log and du /var/log hanged), with **ext4_lookup, real_lookup, do_lookup and do_path_lookup** errors in messages and syslog. Alt-SysReq-REISUB rebooted with "recovery required on readonly filesystem" in dmesg. It is potentialy interesting that a filesystem remains functional, but the /var/log inode gets locked or lost.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-16
I am having difficulty working out how to mount my / filesystem using the option data=writeback, because every method I try gets stuck in a readonly filesystem at boot, so I need to reboot off a CD to test another method. So far I tried data=writeback in fstab, which the boot process dislikes. I then tried the correct method with root=data=writeback in /etc/defaults/grub, but that also did not work.

What I am trying to do, per man mount, is test whether this reduces or eliminates the freeze - *data=writeback: Data ordering is not preserved - data may be written into the main filesystem after its metadata has been committed to the  journal. This is rumoured to be the highest-throughput option. It guarantees internal filesystem integrity, however  it can allow old data to appear in files after a crash and journal recovery."*

**Edit:** I found the method for mounting writeback here [http://www.goitexpert.com/general/ubuntuguide/](http://www.goitexpert.com/general/ubuntuguide/) which involves modifying /etc/defaults/grub with **rootflags=data=writeback** (not root=...) AND modifying /etc/fstab to match AND resetting the filesystem with tune2fs.

**Step 1** - same again, kernel 2.6.32-22 will suspend, but it resumes with the screen dimmed, so I am expecting it to freeze. Kernel 2.6.34 resumes with the screen brightness as I left it, and has not failed since mid-May.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-16
Would anyone else care to try replicating the experiment that I have described in message 487 above? In other words, follow the guide at [http://www.goitexpert.com/general/ubuntuguide/](http://www.goitexpert.com/general/ubuntuguide/)  which involves modifying /etc/defaults/grub with rootflags=data=writeback AND modifying /etc/fstab to match AND resetting the filesystem with tune2fs -o journal_data_writeback.

Your root filesystem will then be in writeback mode, rather than ordered mode. The freeze will lock an individual process and will subsequently lock any further process attempting to access the same inodes.

The running system appears not to be affected by the freeze so long as it does not depend on any locked inode.

NB: This is an experiment to determine further information about the cause of the freeze, **not** a solution. My system appears to continue running and is able to provide data about the locked processes, so any suggestions on what to report are most welcome.

### Post by DaleEMoore on 2010-06-16
I am piling on and adding my name to those whom are experiencing a Freeze or Hang on Ubuntu 10.04. I have several systems that experience a random blank screen, are un-responsive to keyboard and mouse. Some were upgrades from previous Ubuntu versions, and some were scratch installs from CD.

At least one of these systems is running openssh-server and rsyncd. When I attempt to ssh, or rsync into the server the program hangs at the client and never gives an error. This seems odd to me because I am used to ssh or rsync giving up after a few minutes; so, I assume there is some level of activity occurring on the frozen server.

I can make this freeze anytime I want to (like I really want to do this;)

1. log out
2. turn off monitor
3. come back in 5 minutes, turn on monitor, wiggle mouse, monitor power light goes from orange to green, login screen displays.
4. turn off monitor
5. come back in 30 minutes, turn on monitor, wiggle mouse, monitor power light goes from orange to green, nothing displays, monitor power light goes from green to orange.
6. no response to ssh, or rsync from client machines.

So basically every time I log out of the machine and let it sit for 30 minutes it freezes.

I hope to hear that someone discovers a solution,
Dale E. Moore

### Post by paul.drover on 2010-06-16
Hi again. I've another ort of information to add to the pile.

Previously I mentioned that in a fit of desperation I went to 10.10 (Maverick in Alpha), but had plans to revert to 10.04. Well, I couldn't get the 10.04 CD to work at all, so I'm going to tough it out with 10.10.

Bad news is that 10.10 hangs as well :( ... I had high hopes.

Good news is that my son and I have discovered a method of failure that is 100% repeatable:

- First log in. All is normal.
- Lock terminal with Ctrl-Alt-L (I've remapped it to Ctrl-Alt-KPDelete). Screen saver should appear.
- move mouse so that the screen saver disappears and you are prompted for password to unlock.
- Do not supply the password. Instead select the "switch user" option. This is where my son was helpful in discovering this procedure.
- log in as a **different** user. All is normal.
- log out.

The expectation at this point is that you'll be returned to the locked screen where the first user would be able to supply original password to regain access. But no!

On logging out as the second user, the screen goes blank. There is no response from the mouse or keyboard (indeed even the CapLock and NumLock LED's are non-responsive). Alt-F1 will not bring forth a console. Only option for me is the Big Red Button (BRB - which on my machine black :) )

Happens every single time.

Now, can others try this sequence to see if this is common behaviour? It is likely that this has been easily missed because I'm sure not many of us with this issues have entertained a second user.

If this is common behaviour, what does it imply? I don't know. That's a question for someone else to answer.

Cheers,
Paul.

[*]ALT-SysRq-REISUB non-functional
[/LIST]
The only recovery is to hard power cycle. 

No logging at all is left behind that I have found yet.

This does NOT happen when docked with external monitor or when I have external monitor, keyboard & Ethernet cable plugged in separately to the laptop.

This does happen when untethered both with and without AC power, wireless enabled and disabled. 

It seems to not happen for a while, then starts. Once it starts, I basically cannot use the laptop.

I can repeat this by simply logging in, and starting firefox, then downloading something. Freeze happens. Virtualbox also triggers this everytime.

This did NOT happen on initial Lucid build. It started within recent update-manager application.

I have kms-modeset disabled via modprobe.d file with options radeon modeset=0 to work around external monitor wavyness issue. 

I am running 2.6.32-22, Xorg 1.7.6-2ubuntu7 Mesa 7.7.1 open source drivers.

The hypotheses in this thread focus on:

[LIST=1]
[*]disk (ext4)
[*]video drivers
[*]wireless.
[/LIST]
I have one partition, /, running ext3. 

This happens with and without Bluetooth & 802.11 enabled using both kill switch and applet. 

I am seriously thinking about downgrading back to 9.10 as I cannot use my laptop once this starts.

### Post by mminorhsd on 2010-06-17
Well, I may have fixed my problem, though I'm not sure what it was. The 
Ubuntu system has been up for 24 hr ( not a long time, but better than it 
has been ) with no crashes/freezes. I downloaded all of the updates on 
Tuesday. Then on Wednesday, I disabled the wireless portion of my router.
 
About the same time I started installing Ubuntu, one of my desktop XP 
machines network card went out. I didn't want to take the time to crack the 
case and install a new card, so I put a USB Wirless adapter on it, and 
enabled wireless on the router.

I noticed that the other windows machines would sometimes loose the network connection through the wired connections and I would have to reboot the router a couple of times a day.

I then began to notice that the Ubuntu machine would freeze/crash most often while reading the some documentation online, moving from one page to the next. After rebooting the Ubuntu pc, I would have to manually force the eth0 connection to connect to the router.

That got me to thinking about the network connections and the router. 
Yesterday, I disabled the wireless on the router, and have not had to reboot the router due to lost wired connections, nor has the Ubuntu server crashed. I am going to let it run a couple of  more days, and if all continues to go well, then I will have to  get the tool kit out and put a new nic in the desktop machine.

I'm wondering if the Ubuntu problems were related to the network connection going away.....

### Post by MadDawg010 on 2010-06-19
I have this issue as well. I'm using a ThinkPad R51e with 2GB of RAM, a Celeron M 1.6GHz CPU, and an ATI Radeon 200M GPU.

It takes a while for it to finally freeze, but it almost always happens when I do one or more of the aforementioned activities (sometimes it just freezes out of nowhere, but this is rare). This problem occurs with Ubuntu 9.10, 10.04, and Linux Mint 8 (which is essentially Ubuntu 9.10 anyway). I don't remember if this happened in 9.04.

### Post by dpeinado on 2010-06-19
I have perhaps a clue. I've been making a lot of tests, installing ubuntu 9.04, fedora 13, ubuntu 9.10, ubuntu 10.04 and I have always the same problem. In my case is with my nvidia card, BUT this can be with the wifi network card or whatever. It has to do with a irq problem associated with irq 16. In my computer my nvidia card and the usb controller shares this irq. In other computers can be other devices. I've seen spread in the net problems with the following error:

linux irq 16: nobody cared

the kern.log is:

Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422183] irq 16: nobody cared (tr
y booting with the "irqpoll" option)
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422187] Pid: 3772, comm: Xorg Ta
inted: P           2.6.28-19-generic #61-Ubuntu
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422188] Call Trace:
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422189]  <IRQ>  [<ffffffffa0483433>] ? rm_isr+0x95/0x10b [nvidia]
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422360]  [<ffffffff802a07b6>] __report_bad_irq+0x26/0xa0
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422362]  [<ffffffff802a09d4>] note_interrupt+0x1a4/0x1f0
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422364]  [<ffffffff802a110d>] handle_fasteoi_irq+0xdd/0x100
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422367]  [<ffffffff80213d7c>] ? call_softirq+0x1c/0x30
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422369]  [<ffffffff802152b0>] do_IRQ+0xc0/0x110
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422370]  [<ffffffff80212be3>] ret_from_intr+0x0/0x29
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422371]  <EOI> <3>handlers:
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422373] [<ffffffff8052d920>] (usb_hcd_irq+0x0/0xa0)
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422377] [<ffffffffa0571000>] (nv_kern_isr+0x0/0x70 [nvidia])
Jun 19 13:41:39 number-cruncher kernel: [  133.422487] Disabling IRQ #16


When it happens the system seems freezed, but the keyboard is responsive to Alt-Sys REISUB. It's important in my computer to press Alt key, then hit the Sys key, release the Sys key but not the Alt key and while pressing Alt, write REISUB. At first I thought that my system was completely hanged because I failed in doing correctly the REISUB sequence.

There is a lot of different problems related to this in the net.

I think that when two devices sharing the same irq have high activity, there is a conflict and then the manager for this irq stops working.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-20
> **deancasino said:**
> I'm beginning to think we all have different issues, with the same crash/freeze result...

Absolutely, there are several issues causing freezing. I would like to exclude memory leaks, graphics driver issues and USB device issues that seem to have solutions outside this thread. I proposed a checklist for this issue, and would be glad for feedback:

**Identifying an affected system:** It seems that locking the screen (Ctrl-Alt-L) and logging in a guest session will cause the system to freeze when reverting to the original session [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9472718&postcount=496](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9472718&postcount=496). Some other characteristics are:-

**Affected systems:**

    * Computer freezes during normal operation
    * Freeze is not reliably repeatable, occurs with heavy I/O
    * No obvious mouse or keyboard response
    * Caps lock, WiFi key do not change LED status
    * - But Alt SysReq REISUB may work
    * SSH from a wired connection may work
    * On reboot dmesg shows fsck of readonly filesystem
    * On reboot wireless may be disabled
    * Possible "end_request: I/O error, dev sda, sector 75769660" type kernel error, but it is not written to log file on disk
    * No obvious errors written to logs 

**Excluded systems:**

    * Memory use continuously increasing (look for a memory leak bug) Freeze is reliably repeated by a specific application and action (look for bug associated with that application)
    * Computer continuously freezes, then continues (use the system monitor or top to identify the process responsible)
    * Top shows 100% CPU use by rogue process (look for a process bug report)
    * Freeze is caused by a USB device, mouse etc (remove the problem device)

---

### Post by Bristol_Green on 2010-06-20
As I reported at #501 and #506 my clean reinstall seems to have vastly improved the situation. Previously I was getting repeated unrecoverable grey-screen freezes, typically when 7 or 8 tabs were open (including Gmail and this one), and while 2 or more Open Office documents were open, whether or not they were focussed. Closing an unsaved OO document from the bottom panel would almost guarantee a freeze.

Now that I've recovered my system settings, and even activated **more** Compiz effects, the freezes are rarer and short-lived.

---

### Post by HerzyWSU on 2010-06-20
It's very likely that we are all having different issues.  I'll share mine.

I'm running a new install of Ubuntu 10.04 LTS (32 bit) on a Fatal1ty F-190HD, dual processor (can't remember whether AMD or Intel, prob. doesn't matter), and an on-board Radeon Xpress 1200 Series Video card.

To install Ubuntu, I had to resort to the 'nomodeset' trick to get past a persistent black screen.  Then, I encountered the same issues with randomly freezing, specifically when running 5-8 tabs of firefox.  I saw the same sort of pixelation as shown by someones camera in the first 10 pages of the thread.

Switching to Chrome didn't help.  What has helped so far was changing the system->preferences->appearance to "No visual effects" under the Visual Effects tab.  That alone has stopped the immovable mouse/keyboard-lock/hard-boot-needed problem.  

I would expect that this means that for me, either the linux kernel or gnome was to blame for the random freezes.

---

I don't know if this is necessarily the same problem, but I'll post anyway.

I'm getting very frequent freeze ups, that seem to occur more frequently with heavy internet usage (for example, with multiple tabs open in Firefox and/or when using Spotify), but also sometimes when internet usage is light. Mouse and keyboard become completely unresponsive, if music is playing at the time of a freeze then it loops like the last second or two of the track continuously until a hard reset is performed. Highly frustrating!

### Post by puzzler995 on 2010-06-21
I've got Ubuntu 10.04 on a Dell Dimension E310, with 1 GB of RAM. I have been using XFCE over GNOME because of the freezing, but today it happened again. I'm starting to think it may be a problem with the Linux Kernel reading my RAM, as the Dimension E310 has diagnostic lights on the front. The lights that lit up this last time were 1, 3, and 4. 1 and 3 together mean the Memory modules are detected, but a memory configuration or compatibility error exists. 3 and 4 together mean Memory modules are detected, but a memory failure has occurred. 1 by itself means No memory modules are detected. I'll reply when I restart and check the error logs.

### Post by dpeinado on 2010-06-21
> **dnguyen1963 said:**
> I installed Xbuntu 10.04 with kernel 2.6.32-21-generic and had NO freezes.  I performed a system update with later kernel and had random freezes again.  Rolled back to 2.6.32-21 kernel and, again, NO freezes.  Had anyone else try this kernel on their systems?

Yes, I'm back with 2.6.32-21-generic and it seems pretty stable. I've blocked the kernel version so it won't be updated. If it is stable, then I will work for a couple of months before making any test ... I've lost a lot of time (near one month) with this issue. The problem is that I have no 3D acceleration (nvidia G210). I do not know if try to install because in fedora, debian, and ubuntu 9.04 when I installed the nvidia driver I have again similar freezes (keyboard irresponsible, but SysReq REISUB works). It has to do with a "irq nobody cared", that seems to be a very popular bug also ...
It seems that hardware manufacturers have agreed with MS to share information so win7 is working very very well with new hardware, and sadly linux in general is not.

If you want a system working ... block the kernel version to 2.6.32-21.

Cheers

### Post by paul.drover on 2010-06-21
I'm inclined to agree that we have several issues here. I'd like to take an opportunity to make a quick summary of what we are seeing, starting with my own experience.

My issues is *only* related to logout/suspend. I can use my machine for *days* as long as I don't logout or let the machine suspend itself. It has never hung while I'm actively using it (but, to be fair, I'm not stressing it a lot either). On my machine (and at least one other reported here) this is 100% repeatable.

Others report that system-hangs happen during active use. In this realm, there are those whose machines stop suddenly, and those who seem to experience a degradation of performance before system halts. I'm not affected by this failure mode, neither sudden nor slow.

But of those who are so affected, some have suggested a correlation between other system activities: network activity (web browsing, streaming, torrent, etc.), Open Office, or disk intensive operations. Unfortunately, none of these have been reliably repeatable.

Is it worthwhile splitting the thread?

Paul.

---

### Post by eltama on 2010-06-21
I think I am another victim of this problem.

My Dell latitude D420 laptop freezes from time to time. Usually it is a few times a week, although today it happened twice. The keyboard and the mouse does not respond at all, but as some people have noticed, music will continue to play.

The graphic controller is an Intel GMA 950 (Integrated, up to 224MB shared). I use the i915 open source driver.

At the beginning I thought it was related to Chromium or Flash because most of the times it hanged while browsing, but today it froze when I had no browser open and I was just writing a document.

I have been using other version of Ubuntu for years without problems and Windows works fine on it, so I discard a hardware problem. I also have a quad core desktop with an NVIDIA card, but 10.04 has never hanged on that machine.

### Post by RavanH on 2010-06-21
Wow, isn't it sad when even though no actual fix is suggested, you find comfort in the finding out that at least you are not the only one?

Well... that's basically what I am feeling right now. After weeks of frustration caused by totally random system lock-ups (right after first upgrading and then a clean install of Lucid) without ANY hint in any log file or any consistency in the steps preceding a crash (so no way to actively repeat it) and after trying just about everything like disabling services, running disk and memory tests, changing boot options (like noapic, nolapic, nomodeset and more) or switching off every auto started app, I am tempted to throw my machine out the window.

I am in that category that has lock-ups when actively using the system. Sometimes just a few seconds after login, other times after hours of operation. I find most of the times it happens when I have a few apps open at once (but not always the same) while I am using/touching the **synaptic touchpad** to move the mouse cursor around. Lock-ups never occur when I go away and leave the system running. 

At first I thought it was caused by Skype or FileZilla but those are just two apps I usually have running together with my browser. Then I thought it was caused by the Update Manager because I NEVER ever saw any 'there are upgrades available' notices and each time after a crash there turned out the be upgrades available. But ofcourse in the earliest days there where bound to be upgrades available just about every day ;) So far every theory I could come up with has been falsified...

This morning it all was worse than ever. Had to hard reboot 7 times to even get my browser open! The last time I tried to reboot *without* my WLAN PCMCIA card (a ZyXEL card, p54pci using the **isl3886pci** driver provided in the **linux-firmware-nonfree** package) inserted and since then the system has been up for about 8 hours straight, even with that card reinserted after login... Weird.

I had to insert-remove-insert the card to get networking up and found this in the messages log:

```

### Post by BennyBe on 2010-06-22
Just registered so I can chime in on the discussion (and hopefully add something useful to get it resolved).
I experience the same issue on my Thinkpad T60 (1951-CZ1) - random freezes, most of the time when browsing the web using Chrome (but it froze on other apps already as well). Kernel is 2.6.32-22-generic.

What I have however now discovered is that when I am connected using my Ethernet cable (eth0) I can surf however long I want, and it doesn't freeze once.

I just unplugged the cable, went on surfing (Ubuntu switched to Wireless pretty quickly) and *BAM* - it froze on me after not even 2 minutes or so.

I didn't install any special drivers for my wireless chip.

[FONT="Courier New"]lspci -v[/FONT] shows the following for my wlan0:
```

03:00.0 Ethernet controller: Atheros Communications Inc. AR5212 802.11abg NIC (rev 01)
        Subsystem: IBM Device 058a
        Flags: bus master, fast devsel, latency 0, IRQ 17
        Memory at edf00000 (64-bit, non-prefetchable) [size=64K]
        Capabilities: <access denied>
        Kernel driver in use: ath5k
        Kernel modules: ath5k

```

I am using Ubuntu 10.04 32-bit, freshly installed. I'm not sure why it says 64-bit for this Memory address. I'm also not sure whether the driver is actually correct, since I can only download some Intel Wireless drivers from Lenovo (for Windows).

If anyone knows how to get more details on the chip and/or driver I can paste it here. For now I'm off to surfing via Ethernet - which is ok as a workaround for now, but shouldn't be necessary forever.

### Post by paul.drover on 2010-06-23
FYI, I've added the description of my issues to this bug report: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/427947](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/427947).

It seems to have been idle for several months, but most closely resembles that which I am seeing on my machine. If you are seeing crashes related to suspend or screen-locking in Karmic, Lucid, or even Maverick, you might consider subscribing.

Cheers,
Paul.

### Post by vangop on 2010-06-24
I'm experiencing freezes randomly, nothing in logs, sometimes it is related to power management. like video player inhibiting screensaver and afterwards not properly restoring. 
All  my freeze cases were resolved by either going to other virtual terminal and killing the cause of the problem (use top or guess), in majority of cases video player, or by pressing alt+F1 /alt+f2. For some reason when everything is frozen pressing alt+f1 not opens the menu, but highlights the "applications", and on further presses unfreezes the desktop.
Try if that helps any1.

### Post by StuartN on 2010-06-25
> **shantiq said:**
> Could you explain what this is and what it does for the ones among us who are not so technical   thank you

This disables dynamic kernel ticks, which is a method of squeezing some marginal power saving out of the hardware - [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=651&num=1](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=article&item=651&num=1)

You need to enter **nohz=off** in /etc/default/grub in the line that currently reads **GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"**, so the line becomes **GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash nohz=off"**, and then **sudo update-grub2**.

See [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Grub2) for detailed information on modifying kernel boot options.

---

### Post by chay on 2010-06-25
Hello Shantiq,
Is a kernel parameter (I'm about to test it, just waiting the freeze).
Edit the file /boot/grub/menu.lst and in your "kernel" line add it at the end.
Mine looks like this:

```

title       Ubuntu 10.04 LTS, kernel 2.6.32-22-generic
root        (hd0,2)
kernel      /vmlinuz-2.6.32-22-generic root=UUID=3cfde426-79dd-47d6-96e4-8ee8ba0a0d48 ro quiet splash nohz=off
initrd      /initrd.img-2.6.32-22-generic
quiet

```

HTH.

Also:
I don't have gnome-power-manager running (my machine is a desktop).
I checked /var/log/messages, and seems like the freeze happens after any segfault of any program (but no more messages gets printed, until "manual" reboot).
The most popular is npviewer.bin (in libflashplayer.so), but could be any one.

Regards!

ok answering my own question regarding update to 2.6.34 kernel

[/COLOR][/B]                     [SIZE=3][COLOR=DarkRed]**                         [Linux Kernel 2.6.34  installation guide for Ubuntu Linux 10.04]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/2010/05/linux-kernel-2-6-34-installation-guide-for-ubuntu-linux-10-04/")                     **[/COLOR][/SIZE]

                                                                    Published on  19 May,  2010 in [Linux]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/category/projects/linux-projects/"). [19 Comments]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/2010/05/linux-kernel-2-6-34-installation-guide-for-ubuntu-linux-10-04/#comments") Tags: [Linux]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/tag/linux/"), [nVIDIA]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/tag/nvidia/"), [Ubuntu]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/tag/ubuntu/").                     
                                       
                                                           This short walkthrough describes how to get the latest  linux kernel working under Ubuntu Linux without having to compile it  yourself.
 This tutorial should work with the latest version of Ubuntu Linux  (10.04 ) and all distributions based on these versions of Ubuntu Linux  like Mint.
 The included kernel files have been compiled using the generic ubuntu  configuration. 
**Note:** nVIDIA ForceWare drivers are  automatically installed using DKMS
 **Installation Guide**
 
[LIST=1]
[*]Download [linux-headers-2.6.34-020634_2.6.34-020634_all.deb]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/linux-headers-2.6.34-020634_2.6.34-020634_all.deb")
[*]Download your kernel headers package;
I386:  [linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb")
AMD64: [linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/linux-headers-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb")
[*]Download your kernel compile;
I386:  [linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_i386.deb")
AMD64: [linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb")
[*]Install the files in the same order (else it won`t work!)
[*]In the terminal run:
sudo update-grub
[*]Reboot and select the kernel from the bootloader menu
[/LIST]
 [COLOR=Blue]**From[ here]("http://www.ramoonus.nl/2010/05/linux-kernel-2-6-34-installation-guide-for-ubuntu-linux-10-04/")**[/COLOR]


[COLOR=Blue][B]Did not even know what a kernel actually was until I read this



[/B][/COLOR]**Kernel basic  facilities**

 The kernel's primary purpose is to manage the computer's resources  and allow other programs to run and use these resources.[[1]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kernel_%28computing%29#cite_note-Wulf74-0")  Typically, the resources consist of:
 
[LIST]
[*]The [Central Processing  Unit]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Processing_Unit") (CPU, the processor). This is the most central part of a  computer system, responsible for *running* or *executing*  programs on it. The kernel takes responsibility for deciding at any time  which of the many running programs should be allocated to the processor  or processors (each of which can usually run only one program at a  time)
[*]The computer's [memory]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random-access_memory"). Memory is used to store both  program instructions and data. Typically, both need to be present in  memory in order for a program to execute. Often multiple programs will  want access to memory, frequently demanding more memory than the  computer has available. The kernel is responsible for deciding which  memory each process can use, and determining what to do when not enough  is available.
[*]Any [Input/Output (I/O)]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Input/output") devices present in the  computer, such as keyboard, mouse, disk drives, printers, displays, etc.  The kernel allocates requests from applications to perform I/O to an  appropriate device (or subsection of a device, in the case of files on a  disk or windows on a display) and provides convenient methods for using  the device (typically abstracted to the point where the application  does not need to know implementation details of the device).
[/LIST]
 Key aspects necessary in resource managements are the definition of  an execution domain ([address  space]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Address_space")) and the protection mechanism used to mediate the accesses to  the resources within a domain.[[1]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kernel_%28computing%29#cite_note-Wulf74-0")
 Kernels also usually provide methods for [synchronization]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synchronization_%28computer_science%29") and [communication]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inter-process_communication") between processes  (called *inter-process communication* or IPC).
 A kernel may implement these features itself, or rely on some of the  processes it runs to provide the facilities to other processes, although  in this case it must provide some means of IPC to allow processes to  access the facilities provided by each other.
 Finally, a kernel must provide running programs with a method to make  requests to access these facilities.

[B][COLOR=Blue] 


[/COLOR][/B]

### Post by saejin on 2010-06-25
I have seen the same thing, and now have a bit more to offer.  I must confess, I lost the faith, and went back to Ubuntu 9.10   ....
much to my surprise saw the same problems. ..... So I went to Fidora13 ... and saw the same problems ... then the network stopped linking up.....
My mother board is an ASUS P755D-E-Premium    only  a few months old..... so I pulled half my memory down to 4 Gig.  The system would not boot.... I pulled it all, and reset the ram, and started from scratch .....  This time I locked out the automatic overclocking adjustment on the mother board.

No further problems to report.....  That little hardware fiasco cost me a couple of hundred hours and a few inches of exposed forehead......

I was only inches from buying a machintosh .....

Different behavior from the old 2.6.32-22.36 kernel.

- New kernel stays longer (much longer!), but still freeze.
- Now you'll get blinking leds! :-D :-D.
- You'll get new behavior from the new kernel, but no real solution.
- If you have this freeze issue, I fully recomend the new kernel: you'll get the freeze, but in more large time spans.

### Post by rpimn on 2010-06-27
I had used Ubuntu 8.04 for Desktop on a Toshiba Tecra 2.2GHz Centrino Duo T7500, 2GB DDR2, 160GB HDD, Intel Mobile GM965/GL960 Graphic Card without any problem for about 2 yrs. 

When Ubuntu 10.4 (Lucid Lynx) was released a few weeks ago, I removed the Hardy Heron, and made a clean installation of Lucid Lynx (linux kernel: 2.6.32-21) on this laptop a week ago. I experienced the random freeze only once yesterday.

Based on this thread discussion, 
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765), and 
[http://fedoraforum.org/forum/showthread.php?t=245752](http://fedoraforum.org/forum/showthread.php?t=245752) (random freeze on Fedora 13), I would assume, the cause of this problem may well be the kernel and graphic card drivers.

The mostly recommended solution I have read so far is to install the latest stable release of kernel, such as 2.6.34, which may reduce the random freeze. This indicates a compatibility issue between some linux kernels and graphic card drivers. I hope, this is not an early indication made by major linux distributors that linux must be designed for convenience at any cost.

### Post by edwardLS on 2010-06-29
I have been following this thread for quite some time now.  I have resisted the temptation to respond until now because I was not sure I had anything new to add.  Here is my situation:
I installed Lucid on a Dell Studio 1555 within a couple days of the release of Lucid.  On that system I have not experience any of these problems.  However, I finally got around to installing Lucid on my Desktop; I should say "Tried to install..."  I have (tried) installed 32 and 64 bit using the Desktop CD and the Alternate CD.  My mouse and keyboard go dead very quickly.  In approx 75% of the attempts, I can't even make it through the 'Installation' without the mouse and keyboard going dead.  I have noticed that it does not appear that the system hangs.  That is, when the mouse and keyboard go dead, if I had something running prior to this event, it continues to run, and the progress bar continues to fill in until it is complete.  I only install LTS releases on the Desktop.  I reinstalled Hardy Heron 64 bit, and it runs solidly.  I have left this Desktop on 24/7 to see if it will ever have the problem.  So far, it has not.  I did at one time get to the point where I was attempting to update the kernel to 2.6.34, but during that installation the problem occurred, and then the system was corrupted to the point that it would not boot.  Lucid on this Desktop is totally unusable!

### Post by edwardLS on 2010-06-30
I have solved my problem, I think!  I am not at all sure that my problem matches precisely with those of others described here.  My Mouse and Keyboard were freezing extremely frequently.  I was unable to complete the Installation to hard drive from the Live CD of Lucid approximately 80-90% of the time.  On the couple occasions that I got the installation onto the hard drive, the system was (my opinion) totally unusable.  The Mouse and Keyboard would freeze up within approximately 10-15 minutes after booting up; sometimes even less than the 10-15 minutes.  It seemed to me that the SYSTEM was not hung, because on the occasion where I was able to use the mouse/keyboard to get something started, it continued after the mouse/keyboard froze.  

Yesterday, I booted the LiveCD AMD64 Desktop version.  At the menu, I clicked F6 and set "noapic".  The installation went to completion with no trouble.  After booting the Hard Drive installation, I edited /etc/default/grub, and set "noapic".  I have been beating on this system since early last evening with no problems.  Many of the things that would result in a mouse/keyboard freeze now worked without the annoying freeze.

I had previously run Hardy Heron 32-bit for two years with no "noapic" setting.  So, while I am not an expert, my thinking leads me to wonder if the removal of HAL in Lucid has a problem with the APIC?  Afterall, APIC, mouse, and keyboard are Hardware!  Just a thought!

### Post by frbastida on 2010-07-01
You must modify the configuration file of GRUB 
  su gedit /etc/default/grub

and modify  

  GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash  apm=power-off acpi=off noapic"

but shut down have to be manually after system halts

---

### Post by Marko Grönroos on 2010-07-06
I'm also experiencing random freezes and it makes my life very anguished. They don't always freeze up the entire machine, but often just one process, the mouse, the keyboard, it varies a bit. The freezes last from 20 seconds to a few minutes. During that time, two to four processors jump to 100% (see the figure below - the topmost curve is the average).

[IMG]http://www.nic.funet.fi/%7Emagi/tmp/freeze-problem-processor-loads.png[/IMG]

The "top" command doesn't show any particular activity with regular  processes, but the overall load goes to 5 or so.

Tried the noapic nolapic acpi=off parameters, they just made it worse, the keyboard started losing typing. Also updated to 2.6.34-020634 and also tried the older 2.6.31 kernel, but to no avail.

The problem is affected by *something*, as it is sometimes very peaceful for long times, while sometimes the problem occurs every few minutes. Can't say is it Firefox, Thunderbird, Skype or something else. Uninstalling nepomuk may have made the problem more rare. No Flash graphics has been running when the problem has occurred. Enabling the desktop effects might even help a bit, so it's probably not that.

Kubuntu 10.04 (upgraded from hardy), x86_64, GeForce 8800 GT with 'nvidia' driver 195.36.24.

### Post by cmcanulty on 2010-07-06
I lose mouse and trackpad after every hibernate, then must restart or open terminal and type sudo modprobe psmouse. Very frustrating. Started with Lucid install. Is there a shortcut for this command or a permanent fix?

---

### Post by Marko Grönroos on 2010-07-06
It looks like my biggest problems were caused by the Nepomuk service. I removed it entirely with:

```
$ sudo apt-get remove virtuoso-nepomuk
```The service is left running even after removing it, so the machine must be rebooted.

Well, this may not be the underlying cause, just something that made it worse. There are still small occasional pauses, a bit annoying but not fatal. Not sure if the problem is or was the same as others are experiencing.

---

### Post by hoover67 on 2010-07-07
Suffered another freeze on kernel .35rc1 last night, but this time only the keyboard seemed dead while the mouse still worked. 

However I was able to switch to a virtual console by crtl-alt-F2, and a start / stop gdm as "root" restored keyboard input in X. 

So to me, it looks more and more like this problem is gnome / X related. 

Kernel version: 2.6.35-020635rc1-generic #020635rc1 SMP Tue Jun 1 18:38:58 UTC 2010 i686 GNU/Linux

More info on my setup can be found in the dpaste.com log I posted in an earlier reply. 

All the best, Uwe

### Post by billharris on 2010-07-07
I had my first two Lucid freezes this week, after upgrading from Karmic last weekend. :-(  This is all too reminiscent of Jaunty, which was, for me, almost enough to send me back to Debian.  

I have no real insights to offer yet, but I also have no desire to spend the next six months troubleshooting this.  If I don't find an answer without too much trouble, I will install Debian this time. :-( 

I do have two suggestions from Jaunty days (apologies if this repeats something I missed in this thread):

[LIST]
[*]Be careful in your troubleshooting not to fall for superstitions.  Test out theories carefully, trying hard to prove them wrong.  We spent a lot of time in Jaunty time chasing chimeras, but I don't think we ever found the real problem.
[*]Remember Alt-SysRq reisub (look it up before you try it); it can be a nice way to recover as nicely as I know how from a hard freeze.  I had forgotten the incantation when this happened, and so I can't promise it works on my Lucid crashes.
[/LIST]

FWIW, I am again noticing that top reports essentially all of my 4GB RAM in use.  That's the same symptom as in my Jaunty problems.  Then it was often Firefox; killing and restarting FF could delay the eventual freeze.

### Post by twister77 on 2010-07-08
Installed 64bit Lucid when it came out on my new build, nothing too special (AMD Athlon II 245, ECS 6100PM mobo, nvidia 9800GT video card, 500GB WD hard drive).  It started having random I/O freezes a week later.  Tried Karmic, froze immediately.  Tried Jaunty, froze after a week.   After I tried Ibex, which froze immediately, I started thinking that it might be something with my hardware.  I looked up on the net what kind of hardware might cause random I/O freezes, and one thing that came up was SATA cable.  It shouldn't be bent or creased.  Well, in my quest for impeccable cable management, my SATA cable was all of the above (see the pic).  I installed new SATA cable with no bends, did a fresh Lucid 64bit install and after 6 weeks without freezes, I have to say I have found what was the cause.  I know that this post might not help everyone who has the freezing issue, but that's what it was in my case.

### Post by silver.cps on 2010-07-09
Hi all,


I've been bothered by the same problem. In my case, just after installation no problems have occurred, but then, after some new packages, some updates... boom... Most times the hang occurs right after gdm login screen appears. Sometimes I am lucky login in, but hangs if I leave the computer with no use.

I think I have a workaround. For those of you who just use the computer with a unique graphical session, it will be a good workaround. It is because gdm will not be used, and hence, may be the great trouble maker.

Well. The workaroud that is working, at least for me, follows:

- After powering on, wait for grub menu. Then select to boot from safe/recovery mode.
- From recovery options, select just to resume and continue login
- You will be left on console login.
- Type your user name and password.
- After loggin in, just start graphics:
$ startx

I hope this can help many of you who want to keep using Ubuntu.

If some of you try this workaround and does not succeed, unfortunately I do not have much time to help. So please, if you post some direct question for me, I am afraid I will not able to help. So don't get me wrong. 

Cheers.

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-07-10
> **ajwho said:**
> What's the best way to recover from a frozen screen if the keyboard is still working?

Press Alt and SysReq (my SysReq is on the PrntScrn key, near top right) and, whilst holding Alt-SysReq, press R, E, I, S, U and B ("busier" spelled backwards).

### Post by rchar66 on 2010-07-10

After I did that I never had another freeze up again. And it's been a couple of months now.

### Post by garth.d on 2010-07-10
It's been a while since I last posted.  My brother and I run near identical laptops - his with xubuntu, mine with ubuntu.  Mine freezed anywhere from 3-5 times a day, his not even once.  So I ditched gnome altogether.  I've been running a xubuntu 10.04 config now for about three weeks - and not a single freeze at all.  Whilst I don't particularly like xfce I'm perfectly willing to sacrifice some usability for stability.  

Furthermore, _I AM_ aware that some people have experienced freezes/crashes in xfce as well as kde.  Thus I believe the problem to be deeply rooted in the kernel, though it does not affect all in exactly the same way for some unknown reason.

For me at least, the problem seemed to be with gnome only (tried kde as well, no problems).  My suggestion to gnome/kde users out there is to try a fresh install of xubuntu and try it out for a while.  Maybe it will solve your problems, maybe it won't - just give it a go if possible.

And for everyone else for whom this is not an option I sincerely hope a fix gets released soon.

Regards.

### Post by Olly TH on 2010-07-10
I have experienced the same freeze described in (most of) the posts in this thread: PC still running, but no mouse, no keyboard, just the power-off button reacts and shut down my PC cleanly. (PC is a 64-bit Intel Quad-Core Q6600, motherboard P5N-MX, 2 GB RAM)

I was thus suspecting something like a lock in the kernel that would make the USB non-functional. A solution that *completely* solved the problem for me was installing the linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64.deb from <[http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/")>. Since then, zero freeze. I conclude it was a kernel issue!

Regards,

Olly.

### Post by HardBall.21 on 2010-07-11
I think there is a minimum two separate issues, one having to do with the some low level operation of the wireless driver, and the other having to do with some other kernel event like an operation by the MMU on some other I/O device; and probably more than just these two (ACPI, etc).  

The issue I experience, I'm almost 100% certain has to do with the wireless. It is definitely a very low level issue, where no trace dmesg or any system logs records the event.  All elements of the interface freeze at the exact same time, instantly, including keyboard, mouse, display, sound.  The blinking cap-locks is always there, and shows no patterns codes that are typical of HP laptops having some hardware issue that can be self diagnosed.

The kicker that makes me so certain is that: it only ever happens with a single router (not at my home), and only happens after the router gives out a prolonged burst of ARPs, this happens soon after a period of several minutes of failed DNS requests on the network, as I observed.  The router is also very tightly controlled in terms of network association and traffice, probably runs some kind of directory authentication through LDAP with an external gateway w.r.t. users on the network (can't be sure since I'm not the administrator for this network, it is at a local establishment that I frequent).

I cannot reproduce the behavior any where else, or with other routers.  But it is highly reliable, whether my system is waking up from sleep, or fresh booted, with or without ACPI flag.  It happens mostly 2-5 minutes after the described the behavior in the router, or shortly after DHCPACK, both only when some data is being actively transferred through wireless.  Haven't had more time to look into it.  But hopefully this will help to clear the muddle of several different issues with similar behavior up a bit.

---

### Post by ukdoc on 2010-07-11
Ok, I'm another frustrated upgrader and I've added myself to the bug tracker mentioned earlier, so it's up to 65 now including me!

I've been using jaunty and karmic without any problems whatsoever. I mainly use my linux box as an ssh server but also have freenx running on it so have a number of options of accessing remotely, and I usually leave an nx window running with digiguide, which I can serve over the web, and jbidwatcher so i can snipe ebay items, or can tunnel in to my network. It has always been left running 24/7 with the monitor off as it's fairly unpredictable when I'm going to use it. I can also use it via ssh as a socks proxy so I can get my gmail while at work (normally blocked by our network). There is no wireless or bluetooth. I can't remember exactly what processor or motherboard it has but it's some kind of compaq desktop. 

Did an upgrade via the upgrade manager on Saturday night thinking it's an LTS release, what can go wrong, and all seemed to go well. So just left it on overnight. Strangely in the morning I couldn't get into ssh. I can ping the machine but ssh just hangs while trying to exchange keys. When I went to the machine it had completely frozen and I had to hard reset holding the power button. Seemed to be ok again but now I'm at work and can't access the bloody ubuntu machine. I temporarily opened a port on my router to access my windows machine's ssh server and can ping the ubuntu machine, but can't ssh in. If I try to vnc in using ultravnc I get "connection failed - end of stream. possible causes: another user is already listening on this id, bad connection". If I try to log in using tunnelier it gets to "starting first key exchange" then just hangs.

I'll have to try the AltSysrq REISUB to see if that's still working.

I suspect if I upgrade the kernel to 2.6.34 it may stop freezing.

After the first freeze I didn't notice anything obvious in any of the logs, although there were some unusual things in auth.log relating to keys that I've not seen before.

If all else fails I may have to do a clean install of karmic and start all over again, but I've got so many other things to do that take priority. Should have maybe delayed the upgrade until I had a few days to sort out such problems!

### Post by StuartN on 2010-07-12
> **ukdoc said:**
> I suspect if I upgrade the kernel to 2.6.34 it may stop freezing.

I have had kernel 2.6.34 since May 19th without a single freeze. I have tried every update kernel (currently 2.6.32-22 I think) and every one of them freezes.

I do not believe that kernel 2.6.34 contains a fix for the defect causing the freezing (the machine can not resume if it is suspended with an SD card mounted, and Nautilus crashes on some file operations, and there is "corrupted lower memory"), but it works around it to the extent that it has not yet frozen. I assume it would freeze under the right (or wrong!) conditions.

This must be a hardware problem, but the common factor is still unknown - it looks to me like some machines have an out-of-spec disk response time that is interpreted as a hardware error and flags the system until the next reboot. Several people (like me) have stated that they have identical affected laptops, but other unaffected machines with different hardware.

### Post by ukdoc on 2010-07-12
Ok, a bit more information that might be helpful.

From System Monitor: Release 10.04, Kernel linux 2.6.34-020634-generic, GNOME 2.30.2
Memory 2.0 GiB, Processor 0 Intel(R) Pentium(R) 4 CPU 2.80GHz, Processor 1 same as 0
File systems: /dev/sda1 - ext4

I got home and found the ubuntu machine frozen. I managed to reboot with Alt-Sysrq-REISUB.

Installed kernel 2.6.34 - rebooted.

Left a couple of nx windows running and left to stew for a couple of hours. Actually not frozen but checked system monitor and CPU1 was running at 100%, CPU0 at 15%.

Things started to run very slowly.

After a 'w' command, I could see the loads gradually going up and up, from 6-7 to 45-55!!

I ran a ps aux command and found the following entry with CPU 85%!!

```
root       807  2.9  1.0  36392 22284 tty7     Ss+  21:35   0:25 /usr/bin/X :0 -nr -verbose -auth /var/run/gdm/auth-for-gdm-Mntj8A/database -nolisten tcp vt7
```

After a reboot seems to be calm again. I'm going to keep an eye on this.

Does this suggest the problem is something to do with gnome as some people have suspected? Or the X server.

As an aside, I also noticed that I can't get into tty1 by pressing Ctrl-Alt-F1 immediately after a reboot; there is just a flashing cursor in the top left. However, if I log into my account on gnome I can then get into tty1.

I'm not very advanced with linux but I hope that what I've written may help someone, and I'd be happy to provide details of any logs if you feel that would help. Just point me in the right direction!

### Post by sensemagic on 2010-07-13

I really feel that there has to be an issue deep in the kernel, as there have been reports of issues like this on Ubuntu and all it's related distros as well as I think Fedora. ### Post by billharris on 2010-07-13
This (both the responses here and the reported symptoms) sound reminiscent of Jaunty. 

A few thoughts.

Unless one of us has a solution, I don't believe we'll get results by posting problems here.  (Ubuntuforums are great for mutual self-help.)  Learn how to submit bug reports, find the page about reporting freezes, and then submit there.  People maintaining Ubuntu may not see what you post here; they will see bug reports.

It _may_ be that upgrading the kernel (or switching off apic or getting rid of proprietary drivers or ...) will fix the problem, but I'm not optimistic.  There was a lot of that on Jaunty, always followed by reports of a crash even in that configuration.  Changing something, seeing no crashes for some period of time on one machine, and then calling that a fix is what I meant when I referred to "superstition" previously.  

I think this is a vitally important problem to resolve.  I think it won't be resolved until someone finds the offending code and fixes it. I think the people finding and fixing code defects need data -- good data and lots of it.  

Go to [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze) to figure out what it means to troubleshoot a freeze.  Go to [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs) to figure out how to report bugs.  Emotion doesn't help fix this; data does.  The variety of machines out here makes it challenging, so we need to give them all the help we can.

Off my soapbox. :-)

---

I think this is a vitally important problem to resolve.  I think it won't be resolved until someone finds the offending code and fixes it. I think the people finding and fixing code defects need data -- good data and lots of it.  

Go to [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze) to figure out what it means to troubleshoot a freeze.  Go to [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/ReportingBugs) to figure out how to report bugs.  Emotion doesn't help fix this; data does.  The variety of machines out here makes it challenging, so we need to give them all the help we can.

Off my soapbox. :-)

For anyone wanting the bug report it's here:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765)

Please add anything useful there.

Maybe the OP can update the topic to have the link?

---

### Post by billharris on 2010-07-14
As an example of the strange nature, my system froze today with Epiphany active.  When I killed Epiphany from a virtual console, everything started to work again.  AFAIK, Epiphany wasn't even running the other two times my system froze.

Also, I had reported high memory usage.  Apparently that may be intended as a way to speed the system.  Memory usage as reported by top was only about 75% of RAM this time.

BTW, any idea why top and the System Monitor report vastly different amounts of memory usage?  Right now, System Monitor reports 1.1GB in use, while top reports 3.1GB.

---

### Post by TCWriter on 2010-07-14
> **billharris said:**
> 
Unless one of us has a solution, I don't believe we'll get results by posting problems here.  (Ubuntuforums are great for mutual self-help.)  Learn how to submit bug reports, find the page about reporting freezes, and then submit there.  People maintaining Ubuntu may not see what you post here; they will see bug reports.

It _may_ be that upgrading the kernel (or switching off apic or getting rid of proprietary drivers or ...) will fix the problem, but I'm not optimistic.  There was a lot of that on Jaunty, always followed by reports of a crash even in that configuration.  Changing something, seeing no crashes for some period of time on one machine, and then calling that a fix is what I meant when I referred to "superstition" previously.

Off my soapbox. :-)

You might consider it optimism (or superstition), but my 10.04 system would freeze fairly quickly (under 15 minutes) until I started booting under NOAPIC (a trick I believe I learned from someone much earlier in the thread).

It's been rock solid for more than a week of everyday use (like 9.10 always was, and I'm running the two on the same machine), so this isn't just superstition.

For me, it's a fix.

It's pretty clear this "bug" is mostly likely several issues rolled together, but suggesting that our only recourse is to report the issue and wait for the developers to stumble on something feels a little passive.

I found a fix here; perhaps a few other people have too. 

In the meantime, I'm listed on the bug reports, and it would be nice if the developers found something, but in the meantime - and thanks to the folks in this thread - my machine is running pretty much exactly like it should.
