### Post by d_in_Conduct on 2010-07-16
I don't have a million posts like some of you, but I have been using Linux since the 1.0 kernel (or so) and I have tweaked my system into oblivion plenty of times.  So I'm far from expert but not exactly a Linux virgin, either.

This machine is running on an Intel core 2 duo E8500 with 4 Gb RAM. My freeze problems began with the upgrade to kernel 2.6.32.23.  That and possibly something else that was offered in upgrade manager at the same time started causing trouble.  If it had been windows, my first thought would have been "interrupt conflict" because I almost always had to "do something" (move the mouse, scroll a window, launch/close a program, etc) to cause a freeze.  My system runs 24/7 with a Python program I wrote sending commands out the serial port and starting/stopping recording using ecasound at all hours.  I don't recall it ever freezing up while unattended.

I reinstalled Lucid and left off all visual effects (fun, but not important) and did not activate the proprietary Nvidia driver (I don't miss it).  Still, the freezes persisted.

But since installing 2.6.34-020634-generic, my system has been rock solid (for about two weeks).  I don't think I'm going to do any more updates, and I'm not going to run visual effects nor the Nvidia driver.  It's great just the way it is.

I ran 8.10 on my laptop with no updates until Lucid.  Updates kept killing wireless connectivity on that one.  Update Manager is offering the .23 kernel at this time.  I just stop there.

I'm running 64-bit on all my machines.  I think one laptop is running AMD dual-core and the other Intel i3 quad-core.

### Post by siabost on 2010-07-16
This is a hell of a long thread!

Good news first:
I have a year-old PC and it has run Ubuntu 10.04 rock-solidly since installation. Previously to that it ran 9.10 also perfectly.
System info -
AMD Athlon(tm) 7750 dual-core Processor
1.8Gb Memory
nVidia onboard chip running nVidia accelerated graphics driver (version 173)
Ubuntu 10.04 32bit
Kernel 2.6.32-23-generic

And the bad news:
Dell 2350, P4 2Gz, 1.5Gb memory - 4 or 5 years old.
Ubuntu 10.04 & UbuntuStudio 10.04 both freeze & the screen goes black followed by an alternating flashing of vertical stripes.
Mandriva 2010 & Fedora 13* both freeze (in Fedora 13* the mouse/cursor still moves but nothing responds & the keyboard is inactive).
Mandriva 2009, Ubuntu 9.10 & Mepis 8.5 performed flawlessly - absolutely no freezes. *In Fedora 13 kernel 2.6.33.3-85.fc13.i686 seemed to work fine but after a standard update kernel 2.6.33.6-147.fc13.i686 it suffered freezes frequently. Interestingly Fedora's crash detector detected nothing and I could find nothing useful in var/log/dmesg.

I also know that a friend with Packard Bell PC of similar vintage suffered complete freeze ups (mouse and keyboard) with 10.04 but without the screen going black. As he is a newbie I rolled him back to 9.10 which, unsurprisingly, is its usual rock-solid self.

Although the kernel numbers don't match up, I feel that this a kernel problem and is not limited to one distribution. It also seems to affect older equipment much more. If it's a problem with recent kernels reacting badly with certain (older?) hardware then the symptoms will also surely vary a somewhat.

I'm off to boot up Fedora 13 again but with NOAPIC to see if that has any affect.

Most dispiriting all this.

I will add my tuppence-worth to the bug log later.

---

### Post by ELD on 2010-07-16
Well i have done a fresh install today this time 32bit PAE (i refuse to use 64bit until flash has an official 64bit release again...sad i know..but half the web uses flash i swear...).

I am seriously starting to think it is EXT4 that is doing it, i know someone else said they went from ext4 to ext3 using some convertor or something but that could still leave issues, so far so good.

### Post by billharris on 2010-07-17
> **TCWriter said:**
> You might consider it optimism (or superstition), but my 10.04 system would freeze fairly quickly (under 15 minutes) until I started booting under NOAPIC (a trick I believe I learned from someone much earlier in the thread).

It's been rock solid for more than a week of everyday use (like 9.10 always was, and I'm running the two on the same machine), so this isn't just superstition.

For me, it's a fix.


I'm glad it's a fix for you.  My Lucid freezes about twice a week; when it froze today, I set noapic.  It froze within hours. :-(  This one was a really hard freeze; I had to drop the battery out of the laptop for a bit before I could persuade it to come back on (I had killed it, as always, with REISUB; I've never had problems turning it back on afterwards).

As one of the links I posted earlier said, there are many potential causes for freezes.  A fix that works for you may not work for me.  A "fix" that is followed by no freezes for x [hours|days] isn't necessarily a fix of a random failure if it occurs later.  

On Jaunty, I tried a number of kernel upgrades, boot options, and other "fixes," all to no avail.  The only "fix" was a fresh install of Karmic.  I'm not inclined to try each and every fix here in the hopes that one will work for me -- I have better things to do with my time.  

I checked out the noapic suggestion, and there are claims around that Linux in general has been slow at working well with APIC.  Those same claims seem to suggest all those problems are behind us, but I decided it was at least worth trying.  

If I see a fix where the suggestion seems to have a logical relationship from cause to effect, I may try it.  I may also just try lenny.

### Post by RavanH on 2010-07-18
> **tonytraductor said:**
> What's really happening here is that the keyboard and mouse no longer illicit any response.
The system, apparently, is still running.
Currently, my netbook is playing Carlinhos Brown on Last.FM.
I can hear the music.
I can also see the CPU monitor in the taskbar moving.
But I can't type or move the mouse (typing here from my debian/stable desktop, not the xubuntu 9.10 netbook of which I write).

This thread, as I understand, was originally about COMPLETE system lock-up since Lucid. Not a freeze that can be reset by Alt+SysRq+B or allows audio or other processes to continue in the background... But only a full hard reset/reboot ( by keeping the shutdown button pressed for 4 secs for example ) can revive the system. If you'r not seeing that, you probably are facing other issues:

I have had similar problems to the quote above ( system irresponsive to mouse or keyboard imput but actually still active ) with an older Asus laptop since Karmic. This was due to some BIOS issues combined with Intel Graphics i855GM issues and kernel mode setting (KMS). You might want to try the installation procedure described on [http://blog.cyril-ravat.fr/post/2009/10/25/Ubuntu-karmic-freeze-on-Asus-M2n](http://blog.cyril-ravat.fr/post/2009/10/25/Ubuntu-karmic-freeze-on-Asus-M2n) to get your machine stable... 

Hope that helps some.

I have had similar problems to the quote above ( system irresponsive to mouse or keyboard imput but actually still active ) with an older Asus laptop since Karmic. This was due to some BIOS issues combined with Intel Graphics i855GM issues and kernel mode setting (KMS). You might want to try the installation procedure described on [http://blog.cyril-ravat.fr/post/2009/10/25/Ubuntu-karmic-freeze-on-Asus-M2n](http://blog.cyril-ravat.fr/post/2009/10/25/Ubuntu-karmic-freeze-on-Asus-M2n) to get your machine stable... 

Hope that helps some.

Some of us I think have had problems at both ends of the spectrum, and are following this thread in the hope of finding a solution to any kind of freeze situation. The partial and total freezes may or may not be interrelated. Each case may have an individual cause and solution. My solution was a clean re-install of Lucid, which stopped the total freeze-ups, although partials still occur.

YMMV. Here it looks as if **everybody's** mileage varies.

### Post by NoVista on 2010-07-19
The system here has locked up with the Glib warning on a black screen ever since I upgraded. Running on ext.3.
Thankfully I have never lost any data, even though I have lots of things running at once, along with VM Workstation. 
This same machine also still runs Gutsy and Edgy flawlessly.

I also have an old Dell 600 laptop that has never had this issue.
(Clean install, ext4).

I've reported what I could on the error, and dissapointed to find out this far down the road, and with everyone's help, this issue still exists.
Really disappointed as this is a LTR. 

I've been in puters since you had to walk around the back of the 5 foot cabinet and plug in all the wires just to run a program. This is utter nonsense that such a piece of garbage as this has been released by the Ubuntu Team as a LTR. Call that last sentence a rant if you wish, but in my computer circles, Ubuntu has lost a lot of respect among us with this release.

I am unsure what I want to do at this point. I was waiting for 10.04, and was planning on just running it until the next LTR. That plan has now had to be aborted. It was not my plan to go with the next upgrade of Ubuntu, but now, if I stay with Ubuntu, I would have no other choice, hoping that will resolve the problem. But at this point, no one knows it will.

Perhaps the best thing, (for me), to do is sadly abort Ubuntu altogether, and go to Debian stable, as was mentioned in some posts earlier in this thread.

### Post by tjtremor on 2010-07-20
Here's my take on the system hang-up for Ubuntu 10.04

1st had to install the linux-image-2.6.34-020634-generic_2.6.34-020634_amd64 since all other kernels lock up,

2nd had to change my memory timmings to DDR800 4-4-4-12T-2T memory from AUTO which resulted in 5-5-5-12T-2T (even though all other ubuntu version and windows run fine at AUTO)

3rd the radeon-hd driver included is broken and locks up the system, had to remove it, disable effects and install the fglrx with no effects

Since I wanted my special effects enabled, I had to switch to my Nvidia card...
well the Nvidia drivers are also broken, had to compile/install manually

get NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-195.36.24-pkg2.run
gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist.conf
blacklist vga16fb
blacklist nouveau
blacklist rivafb
blacklist nvidiafb
blacklist rivatv
sudo apt-get remove nvidia-* --purge
restart to terminal run level 3
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-195.36.24-pkg2.run

*** that runs fine for days as long as I don't touch my wireless and don't use fuseblk for my NTFS drive 
*** I also suspect more things broken software wise with this Ubuntu 10.4

---

### Post by todak on 2010-07-21
When I have Firefox running and the freeze happens, I can switch tabs, but I cannot scroll with the the mouse, only with the keyboard. After a couple of minutes, I can scroll again with the mouse, but cannot switch tabs with it. On other apps such as Rhythmbox or Kpat or Oo, clicking on buttons or menu headings has no effect for a period of time, but random minutes later, the buttons can be clicked, but no scrolling with the mouse, but the keyboard shortcuts work just fine and I can type text into places that expect text. I can do everything with keyboard shortcuts and tabbing around, but the mouse clicks are unresponsive. I can move the cusror around, but clicking is ignored. However, when I issue the three finger salute (Ctrl+Alt+Delete) and the shutdown menu appears, I do not restart. I click the "Cancel" button and everything is back in working order for the duration of my session, no matter how much time. It seems odd that I can use the mouse just fine in the shutdown dialog, but clicking anywhere on the desktop outside of the shutdown dialog has no effect. If I do restart, the freeze happens again after a random amount of time, but is quickly remedied with the shutdown-menu click-on-cancel-button routine and all is well for the duration of the session. I wonder if it might be a mouse driver gone awry? :confused:

### Post by siabost on 2010-07-21
_A 10.04 SET-UP THAT WORKS!_

As there seems to be a lot of conflicting ideas about what causes this freeze up, I thought it would be useful to give details of a system that works "out of the box" _***with no freezing & no tweaking***_. Then a quick check of the report list attached will allow dismissal of single hardware/kernel/driver problems - though it won't prove that combinations of such are not the problem if one of your components is not listed here.

_General_:
Ubuntu 10.04 (lucid)
Kernel Linux 2.6.32-23-generic
GNOME 2.30.2
Memory 1.8GiB
Processor: AMD Athlon(tm) 7750 Dual-Core Processor

See attached report for details produced via HardInfo 0.5.1

My freezing problems relate to an older Dell PC I run as a second machine. I will run a similar report on that one for comparison.
:)

### Post by 0-merlin-0 on 2010-07-25
Hello folks, this is my first post here and, for what it's worth, I might have a tip on the freezing issue.

A couple of days back I bought a new computer as the old one managed to keep going for more than 10 years but finally passed away. This new system should run both windows 7 as well as Ubuntu. First the specs of the system:

asus p55d
intel i7 870
4Gb ram (Kinston)
1Tb wd caviar black
Ati radeon 5450

After installing windows 7 everything ran fine and from this I conclude that the hardware is ok. I have to admit being fairly impressed by the ease of installation, the looks of w7, especially as I got the system up and running within only one hour.   


Then, with installing ubuntu 10.04 the mess started. I experienced the same freezes as described on the more than 70 pages of this thread. No mouse, no keyboard, no intriguing key combo' s...a blunt reset is all I can do to get out of it.


I tried different things to solve this:

ubuntu 10.04 32bit: freeze problem
ubuntu 10.04 64bit: freeze problem
ubuntu 9.?? 32bit: freeze problem
ubuntu 9.?? 64bit: freeze problem
ubuntu 8: install failed.

I also tried various kernel parameters: freeze problem persists

I tried a different, main stream, kernel: freeze problem persists

I reinstalled the 64 bit version of ubuntu 10.04, this time on ext3 instead of 4: freeze problem persists.

Fairly pissed I went to the back of the computer and tore out the mouse. The mouse is a logitech mx400 usb with a green adapter plug to fit it in a small ps/2 slot. I took the adapter off and plunged the mouse directly into the first free usb port I could find. The mouse immediatly responded again, but still no keyboard so there goes the reset again...From that moment on I have not experienced a single freeze anymore. I rebooted after +30 minutes to see whether this was a lucky shot, but still...no freeze. I went for dinner then and the system was on +120 minutes+ screensaver. Still no freeze.

Then I decided to experimentally demonstrate the firepower of this fully armed and operational battlestation by doing everything the nerd in me would do: 

1) Install emacs by using synaptic: pass
2) Install 289 updates from the updatemanager: pass
3) reboot as suggested by update=> kernel running 2.6.32-24-generic #38: pass
4) install ati closed source drivers: pass
5) run firefox and reread 70+ pages of some thread: pass
6) click the mouse as fast as possible while bashing the keyboard with all sorts of keycombo' s: pass
7) plunge in a usb drive and play 5 divx movies at once using vlc while running firefox, a shell, emacs and downloading some 100 Mb mp3: pass


Up till now, no more freeezes after I took the mouse from the ps2 port and used it on a normal usb port.


system is up from +6 hours now and no freeze yet. I keep you informed, but everyone out there could perhaps try to use a usb mouse and see what it does. It appears to make the difference on my system so if you have a spare one somewhere, try it...who knows.


M.

### Post by lsaffre on 2010-07-27
Don't buy an USB mouse--at least not just to solve the freeze problem. This freezing is driving me crazy! It stopped to happen on my machine after a `dist-upgrade`, with or without usb mouse.

For the moment I'd bet that it's got to do with power management: I had no freezes since the dist-upgrade, as long as I turn all power-saving options off (select "Never") in `System -> Preferences -> Power Management`.

Luc

---

### Post by lsaffre on 2010-07-27
I can reproduce my freeze by using certain certain power management features (details see below). Once the freeze is there, it happens on each startup, shortly after the Ubuntu logo and the mouse cursor is displayed. I can stop this behaviour by entering the BIOS setup menu and choosing "Restore setup defaults". This is surprising because I don't ever do any manual changes in the BIOS. 

So my freeze seems to be related to some invisble BIOS setting. Or maybe these power management features write some buggy information into the BIOS.

Here are some more observations on my machine:

- When I shut down normally (using the Gnome menu command or using the ``halt`` command), everything is okay. But when I shut down by pressing the power button (and choosing "shutdown" in the menu), then the freeze starts.

- In `System -> Preferences -> Power Management`, the "When laptop lid is closed" option has 4 choices:

  - blank screen : works well.

  - suspend : after reopening the lid it asks for the password and continues to work, but the monitor remains dimmed. When I later shut down the machine, then it will freeze on each startup attempt until I do a BIOS reset.

  - hibernate : after reopening the lid and pushing the power button, it asks for the password and continues to work, but (same situation as after suspend).

  - shutdown : when i close the lid, it shuts down correctly... but then it will freeze on each startup attempt until I do a BIOS reset.

(I'm running latest Ubuntu on a HP Compaq Presario CQ61)

Luc

---

### Post by StuartN on 2010-07-28
> **firebreather said:**
> Recently, my update manager has said that I have a kernel image update, updating to the 2.6.32-24 one. Does anyone know if allowing that to update would start the crashes again?

I accepted that update and now I have kernel 2.6.32-24 in the Grub menu, with 2.6.34 being automatically retained as the default option. Booting into 2.6.32 results in freezes and booting into 2.6.34 is fine - accepting a new kernel should make absolutely no difference to your current installation, other than giving the option to test the latest kernel.

So yes, you should be able to safely test the new kernel and then choose which to continue using.

---

Two weeks ago I updated the O.S with the latest version of Ubuntu 10.04 (Lucid).
Now I have X11 freeze randomly (screen, mouse and keyboard).
It freezes with Firefox, thunderbird, Google Chrome, Eclipse, everything.
Before the upgrade it works everything.
When the desktop is frozen, I can connect remotely from another laptop (using SSH) and I can restart gdm:

**[root] stop gdm**
**[root] start gdm**

but the keyboard and the mouse are frozen.
I can just reboot.
[B][root] reboot

[/B]I removed compiz:
[B][root] dpkg -r compiz compiz-core compiz-gnome compiz-plugins libcompizconfig0 compiz-fusion-plugins-extra compiz-fusion-plugins-main compizconfig-backend-gconf
[/B]
I disabled Visual Effect.

I use this NVIDIA Driver:
**[root] dpkg -l | grep nvidia**
[I]ii  nvidia-173-modaliases                              173.14.22-0ubuntu11                                      Modaliases for the NVIDIA binary X.Org drive
ii  nvidia-96-modaliases                               96.43.17-0ubuntu1                                        Modaliases for the NVIDIA binary X.Org drive
ii  nvidia-common                                      0.2.23                                                   Find obsolete NVIDIA drivers
ii  nvidia-current                                     195.36.24-0ubuntu1~10.04                                 NVIDIA binary Xorg driver, kernel module and
ii  nvidia-current-modaliases                          195.36.24-0ubuntu1~10.04                                 Modaliases for the NVIDIA binary X.Org drive
ii  nvidia-settings                                    195.36.08-0ubuntu2                                       Tool of configuring the NVIDIA graphics driv

[/I]I tried also with the lastest version of the NVIDIA driver (NVIDIA-Linux-x86-256.35.run) but nothing is changed.

I tried to disable acpi:
**[root] vi /etc/default/grub **
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="splash acpi=off"
**[root] update-grub**

I tried also gconf-cleaner.

Any idea ?

thanks in advance
Marco

### Post by RavanH on 2010-07-30
> **xenosaga456 said:**
> so dose anyone know if there is a fix or a workaround for this yet?? if u know could u post it?

Well, there seem to be about as many different variations of "freezes" as there are posters on this thread but if you are having these:
[QUOTE=allroys]When I say freeze, I mean StuartN's definition. Random, frozen (not blank) screen, no mouse, no keyboard, power button reboot...[/QUOTE]
you might try adding **acpi_skip_timer_override** to the default boot options in GRUB. It has been suggested by Hqxriven in this thread and I have been using that for about a week now without any more freezes. 

But then, I've seen days in a row without problems before but always followed by days with constant system freezes / lock-ups so...

### Post by wrknight on 2010-07-31
Two observations on this problem:

1)  When installing lucid from the distribution iso file, both of my 64 bit systems would freeze within 5 to 60 seconds after logging on to GNOME.  However, in the GNOME fail safe mode, the system would run indefinitely.   At one point, while in the GNOME fail safe mode, I installed KDE and logged out of GNOME fail safe and into KDE.  In the KDE shell, my system would run indefinitely without freezing up.  However, I could not get compiz to run in the KDE shell.

2)  Last night, I tried upgrading my other 64 bit system to lucid from karmic using the update manager.  The first time I did this two months ago, I had the same problems described above.  This time, lucid is (correction) not running fine in the normal GNOME mode.  However, it takes longer for it to freeze than it did before.  It will run for about 3 or 4 minutes before freezing.  Compiz is working in GNOME (at least until the system freezes).  System runs fine in KDE shell, but Compiz isn't working in KDE.

### Post by wrknight on 2010-08-01
More observations on random freeze problem:

3)  Upgrading to linux kernel 2.6.34 didn't fix problem.  First time logged on, it ran for about 5 minutes.  Second time about 1 minute and third time it froze so soon that I couldn't even open a terminal.  

4)  In failsafe mode, 
    *$cat /proc/swaps*         came up empty (no swap file)
    *$top*                     showed: Swap:  0k total,   0k used,    0k free
    *$sudo swapon -a*          couldn't find device but gave correct uuid for sda2
    *$cat /etc/fstab*          showed uuid at /dev/sda2
after running
    *$sudo swapon /dev/sda2*     
    *$top*                     showed: Swap: 3984116k total, 0k used, 3984116k free 
    *$cat /proc/swaps*         showed swapfile at /dev/sda2
system will run for about 5 to 10 minutes before freezing repeatedly.

5)  Swap file gets deactivated each time the system is powered down and has to be reactivated in the failsafe mode.  Not sure why this is.

PS  System continues to run without freezing in KDE shell, but without Compiz

### Post by allroys on 2010-08-02
I went the entire weekend without a freeze. 

I started with no networking turned on. I read in some Launchpad post that someone was having this problem with PEAP security on Wifi, and with Realtek chipsets (which I will check for and avoid for the rest of my life...) So I used the Wifi at my house (no security) without problem, came to work this morning, and within 5 minutes of activating the Wifi, freeze. 

Spent an unhealthy amount of time trying to find out if there are any other drivers for this frickin chipset (and trying to install them) without much luck. 

Last resort: I went and got the 7-31-2010 build of the 2.6.35-rc6 kernel [here,/]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/daily/2010-07-31-maverick/") and lo and behold, wireless works better than ever, and no freeze (yet). 

I did a little research, and it looks like somebody actually wrote (and got included in the kernel) a reasonable driver for the rtl8187se chipset!  Holy crap! I never thought it would happen!

So this could be a fix for this killer freeze for some of you out there, or for those of you (like me) who have the dreaded Realtek 8187se chipset.

00:1f.0 ISA bridge: Intel Corporation 82801HB/HR (ICH8/R) LPC Interface Controller (rev 02)
00:1f.2 IDE interface: Intel Corporation 82801H (ICH8 Family) 4 port SATA IDE Controller (rev 02)

00:1f.5 IDE interface: Intel Corporation 82801H (ICH8 Family) 2 port SATA IDE Controller (rev 02)

03:00.0 IDE interface: Marvell Technology Group Ltd. 88SE6101 single-port PATA133 interface (rev b1)
07:03.0 FireWire (IEEE 1394): Texas Instruments TSB43AB22/A IEEE-1394a-2000 Controller (PHY/Link)

After talking to the Xorg mailing list I got this:

> 
You stopped the backtrace just before it got interesting.  All we see
there is the SIGIO signal handler being entered and calling into the
input driver, which then tries to eat an event from the kernel and has
no place to put it because the core server is stuck not processing input
events.

So whatever's going on in stack frame 8 is to blame.

In other words, it's very likely your driver messed your graphics device that got stuck somewhere while the input signal handler keeps queueing input events. So almost sure video driver issue.

Under hardware drivers I have: NVIDIA accelerated graphics driver (version current) [recommended].

2. Mouse - can move mouse cursor, but can not click on anything (cursor stuck with 'I' pointer).
3. Keyboard - nothing, not even numlock lights
4. This in the Xorg log file:

[mi] EQ overflowing. The server is probably stuck in an infinite loop.

Backtrace:
0: /usr/bin/X (xorg_backtrace+0x28) [0x4a3248]
1: /usr/bin/X (mieqEnqueue+0x1f4) [0x4a2ac4]
2: /usr/bin/X (xf86PostMotionEventP+0xc4) [0x47cea4]
3: /usr/lib/xorg/modules/input/evdev_drv.so (0x7f2332b91000+0x53cf) [0x7f2332b963cf]
4: /usr/bin/X (0x400000+0x6fca7) [0x46fca7]

Anyone have any ideas. I'm getting desperate. I've had to stop bragging about Ubuntu lately. Just keep quiet when the Windows guys walk by! Ouch

### Post by StuartN on 2010-08-04
> **mbraidy said:**
> I agree.  it took me 2 years to convince the board to move the 400+ computers to Linux Ubuntu.  In June they agreed and I was supposed to lead the project in September.  Now I have to eat humble pie :(
Shall we call it the WSOD?

Are all your 400+ computers identical hardware? If they are different and not all affected, then can you locate any hardware elements common to the affected machines, because this thread has had no success.

My personal theory, for what it's worth, is that a slow I/O response (longer than some kernel-defined threshold) is mis-reported as a storage hardware error, causing the filesystem to be remounted readonly, as per [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/515937](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/515937) - which results in wildly unpredictable symptoms according to whatever might be accessing I/O at the "failure" instant. Reducing I/O load consistently reduces (and for a lucky few, eliminates) the freezing.

A specific kernel level instance of this failure mode is:
[http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f](http://mirror.celinuxforum.org/gitstat//commit-detail.php?commit=6013efd8860bf15c1f86f365332642cfe557152f)
[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=14543)
[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16006)

For me, on my laptops, kernel 2.6.34 has completely eliminated the freezing - I do not think the cause has been removed, just that the conditions for freezing are no longer occurring on this particular hardware. I do not have the issue at all on any desktops.

### Post by mc4man on 2010-08-10
It would certainly appear there could be a multitude of reasons for this, probably hardware dependent.

I've had this start up on only my laptop, wireless with nvidia (restricted drivers.

Went today from maverick (freezes, w/ 256 & 195 drivers), back to lucid (freezes), to karmic (fine) and back to lucid.
There were some indications that the display drivers, network manager and or power management could be involved.

If you're running nvidia (installed thru 'hardware drivers'), after a freeze look for some of these keywords in /var/log/syslog and or /var/log/messages.
Or just grep for them - none of these should return anything on a 'good' install, if they do then possibly a fix and maybe not...
```

grep 'Dazed and confused' /var/log/syslog
```
```
grep 'NVRM: os_pci' /var/log/messages
```
```
grep 'Microcode SW error' /var/log/syslog
```

Edit: fix I was looking didn't work though took a day and a half before freeze - and it only did freeze just once.
(ironically while filing a bug on this 

(If on nvidia I'd run this anyway - have gotten no NVRM freezes since, just the one that caused this edit
sudo nvidia-xconfig

Am going to run w/ a boot parameter of noacpi and see what shakes out - or just return this laptop to karmic and not worry about...

### Post by kokonobs on 2010-08-11
I used to have the freezes as i described about 80 pages ago. Thing is when i used to monitor this topic i still got freezes every now and then. Funny enough, i gave up on getting a solution to it and i've since not had any freezes. Its been months now since it last froze.

I also stopped personal file sharing, bluetooth,print queue applet, ubuntu one and visual assistance from starting up automatically because i did not need them. ### Post by thecityofgold2006 on 2010-08-12
I first posted on page 15 of this thread and have since persevered with a system that would crash randomly at least twice a day.

I stuck with it out of loyalty to the Ubuntu project..

Well, after 3 hard months I installed PCLinuxOS yesterday and have had no problems since. Set-up is very easy and if you install the Gnome version it all works like Ubuntu.

Both this thread and the Launchpad bug are difficult to follow because there are reports of bugs other than the original 'complete freeze, black screen, restart'.

But there most certainly is a core of people that have the original problem and there appears no inclination among Ubuntu developers to put effort into solving the problem. The Launchpad bug has still not even had an importance assigned to it, 3 months later! IT'S CRITICALLY IMPORTANT FFS!

Users need a stable system. This must take priority over everything else. Simply sticking your head in the sand and ploughing on hoping problems will fix themselves will not work. It is clear that Ubuntu has a major bug (since 10.04) that makes the operating system completely unreliable and impossible to recommend to anyone (they may hit a random freeze and lose important data).

I suggest to those in charge at Ubuntu HQ that a period of reflection and consolidation is needed or there will be many more longstanding Ubuntu users abandoning ship.

Users filing reports and posting here are mostly 'normal' users. I count myself in that category. Ubuntu has been promoted as 'linux for humans'. etc. Such users don't know how to differentiate between different bugs and shouldn't have to. 

If resources were allocated to fixing the problem I am sure it would take a minimal amount of time to sort the bugs into different categories.

The biggest problem I have with the situation is the lack of any official acknowledgment or communication. Development for 10.10 goes ahead full steam with not even a thought for the ominous bug lurking in the code somewhere. It's a recipe for disaster.

### Post by StewartM on 2010-08-15
> Both this thread and the Launchpad bug are difficult to follow because there are reports of bugs other than the original 'complete freeze, black screen, restart'.

That wasn't the original post. But I think your post is relevant. More later.

But now you mention it, I had a very similar problem on my ZaReason desktop when fiddling around with 10.04. When I was running the 2.6.32-22 kernel, running the version 173 NVIDIA driver for my GeForce 8600 card, I could *ALWAYS* get a black screen when leaving one user logged in, switching to another, then BAM!--black screen when user 2 logged out. That was fixed by going to a new NVIDIA driver and the 2.6.32-24 kernel (though that broke Firefox user profiles sitting inside encrypted EncFS space or encrypted container files).

> But there most certainly is a core of people that have the original problem and there appears no inclination among Ubuntu developers to put effort into solving the problem. The Launchpad bug has still not even had an importance assigned to it, 3 months later! IT'S CRITICALLY IMPORTANT FFS!

Users need a stable system. This must take priority over everything else. Simply sticking your head in the sand and ploughing on hoping problems will fix themselves will not work. It is clear that Ubuntu has a major bug (since 10.04) that makes the operating system completely unreliable and impossible to recommend to anyone (they may hit a random freeze and lose important data).

I believe that all these myriad problems *are* probably related--they're the result of video chip or card driver problems and X. I had a friend who had the freezes as soon after logging into his desktop--his fix was to simply buy a $29 video card and that solved the problem. My experience also points to a video card/kernel problem. 

And yes, the devs do know about this, and yes, they're working hard to fix it. I have posted several times in this thread about video chip driver updates being issued and AFAIK nobody's gone out and installed them. At least nobody's mentioned my posts. 

However, I do agree with you on the need for stable releases. In particular, I believe that Team Ubuntu is being a bit overambitious in its release cycle, with too-many new features being incorporated into each long-term releases. I think the LTRs should be opportunities for a pause in the release cycle--that each LTR should look very much like its predecessor released 6 months before (i.e., 10.04 should have looked pretty much like 9.10). The goal of each LTR should be a) to fix outstanding hardware issues and longstanding bugs, and b) to create as seamless and easy an upgrade path as possible for people making the jump from the older LTR to the new one. 

Those goals would be a big enough task by itself without complicating it for the need for new features. Save the new eye candy and new features for the short-term releases.

StewartM

---

### Post by mymythtv on 2010-08-16
Hi

Have tried to follow this thread, but it's difficult - a lot of different errors..

In my case, the history is:
Running Mythbuntu since 7.10 - upgraded along the road ( also hardware ).
9.10 was/is stable, but I decided to upgrade around end may to 10.04 ( Myth 0.23 )

During summer I got more and more "lookups" - video playback froze/became sluggish and I had to reboot to gain control again.
Sometime it was stable for a longer period, but for the rest of the time it froze during playback..
If I didn't start playback, Myth was responding ok - freezing started as soon as I started playback

Still able to login with ssh, so I thought it was Nvidia driver, bad graphics or even overheating. Changed Graphics ( had a spare ) - same result.
Upgraded driver to 256.44 - same result

Last week I downgraded kernel from 2.6.32-24 to 2.6.31-21 ( still on server ) and Mythbuntu is just as stable as 9.10.. :D

Seems it IS the kernel making trouble, so I will test my laptop which had some problems to - total freeze; power off/on :(

### Post by oldefoxx on 2010-08-16
My relationship to Linux, Ubuntu, and these forums is in flux at the moment,  I concur with the feeling that new releases of Ubuntu and updates have lost stability since 8.10 or 9.04 came out.  0.10 and 10.04 were particularly bad on initial release, and I even traced some threads that explained what others thought had gone wrong at that point.  I am afraid that I seen no return to the stately pace used previously, and I might even guess  that the increaded lure of Windows7 has motivated a rush effort to get Ubuntu along as far as possible as quickly as possible, even though not everything has been tested adequately.

 But let's say that this is all true, or suspect enough. how to compensate?  You can always reinstall any version of Ubuntu, but then you have a complication:  Either keep /home and user accounts, or do a reformat and go from scratch.  The most direct way of managing this is selecting the last option, Manual Mode,
when the installer's partitoner asks.

Going from scratch, nothing really changes as to the process, butx you lose what you have.  To save what you have, the /home must be available, yet disabled to some degree.  The degree can vary.  Use a new userid and password, and the old account is left alone.  But use the same userid and password, and the old qnd new aqccounts effectively are merged.  NOw this is the risk area.

Each user account also holds data specific to that user's configuration settings.  If these are maintained yet set improperly causing a failure to log in, the same settings will mess with efforts to log in after the install.  Not good.

Preventing this is not entirely easy.  These folders and files for each user must still be under /home or get wiped. but if you break this direct line up

/home/userid

into something like this instesd

home/old/userid

Then the installer will create a new setup with

/home/userid

and after the install you will find that you have this

/home/old/userid
/home/userid

The idea then is go go through /home/old/userodd and its branches of folders and files and move the ones to te kept over to /home/uerid.  When done. just do a rm -R /hone/old and all the old account just go away.

Now here is the hard part.  You must avoid moving any files that have the old settings over as well, because these overwrite the new ones and you may be back in as bad a shape as before.

Knowing which ones to avoid is not always easy, but there is q qir4 chance that uses the fact that each one that starts off with a period in front of the name means that it is hidden.  If you can avoid moving hidden files, that might work, but it is sort of hare to avoid moving hindden folders or files/  You cold of course use something like rm -R /home/old/.??* which does not get confused by a period (.) orf its parent, represented by two periods (..).  That might make it save txo just move what's in home/old back to /home, but this has not been tried yet, as path statements involving rm,cp, and mv can be a bit tricky to get exacty right.
You could always just copy or move specific folders or files from the old tree to the new one, and that might be all you want.

There is another way that I have thought of, which is to first copy the new folders and files over the old, then everything gets replaced by the new verson, except for the folders and files that belong to that user.  Once that is done, moving the /home/old back over /home would bring the new+old combination back home.

I know this seems like an incredible amount of effort with little reward at the end, but if it is your data that is at risk of being lost, you might just be glad for the chance to try.

All this involvement and efforts to work past such problems have fnally shown me something worth noting:  In many areas, Linux's cntinued loyalty to the past. meaning to principles first laid down with Unix, some as much as 30 years ago, are really far too technical and exacting for even a moderately informed PC user.
I mean GUIs do help, but if you cannot do it all through a GUI,
and the decision was made that you are not allowed to, then you have no prospects of entering the average household unless the is alinux guru or devotee on the premise, and then only if he insists and does all the work.
                           
Which party is Ubuntu going for:  The household or the business premise?  There is a tremendous difference when it comes to needel levels of security and where it applies.  For the household, it is protection from the outside, but ease of use inside.   For businesses, it is security qll the way around, and use tech support if problems crop up.  Tech support may be the guy just down the hall, or maybe that is just something else you do, but don't expect everyone else to be up to your level.

I have the feeling that Linux is too locked into the past, and Ubuntu is misdirected when it comes to its present emphasis.  You want to disagree, that is certainly your fight and privelege.

---

### Post by wrknight on 2010-08-18
Like everyone else, I'm still looking for a solution.  Two weeks ago, I reported changing over to KDE on my 64 bit desktop.  It has been running continuously since that time (only a Dominion Power failure that lasted an hour took it out).

I now have two intel core 2 duo machines, two intel P4 machines and one intel atom notebook all running Ubuntu.  

Both P4 machines run Ubuntu 10.04 with GNOME and run continuously with no problems.  Both of these machines also ran all earlier versions of Ubuntu GNOME with no problems.

Both intel core 2 duo (64 bit) machines freeze when running ubuntu 10.04 with GNOME, usually within 10 minutes of logging in.  (When I say freeze, I mean everything simply stops - no mouse, no keyboard no control of any kind.  The display simply stops and resembles a screensave snapshot.)  Now both of these machines run fine with Ubuntu 10.04 KDE.  These are not identical systems.  They have different motherboards, different chipsets, different graphics processors and different network cards, but both behave identically.  It makes no difference if I use PS2 mouse/keyboard, usb mouse/keyboard or wireless mouse/keyboard. It also makes no difference if I connect to the network with a wireless card or with straight ethernet cable.  Before installing Ubuntu 10.04, both machines worked fine under earlier versions of Ubuntu GNOME.

The intel atom notebook has not had a single problem since I installed the notebook version of Ubuntu 10.04 GNOME about two weeks ago.

Given the myriad of problems reported in this thread, it's impossible to say for sure that there's any one cause to this problem.  But given my own experience, I would have to start looking at GNOME to find the problem(s).  

I just hope the developers are paying attention.  I won't be using GNOME on any 64 bit computer until I hear that the problem is fixed for good.

Tried 10.04 LTS both 32 and 64.

In my pc, the keyboard "and" mouse (both at same time)freezes usually within 5-10 minutes of logging in, the system don't freeze or stop at all, just keyboard and mouse, by example: hiting the power button (the physical one, in the pc case)start the auto shutdown.

I don't think hardware is the problem, because I have an Archlinux in the same box running for days whitout problem.

Sorry for my bad english.

Salu2.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2010-08-19
FYI...this might help.  I have been using PCLOS/Xfce ever since I had freezing problems with 10.04 Gnome without any problem.  Last night, out of curiosity, I ran PCLOS/Gnome on my computer and during booting I noticed some error messages that X...something...17 was overloaded (This happened before the login screen, but it flashed too fast for me to read).  The computer locked up within 10 min of running PCLOS/Gnome.  I switched back to PCLOS/Xfce and the computer ran just fine.  Any idea?  I'm beginning to think that to run Gnome successfully, the graphic card must have a lot of memory.

---

### Post by sambutoki on 2010-08-19
> **komitaltrade said:**
> I have cyber with 12 clients (new intel D410PT) and one server (intel D510MO). All machines are freezing and it is no rules. Customers are on the edge of the patient. 

Post is too long and I didn't saw any conclusion/solution. Is it some solution, or I should to switch to Mandriva/SuSe (I don't want, but it can be complete business disaster for me with nice amount of investment)?

You don't need to switch to Mandriva or Suse, Debian Stable is totally unaffected by this, and so far I've found nothing that can be done in Ubuntu that can't be done in Debian.  It would be a much easier transition for both yourself and your customers as Debian and Ubuntu both operate in a very similar fashion (remember Ubuntu is based on Debian) but Debian is far more stable. If you want to chat about this more you can contact me directly.

---

### Post by x86scorpion on 2010-08-20
Yesterday, I decided to install the first maintenance release of Ubuntu's 10.04 LTS..

Ubuntu 10.04.1 LTS
[http://nl.releases.ubuntu.com/releases/10.04/ubuntu-10.04.1-desktop-i386.iso](http://nl.releases.ubuntu.com/releases/10.04/ubuntu-10.04.1-desktop-i386.iso)


..but this time, all my partitions to my ol' friend "ext3". It simply amazed me, as I had no single hick ups or whatever compared to my previous install.

The sad part :mad:, in my case, is that I wasn't able to determine the real cause of the /freeze.


So far so good, anyway, with my new install. :wink:

### Post by ckonstantinos on 2010-08-20
This is very serious bug.

I've been using Ubuntu LTS for years with only minor problems and for real work not distro hoping. This is the first time (on the same computer) I have to abandon Ubuntu because the crashes won't stop (Ubuntu 10.04).

I have done all the updates, tried kernel 2.6.32.21, 2.6.32.22, 2.6.32.23, 2.6.32.24 no luck, all freeze.
At the begining I thought it was the X.org freezing, but now I think it is the whole system. Today I was typing a text and listening to music and only after 5 minutes it froze again. I was listening to an mp3 song with xine at the same time and the last couple of seconds kept repeating after the freeze...

This bug has lasted very long and I can't work like this any more...

### Post by Nonsense on 2010-08-21
> **cottfcfan said:**
> 
The problem now is that the answers for some may well be in this thread, but its that long that no one will have the time to trawl through it.

I find it incredible that Canonical or anyone else isn't taking this seriously (this post is number 890 on the topic!).
I've had the freeze problem since I first upgraded from 9.10 to 10.04 in May. Yesterday I installed a fresh 10.04.1, but the problem remains. I've also tried using the 2.6.34 kernel, but no change.

I've spoken to several users about this and most feel that unless this is fixed they can't continue to promote Linux since this seems to effect most common Gnome/KDE distros. I am myself pondering if Mac is the way to go, though I really want to stick with Linux.

### Post by anselm on 2010-08-21

### Post by Strongmind on 2010-08-21
I've done a lot of tests myself about all of this. Maybe my results will be the same for some of you. First off, my machine was crashing in Ubuntu and Windows. Memtest86 said that my memory was faulty, so i reseated it. This stopped Windows from crashing. Next, I rolled back from kernel 2.6.32-24 to 2.6.32-23. Still crashing. Finally, I rolled back from Nvidia 256.35 to 195.36. The crashes are now completely gone. I also tried using the -24 kernel with the 195 driver, and there were still crashes.

I'm not sure what the ultimate culprit was, because i had to change half of my system. I'm leaning more towards the kernel though, because it is the only cross-distro software I'm using. Other people have mentioned freezes in PCLinuxOS and Suse. The only similarity between the systems is the kernel.

I hope this information was useful to all of you.

---

### Post by billharris on 2010-08-21
> **Nonsense said:**
> I find it incredible that Canonical or anyone else isn't taking this seriously (this post is number 890 on the topic!).
I've had the freeze problem since I first upgraded from 9.10 to 10.04 in May. Yesterday I installed a fresh 10.04.1, but the problem remains. I've also tried using the 2.6.34 kernel, but no change.

It's very easy to get frustrated here, but remember that open source software is largely done as a volunteer effort.  We're part of that effort, and submitting clear bug reports is a big deal.  Yes, I've wished that Mark Shuttleworth would wave his magic wand, but it's likely more helpful to use this forum to figure out how to submit clear and useful bug reports to Launchpad.

> **Nonsense said:**
>  I've spoken to several users about this and most feel that unless this is fixed they can't continue to promote Linux since this seems to effect most common Gnome/KDE distros. I am myself pondering if Mac is the way to go, though I really want to stick with Linux.

I can't suggest the right way for you to go.  I moved to Debian stable with Gnome the first weekend in July, and I've only had one crash since then, when I ran afoul of a known and reported XORG bug.  That was before I installed the nvidia drivers; I don't remember if that was a factor.  

I realize everything may change tomorrow, and YMMV still applies, but I'm pretty happy so far.  Just a note: Debian stable gets that moniker because it's well tested, which means you don't often get bleeding edge versions of packages.  There are backports, but I presume the more you blend stable with testing or sid, you run a higher risk of problems.

---

### Post by thecityofgold2006 on 2010-08-22
> **billharris said:**
> It's very easy to get frustrated here, but remember that open source software is largely done as a volunteer effort.  We're part of that effort, and submitting clear bug reports is a big deal.  Yes, I've wished that Mark Shuttleworth would wave his magic wand, but it's likely more helpful to use this forum to figure out how to submit clear and useful bug reports to Launchpad.



This is the relevant bug, submitted 26th May: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765) 

Three months later and the importance is 'undecided'!!!! :lolflag:

I take on board about this being open source software but Ubuntu has always promoted itself as Linux for the masses. It is meant to 'just work'.

The lack of acknowledgement or effort to fix this critical bug has disappointed me and makes it impossible to have faith in Ubuntu as a mainstream OS. Priorities need to be examined with stability made paramount.

I switched to PCLinusOS a few weeks ago and get no crashes. Windows XP also doesn't crash.

### Post by NoVista on 2010-08-22
Since the next version of Ubuntu is now down to 2 months away, I presume the rest of you are like me and waiting for the day to see if it solves the 'Glib from hell warning'.

In my case, if it does not, I will be leaving Ubuntu and head for Debian stable or PCLinuxOS, or gosh forbid some version of Windows.

4 months in a row of Glib warning and crashing every day.
I used to never reboot unless needed.
This current OS can freeze all on it's own overnight if left on.
5 crashes a day, or more, for the last 4 months on my main setup.
At least my laptop has never been affected by this.

 All I have done for the last few years is tell everyone to ditch whatever they have and run Ubuntu.
Sad state of affairs though now regarding Ubuntu's reputation, and as it turns out, the way my 'puter friends see it, mine too.

### Post by lgately on 2010-08-22
I upgraded on 21Aug2010 from 8.04 lts to 10.04 lts and encountered same problem with mouse and keyboard. Upgrade goes smoothly and can login but after a few minutes the mouse and keyboard become unresponsive. I tried both an usb and ps2 mouse. Keyboard is ps2. The system is still running. I can connect to the machine from an other on the network and transfer files. Hard reboot is only way out. Machine comes back up but after a short period mouse and keyboard again unresponsive. This is the same machine that had run 8.04 LTS rock solid for two years. 

There are some post at ubuntu regarding this problem going back a couple of years. There have been solutions proposed of altering the xorg config file and deleting packages and going back to older packages. I really don't have the time or proficiency to debug software. Unfortunately my solution was to reinstall 8.04LTS. I hope it is supported long enough for the bug to be found


Running a plain box with intel E5300 cpu and intel chipset. DMESG out put for machine running 8.04. Hope large output is okay on board.

### Post by RavanH on 2010-08-22
> **Hqxriven said:**
> Try acpi_skip_timer_override in the line of kernel...

Happy to report this seems to have fixed it for me -- or else it has been a recent kernel update -- because I have not have any freezes since three weeks now :)

**Quick how-to** for anyone that has no idea what this means :

1. Using an account in the sudoers list (usually your normal account created at install) hit Alt+F2 to open a terminal and enter ```
gksudo gedit /etc/default/grub
```
2. After your password, gedit opens. Find the line that starts with **GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=** and put **acpi_skip_timer_override** in there. It will probably be something like ```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_skip_timer_override"
```
3. Save and close gedit.
4. Enter in terminal ```
sudo update-grub
```
5. Reboot :)

Hope this helps at least SOME with this prevailing issue...

---

### Post by yahou on 2010-08-23
Hello, dear Ubuntu Forums!
The crashes and other stability problems on 10.04 have now stopped completely on my machine. I've gone through many stages since upgrading to this version of Ubuntu.

I'm running a Toshiba Satellite A-50 laptop with Intel 8xx chipset. Soon after upgrading to Lucid Lynx through Synaptic I had the issue of xorg not working properly. I've had experience with every such upgrade causing problems, so decided to simply make a clean install. That helped for a short time.
Next, it started freezing for no apparent reason, with mouse and keyboard not responding (apart from the alt+sysreq+REISUB), screen frozen. If a video or song (whatever, from rhythmbox or youtube) was being played at the time, the sound would usually keep playing after the freeze. I tried the workarounds from this guide: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes) . Nothing seemed to help for longer than half a day. Apparently the bug was sneaking on me, waiting for me to feel safe and then... STOP.
Then, still without any apparent direct reason, freezes became more frequent and started interweaving with another kind, the hard freezes, with nothing responding and everything completely stuck. Only hard shutdown by holding down the power button.
All this time I tried to keep up with all updates in Synaptic, but nothing seemed to change the situation directly. As always, whenever I thought they were cured, the freezes would come back, just like that.
I also tried going back to some older kernel, to no avail.
After all that, some of the updates, about 2 weeks ago, DID help. No freezes after that, but still issues with the graphics (starting in low-graphics mode every time, no hardware acceleration). BUT, yesterday I installed another batch of updates from Synaptic. Now I have a fully functioning, freeze-free system! I'm back home!

I hope this helps anyone, if only to retain hope :p.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2010-08-23
I have been playing with different DEs on PCLOS these past few weeks.  PCLOS/Xfce has been very stable on my computer.  However, adding Gnome, E17, and KDE had caused the freezing problems to appear on my computer.  The problem is really bad with Gnome and less with KDE.  Once I completely removed all DEs except for Xfce I observed no more problem. I then decided to give Xbuntu 10.04 a try.  Well, with kernel 2.6.31 Xbuntu is very stable.  Any kernel after that simply does not work.  We need a good statistician to perform a multivariate analysis to figure out this problem.  Any one out there?

### Post by Nonsense on 2010-08-23
> **RavanH said:**
> Happy to report this seems to have fixed it for me -- or else it has been a recent kernel update -- because I have not have any freezes since three weeks now :)

**Quick how-to** for anyone that has no idea what this means :

1. Using an account in the sudoers list (usually your normal account created at install) hit Alt+F2 to open a terminal and enter ```
gksudo gedit /etc/default/grub
```
2. After your password, gedit opens. Find the line that starts with **GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=** and put **acpi_skip_timer_override** in there. It will probably be something like ```
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash acpi_skip_timer_override"
```
3. Save and close gedit.
4. Enter in terminal ```
sudo update-grub
```
5. Reboot :)

Hope this helps at least SOME with this prevailing issue...



Tried it, but alas, it didn't work.
So far I've tested the following:

- No Nvidia driver
- No Compiz
- Update Kernel to 2.6.34
- Downgrade Kernel to 2.6.31.13
- Uninstall Ubuntu One
- Use Opera or Google Chrome instead of Firefox
- Don't use flash
- no acpi and acpi_skip_timer_override on boot


(This thing have gone so far that when I'm at another "freeze-free" computer I still wait for the moment when i freezes. It's really stressing me out all this since I use my Ubuntu-installation for work)

I remember having issues with the keyboard and mouse not working properly when I was using Dapper (I think), but that didn't make the OS freeze. I seem to recall that the problem was brought on by the an unsupported chipset on my motherboard. Could it be a problem with my motherboard? Anyone else with an ASUS P5B?

EDIT:
My MB is using the Intel P965 chipset, I found no fixes for that particular chipset, but quite a few others:
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes)
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/IntelDriverXFreeze](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/IntelDriverXFreeze)

Don't know if this is related.

I found this though, which may be worth a try:
"In Lucid, apport will automatically detect and report freeze bugs and take care of collecting a batchbuffer dump and other assorted information that upstream needs. By default Apport is disabled in stable Ubuntu releases so it has to be activated temporarily: 

sudo service apport start force_start=1

Apport may not be able to capture a dump on every freeze, so try reproducing the freeze a few times and see if apport will collect it.

For kernels 2.6.32 (Lucid) and 2.6.33, the GPU resets itself after a hang and therefore a batchbuffer dump is not useful since it only captures the reset state. The drm-intel-next mainline kernel builds beginning with 2010-02-24 available here have a kernel patch which will automatically record the failed batchbuffer information in /sys/kernel/debug/dri/0/i915_error_state when the GPU resets. Install and boot with one of these kernels, reproduce the error, and attach a copy of the /sys/kernel/debug/dri/0/i915_error_state file to the bug report. In order to have consistent logs, we would also like attached /var/log/Xorg.0.log and the output of dmesg from the same time. We hope to capture this information automatically soon."
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Troubleshooting/Freeze)

I guess this technique could be used with just about any MB?
The latest kernel is for the moment 2.6.34.997
I installed that kernel, but I haven't activated Apport or looked for the error_state-file

### Post by treddy on 2010-08-27
**Make "Secure Graphics Mode" Permanent **

My family has 4 machines that use Windows and Ubuntu 10.4 in double boot installations: a recent Vaio laptop on Win 7, an Asus eee Netbook on XP, a Compaq Presario lap on XP and a home-brewed desktop with an Asus motherboard and ATI vid card on XP. With the exception of the desktop, Lucid as not given a moment's trouble or lock-up on these computers, since the first beta.

However, after upgrading to Lucid, my double-boot desktop has had endless freeze-ups, which never happened before 10.4. The problem seems to be linked to the ATI Radeon X1500 series video card (now legacy in AMD)and I have finally found that the only "permanent" solution is to boot up in recovery mode and select "Start in Secure Graphics Mode". At that point the machine becomes perfectly stable, and the graphics are acceptable. I have tried various video start-up configurations that were suggested in the 90 odd pages of this thread, but none were as successfull as "Secure Graphics Mode"

How can I configure GRUB so that "Start in Secure Graphics Mode" becomes permanent, and I do not need to go through Recovery Mode?

(Linux kernel 2.6.32-24-Generic, Athlon 64 bit Dual core- Radeon x1500 series Video card, Asus M2N32-SLI Motherboard, NVJDIAnFORCE590 SLI chipset, 2 gb Ram)

Can someone please explain how to achieve this modification, presumably to GRUB - I would be very grateful.

Another factor that came to light starting in Recovery Mode is that freeze-ups seem to produce a lot of broken packages; now I frequently run "Repair Broken Packages" during the start-up and it usualy finds something to repair after a freeze-up. Seems to improve stability.

I am not a newbie (rather an oldbie with limited computational ability) and I would be glad to give any feedback on my 4 machines if this is of interest to anybody.

Cheers

---

### Post by hoover67 on 2010-08-28
Hello Treddy, 

there should be a grub menu entry for the "safe graphics mode", you need to count down from the top of the grub menu, starting at zero for all the bootable entries. Once you've found the "secure graphics mode" entry, you can enter that number in 

/boot/grub/menu.lst

in the 

default = 0 

line, replace 0 (zero) with the entry you wish to boot by default. 

HTH Uwe

---

I really don't understand how the stability of a distribution like Ubuntu doesn't improve over time. Do they basically start from scratch with every new release? It certainly seems to me to be the case. With every release a couple of bugs disappeared but a couple of new bugs appeared.

### Post by trolleybus on 2010-08-30
Let me tell you my story.
I have Dell Vostro 1310 and also suffered from random freezes. In the beginning there were many random and sudden logouts. The system simply closed session and i had to log back in. Maybe it is Nvidia driver bug but never used nv or nouveau because these didn't satisfy me with reasons. One day i figured out that renderaccel can cause these random logouts and after disabling it logouts just stopped. But freezes didn't. These occurred even with 2.6.34 mainline kernel which is recommended here by so many users. I've done some reinstalls without luck. During last reinstall i replaced ext4 filesystem with ext3 and now with renderaccel enabled there hasn't been any random logout around for several weeks. Anyway, total freezes still occurred. In this thread several pages ago someone suggested that booting into console and starting session with 'startx' would end freezes. After trying that method no freezes have occurred around for several days. Maybe too early to say if this really helps. But i cross my fingers.
I currently use nvidia driver version 195.36.24 (256.40 made hangs worse) and kernel 2.6.32-24.

### Post by shakethenation on 2010-08-30
I was experiencing this and i run ubuntu 10.04 LTS on an ASUS 64 bit laptop with a ATI Mobility Radeon HD 5870 Graphics Chip.  Caused windows to freeze up at start up too.  Finally found it is major issues with the compatibility of the ATI display drivers and misconfigurations between xorg and fglrx.

These are the steps you take to solve this issue: 
1.) Check proper installation of kernal headers with

    ```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-$(uname -r)
```2.)Install the fglrx driver

    ```
sudo apt-get install fglrx
```3.)Select fglrx to use, selection for me was 0

    ```
sudo update-alternatives --config gl_conf
```4.)Then:

    ```
sudo ldconfig
```    ```
sudo update-initramfs -u
```config xorg

    ```
sudo aticonfig --initial
```And that fixed it for me. I'm starting a new topic for this too as it took me several hours to get it fixed and this thread is too broad to be useful for finding a solution.

---

### Post by Derek S on 2010-08-31
I seem to have fixed my random freeze issue (Fingers crossed)

The fix for me was to start NVIDIA X Server settings, under X Server XVideo Settings disable Sync to VBlank, under OpenGL Settings disable Sync to VBlank and disable the Allow Flipping option.

My machine has been running under load / normal usage for over a day now without a crash.  Before this I would certainly crash before 20 mins.

System:
Kubuntu 10.04 amd64   (Gnome never crashed for me)
KDE 4.5 upgraded from ppa
Nvidia drivers Ubuntu provided NVidia-current (195.36.24)
Video card NVidia GT240, HDMI output
AMD Athlon II 250
Gigabyte motherboard

### Post by cd-r80 on 2010-09-01
That disable ACPI in grub has helped me. I hope so...

..then I disable ACPI in GRUB configuration

   1. Open the terminal: Applications > Accessories > Terminal
   2. Type in: sudo gedit /etc/defaut/grub
   3. Enter password if required. DO NOT CLOSE THE TERMINAL YET
   4. In the file, look for.. GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"
   5. Add in: "noacpi noapm"
   6. Mine looks like this: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash radeon.agpmode=1 noacpi noapm"
   7. note: radeon.agpmode = 1, thats the GFX mode/tweak for my PC
   8. note: noapm = apm = Advance Power Manager
   9. Safe the file.
  10. Go back to the terminal, type in: sudo update-grub
  11. This process will update the (as I can say) system GRUB. After all done, you can close the terminal.. and restart.

### Post by greylander on 2010-09-05
Hello Everyone,

I may have found the solution for random freezes with Nvidia graphics card.  Upgrade you kernel -- easier than you think.

Look here:  [http://my.opera.com/linuxonlinehelp/blog/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx-amd64-nvidia-195-current-freeze-hang-randomly](http://my.opera.com/linuxonlinehelp/blog/ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx-amd64-nvidia-195-current-freeze-hang-randomly)

I'm copying the instructions below, along with my own notes:
[INDENT]
Solution:
*(EDIT: these instructions seem to assume that you have removed/disabled the Nvidia-current driver (system->administration->Hardware Drivers.  I suggest you 'remove' the driver and reboot, then follow these instructions)*
- go [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/v2.6.34-lucid/)
- download kernel images for your System and headers into a folder named kernel  *(EDIT:  you will need i386 or amd64 header *and* the "all" header files, and of course you will need the i386 or amd64 kernel image file -- so a total of three files.. your browser may want to install each file as you download, but to avoid error messages about dependencies, just save the files and follow the remaining instructions).*
- go into folder kernel with #cd kernel
- install pakets with #sudo dpkg -i *.deb
- reboot
- if Xserver fails select "start Xserver with low resolution"
*    (EDIT: if you already removed Nvidia driver and rebooted, then you should be fine here)*
- go Systemsettings/Hardware Drivers
- select Nvidia-Current install again
- reboot and test the System [/INDENT]

I have not frozen yet... but it has only been about 12 hours... I was averaging about one freeze per day, so if I go several days with no crash, I will be confident the problem is truly solved.  I will post here again to let y'all know.

Prior to doing this 'fix', I notice quite a few graphics abnormalities, including quirky, glitchy graphics behavior during bootup, during shutdown, and during some video playback on websites (flash, etc.).  After this 'fix', the graphics during boot and shutdown all look clean, and for lack of a better way of putting it, the graphics feel cleaner in general -- maybe I am just imagining it, but it seems like basic window opening/closing and other graphic transitions are crisper/cleaner somehow.

So if you have nvidia graphics and you are experience random freezes, I would recommend trying this.  Like I say, I'll report back here if/when I've gone several days with no freeze.

---

### Post by greylander on 2010-09-05
I am pretty sure this is a bug in the Linux kernel.  See my post #964 or go here:

    [http://my.opera.com/linuxonlinehelp/...-hang-randomly](http://my.opera.com/linuxonlinehelp/...-hang-randomly)


I think you need to keep the nvidia "version current" driver and just get a newer version of the kernel with the bug fixed.  Upgrading the kernel is easy --just like installing any .deb packages, then reboot.

So far, about 24 hours and no freeze.  I won't be completely sure until 3-4 days have passed, but getting more confident by the hour.

### Post by greylander on 2010-09-05
Just wanted to make another comment about this "freeze" problem with Nvidia.

Up until yesterday, I thought it was "harmless".  Just reset or powercycle and reboot, and nothing damaged.  

However, after it froze yesterday, after reboot, my ALSA (sound) drivers were corrupted somehow.  There may be something particular about the ASLA sound system that is affected by this bug, but this may be an indication that the freeze can cause file corruption in general.

My point is that you may want to make fixing this a high priority rather than "put up with it".  Hopefully the fix I suggested above (kernel upgrade) does the trick.

### Post by bilodeau on 2010-09-06
Howdy All!

My two cents on this:

I followed the instructions at:

[HTML]http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1478787&highlight=system+freeze&page=91[/HTML]

and added the 'acpi_skip_timer_override' to my kernel boot options.  This has stopped the weird system halts for me.

On my system, the halts happened at random and resulted in my monitor going into power save mode with no keyboard or mouse input having any effect.  I had to manually reset the system to get things back in working order.

Adding the 'acpi_skip_timer_override' to the file at /etc/default/grub has _completely_ eliminated this for me.  I didn't have to remove _any_ software.  I have been running for two weeks with the modified kernel boot parameters without any problems.

For those who may find such information useful, I am running:

64-bit kernel: 2.6.32-24-generic #42-Ubuntu SMP
CPU: AMD Athlon x4 630
Motherboard: ASUS M4A785TD-M EVO

If you're having weird system halting problems, I suggest you try the solution posted in the link above.  It may resolve your situation.

Cheers

### Post by Andrew.Stevenson on 2010-09-08
Hello all,

re-posting a summary of my struggles with this bug.

system:

        - asus p7h55d motherboard and intel core i7
        - USB mouse & PS/2 keyboard
        - debian squeeze 32bit

problems:

        - random freezes can happen any time (computer idle or under workload), and many times a day
        - *only* the PS/2 keyboard freezes (USB mouse never freezes). a wild guess is that using USB mouse & USB keyboard would fix the freezes, but since i want to keep using my old ps2 keyboard it's not a solution for me :)
        - when a freeze occurs, it sometimes happens that the last key that was pressed will keep repeating on shell input.
        - after a freeze, system still works normally, it's therefore possible to ssh to it without problems. besides, even though the keyboard is frozen, "Alt+SysREQ + R-E-I-S-U-B" does work.

system usage:

        - freezes happens with Xorg (while doing firefox browsing and using only a light window manager)
        - freezes happens with framebuffer only (even happened when installing debian in console mode interface)


kernels tested:

- 2.6.26 (never had a freeze)         
- 2.6.31 (if i remember correctly, it was working properly with this release)
        - 2.6.32 (freeze)
        - 2.6.33 (freeze)
        - 2.6.34.1 (freeze)
        - 2.6.35.3 (freeze)

options tested that did not work:

        - disabling Plug&Play OS in bios
        - tested with KMS enabled or disabled (using normal vesa framebuffer)
        - "i8042.nopnp" "i8042.reset" "i8042.nomux" on grub kernel command line
        - "noacpi" and "noapm" on grub kernel command line
        - "nohz=off" on grub kernel command line
        - "noacpi" and "nolapic" on grub kernel command line
        - "acpi_skip_timer_override" on grub kernel command line
        - combination of all kernel command line options above

remedies that works after a freeze:

        - "Alt+SysREQ + R-E-I-S-U-B" to reboot the system safely.
        - "rmmod psmouse ; modprobe psmouse" will unfreeze the keyboard, and system will work normally until next freeze.

debugging:

        0) enable i8042 verbose debugging mode in "dmesg" log:
                echo 1 > /sys/module/i8042/parameters/debug

        1) after a freeze, you should see in "dmesg":
                [...]drivers/input/serio/i8042.c: interrup 1, without any data

        2) and indeed, running:
                watch "cat /proc/interrupts | grep i8042"

        shows that interrupt 1 (keyboard) is blocked/frozen and isn't ticking anymore while pressing keyboard keys.

        3) but "Alt+SysREQ + R-E-I-S-U-B" still works and "rmmod psmouse ; modprobe psmouse" will kickstart interrupt 1

my wild guess about the problem:

        - a motherboard bug that happens only from kernel 2.6.32
        - or a bug introduced in kernel 2.6.32 in the PS/2 linux subsystem
        - or a bug related to interrupt handling


hope this helps.

---

### Post by greylander on 2010-09-08
4 days with no freeze, using kernel 2.6.34 and the standard Nvidia "Version Current" driver in System>Administration>Hardware Drivers.

4 days is an extremely long time for me to go without freeze, especially when I have been using the computer pretty heavily all that time. I'm about 99.9% sure the problem is fixed for me.

I also want to note that my graphics in general seem much more stable -- many weird artifacts, especially on bootup and shutdown, are now gone.

For anyone coming here who jumped to the last page, if you have an Nvidia graphics card, see post #264 on page 97 of this thread: [http://ubuntu-virginia.ubuntuforums....478787&page=97](http://ubuntu-virginia.ubuntuforums....478787&page=97)  (go check it out -- upgrading your kernel is not as scary as it sounds, it is pretty much like installing any other package)

I agree with those who say this seems like an xorg bug (and I assume, perhaps incorrectly, that xorg is not part of the kernel), however, upgrading to kernel 2.6.34 nonetheless seems to have worked for me.

The freezes are probably due to some subtle interactions between motherboard hardware, video hardware and drivers, kernel, and xorg.  There is probably more than one "bug" at fault.  Perhaps there is a bug fixed in the upgraded kernel, or perhaps some other change in the upgraded kernel somehow avoids a bug in xorg or the drivers.

### Post by g4lch on 2010-09-08
Guy's me CD's from Canonical arrived today and guess what a freeze on my Intel atom just after loading 10.04, so I added a static ip address in safe mode restarted and saw it freeze about 10 times, i could now ssh into the box and see what was happening. As I suspected it was the xserver that was bending over.
 
Proved that the machine has not hung its just the xserver that has stopped responding

now lets see what card we have an Intel card,

apt-get remove xserver-xorg-video-intel

wait for it to finish, restart and no more freeze issues

The give away is when you see frame buffer errors and xserver errors in the logs.

Gave it a real hammering downloading 9.x 62 and 32 bit but loads of torrent stuff and MP3 plus video and email and it actually seems a little faster as well.

I am going to try the same on the Acer Aspire as I am convinced that this is the problem as that has a derivative of the GMA950 graphics adapter.

Which is what is in this atom based machine.


Regards
Mark

---

### Post by g4lch on 2010-09-08
Guy's I have been able to rid my systems all of all the unwanted freeze issues with 

apt-get remove xserver-xorg-video-intel 

Both of the intel atom processors and the Acer are now fully operational and no crashes, I reinstalled all of them from clean canonical disk's and then verified that they froze after install then removed the intel xserver support and they are now operating as normal.

It seems that the xserver with this intel support just dies for no reason I have yet to install get it to freeze and then troll the log files.


Regards
Mark
):P
