### Post by StewartM on 2010-09-08
> **daviescr said:**
> I didn't have any freezing problems until I upgraded from 8.xx. Initially I had problems with the kernal 2.6.32.24. In my eyes this is unsable and should be removed. Some sort of error in the grub which would stop the booting process at intramfs.

That was because intramfs failed to update on that kernel.

See here for the workaround:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1565714](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1565714)

> 1) Boot the computer. Hold down the "shift" key when it's booting.

2) You will enter Grub 2. It should show you the list of kernels on your system. Select the one before 2.6.32-24. (Don't choose a "recovery mode" option.

3) Boot your system--it should boot normally.

4) Run from the terminal:

sudo update-initramfs –u -k 2.6.32-24-generic

(assuming that is the problem kernel for you. You can always check your kernel by typing "uname-r")

5) Reboot


StewartM

---

### Post by Mumbly on 2010-09-08
Like many of you, I have had the random freeze issues since going to 10.04 (keyboard not working, mouse moves but screen totally unresponsive). I was following one of the earlier posts regarding disabling ACPI in the BIOS. Feeling brave, I did a clean install of 10.04, and went into the BIOS to disable ACPI. After doing so,I rebooted only to have my BIOS stuck at the "Verifying DMI Pool Data" portion of the post. I restarted, went into the BIOS and reset it to defaults (which enabled ACPI again). I booted into 10.04, and here I sit almost 6 days later with no freezes (previously I could not get 30 seconds past logging in). I did an apt-get upgrade to get everything to the latest and greatest after the install, still no freezes. I also disabled Power Management per this [post]("http://digitalspine.blogspot.com/2010/08/freezing-ubuntu-1004.html"). Note that I have not messed with GRUB at all as recommended in the link. 

I'm using the 2.6.32-24-generic kernel. I have two 7300GT NVIDIA cards running in SLI, and I am at 195.36.24 for the drivers. I have thrown a number of things at this system to test it, and it still continues to run. There are now nearly 1000 posts on this thread, but I did not see one that recommended resetting the BIOS to defaults. Granted, I may have missed one in here somewhere. I just thought I'd pass it on to see if others may be helped by it.

---

### Post by malv83 on 2010-09-09
> **aer0usa said:**
> I'm glad I'm not the only one getting that... :)

Wouldn't it be easier if we all just drank the MS Kool-Aid? Personally, I prefer the flavor of the Linux Kool-Aid. Usually. :-\

I used to recommend Linux to novice computers users like my parents. After having to solve too many broken updates with Ubuntu I had to finally concede that Windows is the most appropriate environment for a user that doesn't want to have to fight with their OS. There are just far too many obvious and critical bugs in Ubuntu to recommend it to an inexperienced user without a strong hacker ethic. While I still prefer using Linux over Windows as a software developer, I find myself beginning to lose more and more patience with Ubuntu with every new release. When will we get to a point of having a solid release of all the core functionality? Why are more features being added when the current features remain in such an unstable state? I wish the Ubuntu devs would spend more time on stability and polish and less time on features. This bug should have never reached release, and the fact that it did makes me question the whole entire Ubuntu development process and the feasibility of a community-developed OS as a contender to commercial OS for the casual desktop user. It's been months and this bug affecting so many users has not been resolved. Why not?

### Post by Peter Bell on 2010-09-10
I have two machines running 10.04, both with Intel graphics.  Both were experiencing problems - one would just stop with a hung X.  The other gives frequent reports that the video configuration can't be determined (I can't remember the exact message at the moment) and that it is reverting to low resolution mode (in fact, the resolution stays the same (1280x1024), but the screen updates are much s-l-o-w-e-r.

On the first machine, I updated to the Maverick backport kernel (2.6.35-19), and the problems have gone away.

On the second machine I updated to 2.6.35-20 kernel, and the problem showed up within an hour.

I am convinced that there are several faults affecting graphics/X, particularly associated with the monitor entering (or leaving?) power-save mode - kernel/X/nvidia/intel.

I am continuing to search for the fix for the second machine.

### Post by eltama on 2010-09-10
> **bilodeau said:**
> Howdy All!

My two cents on this:

I followed the instructions at:

[HTML]http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1478787&highlight=system+freeze&page=91[/HTML]

and added the 'acpi_skip_timer_override' to my kernel boot options.  This has stopped the weird system halts for me.

On my system, the halts happened at random and resulted in my monitor going into power save mode with no keyboard or mouse input having any effect.  I had to manually reset the system to get things back in working order.

Adding the 'acpi_skip_timer_override' to the file at /etc/default/grub has _completely_ eliminated this for me.  I didn't have to remove _any_ software.  I have been running for two weeks with the modified kernel boot parameters without any problems.

For those who may find such information useful, I am running:

64-bit kernel: 2.6.32-24-generic #42-Ubuntu SMP
CPU: AMD Athlon x4 630
Motherboard: ASUS M4A785TD-M EVO

If you're having weird system halting problems, I suggest you try the solution posted in the link above.  It may resolve your situation.

Cheers

I've tried this option but it didn't work for me.
I have a Dell laptop with and Intel graphics card that I use at work. I've already lost too much work and time with this problem I'll try 10.10 or even another distro.

I also have 10.04 on my desktop with an NVIDIA card at home but I only experienced a freeze once.

### Post by Dragynn on 2010-09-14

### Post by siabost on 2010-09-14
Yo Dragynn,

Let me clarify: I agree you didn't trash anyone. But also you didn't wave goodbye to Ubuntu as you are replying on a reliable & stable installation of same.

I am subscribed to this thread because I have had a problem same as you and the other posters. I also am replying on a stable installation of 10.04.

The problem that all Linux OSes suffer from is a lack of support by manufacturers who are either hugely pressurised to "recommend" Win7 or are completely tied in by Apple for their OS. This is what leads to these incompatibility bugs. Even so, we can still install Linux off a single disk and this has to cover/support all versions of all hardware since 2000.

An impossible task, until Linux distros get to a level where it's in the interests of manufacturers to support Linux Operating Systems.

Having said that I know a number of folk who didn't have a problem on 9.10 but have had some on 10.04. Like them & you I'm waiting for a solution but not everyone seems to have the same one.

My point is there will be blips but ultimately if you like it as I do, and you seem to, we can get annoyed but we, hopefully, will stick with it.

Overall my experience with Ubuntu has been extremely positive.

Best,

:D

Wow, if you think Puppy is "stripped down", you need to take another look, i'm writing this on Puppy 5.1.1 through the Iron browser on the very machine that Ubuntu could no longer support, with nary a glitch, and faster than Ubuntu, Windoze or any other OS could run it, got 3872 last night on Peacekeeper with this ancient relic. Currently there are over 170 different apps/utilities/programs on the default menu I have before loading any more that are available, I am actually begging the devs to MAKE a stripped down version.

Look, sorry if my post offended you, that's always gonna happen though on public forums, somebody always gets their feelings hurt, but the squeaky wheel gets the greasing as they used to say, and I was a bit appalled by the multitude of people experiencing these problems, so I used a little more direct tone on theirs and my behalf. I didn't automatically assume something was wrong with Ubuntu, I spent hours doing my own diagnostics and back-tracking hardware changes etc., figuring it was MY fault somehow, as it turns out, it was not. And from reading thousands of posts in this forum, it seems to me that this problem has been tossed out to the people in the forums to group-fix, that is a BAD precedent if your stated intent is to get widespread public acceptance and usage of your OS.

That's all. I apologize if I offended you with my posts. But I still stand behind my words and actions.

No need for apologizing.  I just feel bad for the developers who work really hard to provide something for us to use...at no cost (money-wise.  I am sure many of us are keeping tab of the time we spend trouble-shooting Ubuntu).  Keep in mind that many developers hold regular jobs like ours and that Ubuntu 10.04 LTS works fine for many other users.

### Post by magneze on 2010-09-16
My problem came back after upgrading to a later kernel. However, I noticed a couple of helpful things recently: it never locked up when using a wired networking and, more usefully, wireless networking doesn't lock up if I disabled Bluetooth.

So it seems to be some kind of interaction between wireless networking and Bluetooth. I don't use Bluetooth so I just leave it disabled all the time now.

---

### Post by Blattone on 2010-09-17
I finally get to solve the freeze problem on my Pc.
I uninstalled *network-manager-gnome*, and switched to **WICD** for managing Wireless connection. Since then, freeze happens only if I press "refresh" button on WICD. But if I don't touch WICD, as I am doing from several weeks, there are no freezes at all.
Most interesting, if I try to switch back to network-manager-gnome, the freeze problem reoccurs.

---

### Post by Vrroom on 2010-09-17
If using compiz effects, what about monitor refresh rate?? Compiz may not set refresh rate correctly in auto detect settings. I had hangups initially but then I corrected monitor refresh rate manually in comiz settings and there were no more freezes.

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-09-21
I've not posted before but I've got this problem on a desktop PC I made up a few weeks ago. It's got an Asrock Dual-Sata mobo with Belkin wifi card and it makes no difference if I use a single or dual core Athlon cpu. I did a fresh install of 10.04 which brought grub2 and ext4. Memory and hard drive testing show no errors

I've an almost identical PC which doesn't freeze, an Asrock Dual Vsta with wired ethernet, runs 10.04 (because it was upgraded it has Grub and ext3). I've also got a third older and slower PC which runs 10.04 without any freezes, probably because it too wasn't a fresh install, has Grub and ext3.

The best I've managed so far is to reduce the number of freezes by adding pci=noacpi in Grub2. I'm not able to shut down properly if I boot with acpi=off.

A freeze here looks like this-
screen dims and goes grey
I can still move the mouse pointer initially
I might be able to open one dialogue box but nothing much more happens
little response from keyboard
nothing in syslog and message log files
hard drive red led on
ctrl+alt+del sometimes gives a I/O error message
mostly I'm left to do a hard reset
on reboot a wifi connection takes a while to be established

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-09-22
I have had the same problem with hangs ever since I installed 10.04 months ago - it was an upgrade from 8.04LTS.  It's odd that it has been happening for so long and still there is no solution?  Do the powers-that-be believe there even IS a problem at this point?

My system:

AMD Athlon XP 3200 Barton
ASUS A7N8X-E Deluxe
3GB RAM
nVidia 6800XT AGP 256MB (have tried both available 3D-capable hardware drivers)

Also, I have an SBLive! sound card, and was having trouble with simply playing audio (never had any issues in 8.04) so pulled the card out... but I still get the hangs even with only the on-board audio.

All of my hangs have been a sudden freeze/lock-up... I can't do anything to resolve it except power-cycle.  The screen freezes (no gray-screen or anything like that - just a "freeze frame"), the mouse won't move, and the system is not answering pings/SSH/etc.  If I am playing sound, it will get stuck and loop for about 5-10 seconds and then stop altogether.  Generally, at the time of the hang, I'm working in a bunch of gnome-terminals, have Firefox and/or SeaMonkey open (sometimes actively using them, sometimes not, but always one or both are open). Sometimes, I'm listening to music.  Sometimes I'm watching a video in a browser.  FWIW, it seems that if I watch videos in the browser for an extended period of time, eventually, I get the hang.

I've noticed that since I started using 10.04, the X display will do this weird "refresh" kind of thing from time to time... like the entire screen will flash white followed by a normal X refresh/redraw (all of which happens in a blink of an eye).  It's that sort of thing (and the fact that a number of lock-ups have occurred when watching videos) that makes me think that it might be the proprietary nVidia drivers or sound-related (why can't they just stop mucking with the SOUND SYSTEM in Linux for cryin' out loud - just use ALSA or whatever and quit the "okay... now everything uses Elightenment Sound Daemon" / "Pulse Audio" / "This shiny new sound thingy that still ends up requiring OSS/ALSA", etc.).  But then again, the hangs don't always involve video/audio.

I'm not sure what to do at this point.  The reason I upgraded from 8.04 to 10.04 was to get the latest versions of all the packages, new Firefox, etc., without having to install stuff by hand (which is futile when the new stuff requires newer versions of the Gnome libraries or X or such).  After upgrading, I was bitten by the lack of ATI driver support... had a Radeon 9600XT in there previously, so bought the nVidia 6800XT to sidestep that problem, but then 10.04 turns out to have this major "random hang" problem.

I haven't read all of the posts in this thread... but can't the kernel guys give us an instrumented kernel that can isolate what is happening when we get a hang so we can actually FIND the solution?

More info:
My BIOS has everything on the power management page turned off except for DPMS.

There is another setting called "APIC Mode" that is enabled.

I disabled "Power Manger" under "System->Startup Applications" but it seems to have no effect on the problem.  However, despite this and the lack of the ACPI stuff being enabled in the BIOS, there are a number of process running that have ACPI in the name:

```

root        16     2  0 Sep21 ?        00:00:00 [kacpid]
root        17     2  0 Sep21 ?        00:00:00 [kacpi_notify]
root        18     2  0 Sep21 ?        00:00:00 [kacpi_hotplug]
root      1173     1  0 Sep21 ?        00:00:00 acpid -c /etc/acpi/events -s /var/run/acpid.socket
107       1648  1538  0 Sep21 ?        00:00:00 hald-addon-acpi: listening on acpid socket /var/run/acpid.socket

```I don't have "pci=noacpi" set at boot.  From what I've read... it seems to mostly just reduce the number of hangs for some people.

### Post by coffee412 on 2010-09-22
Hello,

I have been experiencing alot of these problems on an older emachine for a customer. I went thru everything and performed the following:

1. Tested memory (memtest) for a few hours.
2. Replaced memory.
3. Replaced video card (built in sis card to nvidia MX400 card).
4. Removed flash in FF.

I would experience the total lockup of the system to where I would have to do a hard boot. This was very annoying to my customer and I had to find a solution.

I ended up monitoring his system from across the 'net and found the following clue.

> Clocksource tsc unstable (delta = -150788813 ns)I edited my /etc/default/grub file and added "apci=off" and updated. So far no more issues from the customer. It has been only one full day though.

Perhaps you are getting this and just not noticing it in the logs? Im not saying this is a cure-all just that so far it seems to be working for me. (probably until I actually post this and then the customer will call me with another lockup! :P).


> There is another setting called "APIC Mode" that is enabled.
Did you try turning off apic in bios and see if problem still exists?

---

### Post by gwilki5691 on 2010-09-22
Hi all,
I too have had random lockups for the last few weeks, which has been rather frustrating:confused:. The lockups can happen from within minutes of booting to within a couple of hours, but usually no longer than that. My PC runs fine under Windows and has been working fine for a couple of months since installing Lucid so I am pretty sure it is not hardware.

My config:
10.04 64bit, 2.6.32-24-generic (Dual boot with WinXP)
AMD Phenom II X2 550
8GB RAM
Asus M4A78LT-M Mobo with AMD760G/SB710 Chipset
Integrated ATI Graphics (1GB shared) with 2 x 1920x1080 Monitors

Anyhow, after my last lockup, as these forums have advised, I looked at the sys.log and noticed;
> Sep 21 23:23:31 gary-desktop pulseaudio[2498]: pid.c: Daemon already running.
Sep 21 23:23:36 gary-desktop pulseaudio[2393]: ratelimit.c: 2 events suppressed
These were the last entries before the lockup. I am unsure whether this has any bearing on the problem but thought the problem may be with the sound. I then thought about what programs I have installed recently which are audio related and remembered installing MythTV. Out of total frustration and being willing to try just about anything I completely uninstalled MythTV and rebooted.

So far so good, I have been running perfectly now for 6 hours and my sys.log seems clear of any similar errors.:)

I don't know whether this info is useful to anyone but I thought it was worth mentioning. I will update if the problem comes back.

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-09-22
[QUOTE=coffee412]Hello,

I edited my /etc/default/grub file and added "apci=off" and updated. So far no more issues from the customer. It has been only one full day though.
[/QUOTE]

I don't appear to have an /etc/default/grub file on my system.  Did you have to run something to generate it?  According to the docs I've found, it looks like you have to add the boot options to an existing line that should already be there and have the default options in it.

Do you mean "acpi=off"?

[QUOTE=coffee412]Perhaps you are getting this and just not noticing it in the logs? Im not saying this is a cure-all just that so far it seems to be working for me. (probably until I actually post this and then the customer will call me with another lockup! :P).

Did you try turning off apic in bios and see if problem still exists?[/QUOTE]

I do see similar log messages from time to time, but they don't seem to correspond to the hangs (i.e. there are other messages later, and not any messages that would "point the finger" at something).

I don't like the idea of turning off "APIC mode" in the BIOS... that sounds like new PCI IRQ troubles just waiting to happen.  I've never had to turn it off before, so there's no reason I should have to now on the same hardware, and I wouldn't expect the system to operate correctly when changing this, especially if my USB controllers and the rest of the incidental hardware add up to more than 16 IRQs.

I might try "pci=noacpi" in the boot options... maybe I'll just reboot and type it in by hand (if I can figure out how to do that) and then see if I get a hang.  I'm not sure why either of these changes should be needed on the same hardware... but I WOULD like to avoid the hangs until someone figures out the actual cause (<duh>)...

Do you still see "acpi" related process running even when you run with "acpi=off"?

Another data point: I run VirtualBox... does anyone else notice whether they get hangs only with/without VirtualBox running?  I think, so far, I've always had it running when the hangs occur.  There are some messages (that don't coincide with hangs) in the log that look related to PIT and TSC specific to VirtualBox (but similar to what coffee412 noted with pulseaudio) but they seem like VB startup messages... an example:

```

Sep 21 15:41:32 toaster kernel: [    0.000000] Fast TSC calibration using PIT
Sep 21 15:41:32 toaster kernel: [    0.179477] Switching to clocksource tsc
Sep 21 15:41:37 toaster kernel: [   59.700467] vboxdrv: TSC mode is 'synchronous', kernel timer mode is 'normal'.

```

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-09-23
I tried booting with "pci=noacpi" by editing the boot options interactively at boot time... no dice.  Machine resets immediately upon trying to load the kernel.  Also, tried "acpi=off"... same result.

I did an upgrade... maybe it didn't actually change my bootloader to Grub2...?

```
dpkg -l | egrep -i grub
ii  grub                                       0.97-29ubuntu60                                            GRand Unified Bootloader (Legacy version)
ii  grub-common                                1.98-1ubuntu7                                              GRand Unified Bootloader, version 2 (common files)

```

Actually, according to the file lists for these packages, it would appear that I have a mix of grub versions... /sbin/update-grub is from the "grub" package, while there are other grub programs like "grub-mkconfig" that are part of the "grub-common" package.  What a mess.

### Post by richie.r on 2010-09-25
Guys, this is the first time I have have posted on this forum, and it all has to do with this random freezing thing. I have been monitoring this thread and will my hardware setup and experiences trying a few different things. These are just observations, no cures here.

My Hardware
ASUS P5GL-MX m/board
1GB RAM
Celeron D Processor 2.8 GHz
500 GB Seagate Hardrive/ Asus DVDRW
Asus Nvidia 7200 256MB Video Card

The following linux packages have all frozen for me (all latest releases): Ubuntu 9.04/9.10/10.04/10.10; PClinuxOS; SUSE; Xubuntu.
Slitaz was the only stable release.

I am currently in 10.04 and have my setup as follows:
Kernel 2.6.32-24 (EXT 3 partition)
Gnome 2.30.2
Nvidia Driver 195.36.24
Firefox 3.6.10 with flash installed

This was a clean install on a 50GB partition, with 9.04 on another 10GB EXT 3 partition, a 200GB home partition for 9.04 and 2GB swap. Visual effects are set to normal.

I noticed in this set up initally that freezes happened randomly, but the highest proportion were happening with firefox and more so when I had flash installed. (I played around with having it installed and uninstalled)

Last night I took some recent advice and set my bios power settings to 
Suspend Mode = Auto
Repost Video on S3 = NO
ACPI 2.0 Support = NO
ACPI APIC Support = Disabled. 

This morning I turned on the computer and have left it on for 12 hours solid with no freezes. I have used Firefox with lots of video, downloaded large files opened and closed lost of windows and applications, no problems. I have also let it sit idle for 4 hours. I willcontinue to update my progress in the hope that it can help anyone out there or the developers. Cheers

### Post by SwedishWings on 2010-09-28
I think i have found something that is related to this issue, but am not sure. When making heavy use of the network, the machine freezes randomly after a few minutes of heavy network traffic, but always resume after a few seconds.

In my case, I'm 100% sure this is a network issue. If i do
```
$ tar cf /tmp/home.tar /home
```
i get no freezing at all, however, this creates random freezing after a few minutes:
```
$ tar cf /mnt/nfs-server/home.tar /home
```

Note that my /home folder is about 8G.

Can anyone try this and see if it's related?

### Post by SwedishWings on 2010-09-29
> **SwedishWings said:**
> I think i have found something that is related to this issue, but am not sure. When making heavy use of the network, the machine freezes randomly after a few minutes of heavy network traffic, but always resume after a few seconds.

In my case, I'm 100% sure this is a network issue. If i do
```
$ tar cf /tmp/home.tar /home
```
i get no freezing at all, however, this creates random freezing after a few minutes:
```
$ tar cf /mnt/nfs-server/home.tar /home
```

Note that my /home folder is about 8G.

Can anyone try this and see if it's related? 

Thanks,
Mike



I noticed something interesting after noodling around a bit... If i stop GDM, and then run the above command in one console <Alt-Ctrl-F1>, and then switch to another console <Alt-Ctrl-F2>, there are no hickups what so ever. I wrote a little test program that i run in console 2 that animates @ 10Hz and there were NO freezing at all (i stared at that animation for 15 minutes, not a single slow down)! 

This leads me to believe that it is a combination of Video/X.org/Gnome and network traffic that causes this issues in my case. 

Someone earlier in this thread suspected a PCI issue, and that would make sense in my case too.

I have tried a couple of things with no change: switched between the X.org driver and NVidia drivers, added "noacpi" to grub.cfg, removed the nVidia PCI card, run the tar command as root.

### Post by Blattone on 2010-09-29
For sure is a network related problem.
So,**_ I solved ALL my problems switching from network-manager-gnome to WICD_**. Please try this, and then report the results!

---

### Post by SwedishWings on 2010-09-29
> **Blattone said:**
> For sure is a network related problem.
So,**_ I solved ALL my problems switching from network-manager-gnome to WICD_**. Please try this, and then report the results!

The ***network-manager*** is evil, Blattone is right!

I had never expected to see such a big difference after getting rid of the evil network-manager in my machine. The overall CPU load have dropped significantly, network traffic is much faster, and most importantly, no more freezing! 

I tried wicd, but went a step further and used good old fashioned /etc/init.d/networking and configured /etc/network/interfaces manually (the machine is stationary cabled anyway, so WiFi is not an issue here).

Another very interesting aspect is that my backup program daemon (written in C, launched hourly from crontab) for the first time ever runs smooth with high nice-value when backing up to my NFS servers. Before, it loaded the machine a lot and slowed down Xorg, but now i can't even notice that it's running - I'm stunned...

I encourage others to try this out as well.

Thanks Blattone, you saved my day!

*To the developers of network-manager: PLEASE, take this (and earlier criticism) for real, this is a significant problem!*

---

I have posted before about this issue with APCI however, There are alot of posts everyday and it might have been missed. Im going to post it again here if you dont mind as this might solve alot of problems. 

Here we go:
===========

On your motherboard you have a circuit that keeps track of time periods. This is not related to your date or time. This is more related to the timing of components in your computer and is directly related to ACPI. I have discovered that on some boards this circuit does not keep a very accurate period. Thus, Your system locks up. If you were to take a look at your logs you will get an error similar to this:

> Clocksource tsc unstableThis means that your clock source is UNSTABLE. This means you will experience all kinds of weird lockups. This means that you will have to select a different clocksource.

This may very well be your source of the problem and now this is how I fixed it:

First we want to see what clock source you are using. From the error message you can deduce that you are using tsc.

Second, We want to figure out what available clocksources are on your system and try one of the others instead. We do this by opening up a term window and reading the contents of your available_clocksource file.

> cat /sys/devices/system/clocksource/clocksource0/available_clocksourcePossible entries are: acpi_pm pit jiffies tsc acpi_pm hpet

Now we will select a new clocksource by adding a line (or altering one if its there) in your /etc/default/grub file.

> ACPI=whichever clocksource you selectedNow we want to incorporate the changes into grub:

> sudo grub-updateNow reboot and see if your still having problems.

If your change does not help then select another and try again. From what I remember, jiffies is a software clocksource. 

If you do not have the "Unstable" error in your logs then this probably will not help you and you will have to look elsewhere for your issue.

Hope this helps :P,

---

Here we go:
===========

On your motherboard you have a circuit that keeps track of time periods. This is not related to your date or time. This is more related to the timing of components in your computer and is directly related to ACPI. I have discovered that on some boards this circuit does not keep a very accurate period. Thus, Your system locks up. If you were to take a look at your logs you will get an error similar to this:

This means that your clock source is UNSTABLE. This means you will experience all kinds of weird lockups. This means that you will have to select a different clocksource.

This may very well be your source of the problem and now this is how I fixed it:

First we want to see what clock source you are using. From the error message you can deduce that you are using tsc.

Second, We want to figure out what available clocksources are on your system and try one of the others instead. We do this by opening up a term window and reading the contents of your available_clocksource file.

Possible entries are: acpi_pm pit jiffies tsc acpi_pm hpet

Now we will select a new clocksource by adding a line (or altering one if its there) in your /etc/default/grub file.

Now we want to incorporate the changes into grub:

Now reboot and see if your still having problems.

If your change does not help then select another and try again. From what I remember, jiffies is a software clocksource. 

If you do not have the "Unstable" error in your logs then this probably will not help you and you will have to look elsewhere for your issue.

Hope this helps :P,

### Post by coffee412 on 2010-10-03
I just want to chime in here:

One thing that really hampers me from helping others is the lack of information provided about the problem. If possible (even newbies) one should provide as much information as possible. Otherwise you are left asking questions and wasting time.

A big help would be to see your /var/log/messages log file as this will hold clues to what is going on with your system. 

Next file is the /var/log/Xorg.0.log for any video problems. 

I dont think its a big security issue to attach the logs to a post. I could be wrong but trying to help without info is pretty tough. 

thanx

---

### Post by davidvandoren on 2010-10-03
I am pretty sure the freezes are sata related. Or sata has something to do with it. I am using an ide drive now with 30G and no more crashes. But when I access my 1TB sata drive and start pulling some files strange things happen. 
For example the progress-bar pauses and the monitor dims. Then after a few seconds comes back and the file progresses to copy to my 30G drive. Also when the sata drive is mounted application close unexpected.

---

### Post by Dragynn on 2010-10-03
> **edward1978 said:**
> Nope, but I read a post saying someone has tried 20 distros & none freeze accept Ubuntu & that is with the same hardware. F13 may not freeze, so it might be a waste of time installing it.

Yep. And Windows too, no freezes but Ubuntu....

Just wondering something I don't recall being mentioned yet in this loooooooong thread (could be wrong, haven't read all 1100 posts), but i'm wondering if part of the problem might be in the switch to the ext4 filesystem by Ubuntu? After some thought, I realized that after Ubuntu died, and prior to trying all the other distros I have tried, I re-formatted the partition I run Linux on, to ext3, and have not allowed any other distro to change that.

To re-cap my timeline/problem:

Wiped a harddrive clean earlier this year.

Clean install of Windoze XP.

Clean Install of Ubuntu 10.04 Lucid Lynx, dual boot with Windoze.

Runs fine for a month.

Change hardware= took out old AMD single-core 3200, swapped in AMD dual core 3800. Also added some ram. Mobo and all hardware supports the swap/add.

Booted into Windows first, checked operation, found new hardware and installed with no issues, all progs run fine, better than ever in fact as one would expect from a minor upgrade like this.

Booted into Ubuntu, everything fires right up at first, 10 minutes later, first freeze occurs, re-boot, 5 minutes later it freezes again. After 20 re-boots or so, i'm starting to believe there is a problem.

Booted from CD on Puppy Linux 5.1.1 "Lucid Puppy" based on Ubuntu, boots fine and runs great, no problems, tried Slitaz and several other versions of Puppy and other ram-install distros, no problems.

Started checking forums, try half a dozen suggestions, no luck.

Try to remove Ubuntu so I can try a new clean install, Ubuntu crashes right as i'm beginning the process, computer will no longer even boot afterwards, get nothing but "grub rescue" prompt.

Fixed MBR with Windoze disc, wWindows back up, running smoothly still. Re-format partition to ext3 with G-parted. All other distros continue to work fine. 

A week later, a laptop with an identical dual-boot Windoze/Ubuntu install starts exhibiting similar psychosis, I don't even bother at this point, just start getting rid of Ubuntu, and in the middle of this process the laptop harddrive freaks out and locks up, laptop now a brick until I get a new one ( but will still boot and run just fine with Puppy).
 
Hard-drive install of PCLinuxOS on the desktop and specified it install to existing part/filesystem (ext3), runs like a champ, no issues at all. Still running smoothly a week later.

I recall some articles being written, and potential issues with ext4 in Ubuntu being discussed, I wonder if there still might be problems that are only set-off if certain conditions are met?

### Post by Dragynn on 2010-10-04
> **cd-r80 said:**
> I have Lucid with ext3. Nothing to do with the freezes (ext4).

Still doesn't mean it's not the problem, if there are conflicts with the filesystem, it can go both ways due to software, i.e. some of it has been written/optimized for ext4 and some has not. You might get freezes using either one, originating from different sources within your system, with the end result still being that your system freezes, and the basic culprit in both cases being the filesystem compatibility with the software.

### Post by elmy on 2010-10-07
> **malv83 said:**
> I used to recommend Linux to novice computers users like my parents. After having to solve too many broken updates with Ubuntu I had to finally concede that Windows is the most appropriate environment for a user that doesn't want to have to fight with their OS.

I hear that.....

Windows ME was more stable than this release.

After 6 months, I have managed to fix the issue with the Sound Card not initialising, which would sometimes work and sometimes not. Finally turned out that by adding myself to the audio group, the problem cleared up.

The same solution fixed the issue with not being able to mount my USB drive (whenever the sound didn't work this didn't either). I added myself to the disks group and fixed that.

All through this I have also had the "cannot shut down the computer" issue where it just goes back to the login instead of shutting down. It is still doing this. all the while I have had crash after crash after crash, at random intervals both when its idol or under load.

Its not compiz, its not the ati radeon mobility with mesa drivers not being installed correctly, its not cairo dock, and it sure as hell isn't a hardware failure. I've stuffed around with it for ages and still the mouse and keyboard locks up and the screen goes black and all you can see is the stuck cursor in top left and the white mouse pointer. caps lock light and power light flash (indicating a crash). If this happens during the night while torrenting then my computer starts to overheat, because the CPU is still active, but the fan cuts out and it sits all night.

I'm fed up with spending all my time trying to fix and update my computers (both this one with Linux, and my Windows 7, and my windows XP). I just get things working then Microsoft send an update that screws it up, or 10.10 will come out and screw up everything I just spent the last 6 months fixing. However in fairness, Windows 7 is no better. I've had a nasty little wifi knot to untie on it recently, that suddenly stopped working after a Windows Update. At least with Linux it doesn't cost me $400 Australian for an OS that in my opinion is not really any better than XP. Windows 7 is simply Vista Service Pack II

I want to actually use.... yes USE my computer for a change. That's a novel idea isn't it, actually doing something productive with it would be nice, and Windows is just as guilty of this. Seems that whenever I get anything running smoothly, something else breaks.

Sigh... I'm just venting, but it is getting quite frustrating, as I'm no closer with this crash issue. I can live with work arounds and bugs, but constant crashing is a serious problem in regards to reliability.

I'm no expert, but I do try to learn, and I'm stoked that I actually resolved the sound and usb drive problem I was having. Some progress is at least better than none at all. Its for this reason that I won't put Windows back on my laptop. The only way to become more experienced and understand Linux better is to persist. I must admit it sure is easier to get to the bottom of problems in Windows, it does give it somewhat of an advantage, which makes it tempting to quit and go back. But I admire the open source and free nature of Linux. It is certainly a community OS, and the community certainly does improve it, and the more I persist the more I learn. I get frustrated, but that means its time to take a break, and have another crack at it tomorrow, because that frustration comes mostly from my impatience and lack of experience and understanding and having to rely on support. I like to be self sufficient with my computer (as I am in Windows), and I'm not there yet with Linux, and I feel it will be some time before I am.

I hope 10.10 fixes the crash issue, but I fear it won't, because the underlying cause seems to elude everyone, or be due to different issues due to varying machines (but with similar symptoms). It might not be possible to do a generic fix that solves the problem for everyone.

### Post by jrl on 2010-10-07
I have read the thread and suffer the same problem but only to a limited extent.

Systems:

Two intel servers running 10.04.1LTS.  

One has a single guest (virtual machine) with two cores allocated to it.

The other server has four guests, one guestv with three threads assigned to it and the others sharing a core.

The hosts run the standard generic server kernel and the guests the virtual machine kernel.

An Asus laptop running the generic kernel with a mix of gnome and lxde.

All except the hosting servers are updated regularly (weekly).

The only box running a graphical interface is the laptop.

Of the eight "machines" only two have had any problems with freezing, the laptop once and the virtual machine with three threads assigned to it about weekly although sometimes as regularly as daily.

The virtual machines are very similar and run on identical hardware (Intel case, Intel motherboard, Intel nics, Kingston ram and WD discs). The laptop is very different of course.

So what have I tried.

Different virtualisations of the nics and drives. Helped dramatically as there are issues with multi threaded guests and some types of virtual I/O.

Kept updating the kernel as new releases occur. Hard to tell whether recently this has made a difference but one lives in hope. Initially it was almost possible to predict when there would be a freeze. Tried the mainstream 2.6.35rc1 kernel but no difference.

This all still begs the question what is different between the six hosts that have performed faultlessly and the other two.

Forgetting the laptop for a minute what is different between guests?  Two have multiple threads assigned to them four don't. All see exactly the same hardware. Most do a reasonable amount of disc activity.  What really differs is that the host that freezes passes much more data than the others and acts as a router (about 4 TeraBytes per day). The host running the guest of courses sees this and more but without the same degree of processing, it not doing detailed traffic accounting, queuing or the same degree of firewalling.  

When the host "freezes" it does not stop. The virtual machine instance on the host goes to 300+-5% (matching the three threads assigned to it). Logging onto it through VNC is unsuccessful and it has to be restarted. Logging on the guest stops so there is nothing to help here and nothing is logged on the the host either.  

With the initial kernels the transfer of logged data from the high data guest to another guest on the same host could cause a freeze but never on the receiving guest.

Previously we ran a similar system on individual hosts until the hardware began to fail.  They all ran earlier kernels (Debian Ubuntu and Arch.)

This has lead me to the conclusion that there is a kernel issue related to IO brought on predominantly by network activity. It is more than a networking problem otherwise logging would continue, it is not a network hardware issue otherwise the hosting instance would also freeze. It is unlikely to be a virtualisation problem otherwise the receiving guest would also have frozen and we run a very similar guest without incident. 

It isn't an X problem and it is unlikely to be a display problem as I am essentially not running a display. Being a virtual machine the host only has a limited range of drivers.

What to do about it.  We will most likely build a replacement  virtual machine based on a previous LTS for the affected host or try Debian.  Ubuntu and Debian are so similar configuring the replacement virtual machine is relatively straight forward.

### Post by tc-progra-soft on 2010-10-07
**[SOLVED] **
I have tested folowing  drivers:
nvidia-current
nv,
nouveau,
nvidia closed:
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-173.14.25-pkg1.run
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-195.36.15-pkg1.run
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-195.36.24-pkg1.run
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-195.36.31-pkg1.run
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-256.53.run
NVIDIA-Linux-x86-260.19.04.run
 with all these drivers I experienced minor ( artifacts only, artifacts  and X crash ) or major problems ( crash of Xserver before login to gnome  ).
I was lucky to get frequent traces in logs - minor crashes starts with the following statement in Xorg.0.log
[SIZE=2][COLOR=Black][mi] EQ overflowing. The server is probably stuck in an infinite loop.[/COLOR][/SIZE]
2 Weeks lost, with approx 50 X crashes. Linux are you Windows ME clone?
[COLOR=Black]_IT IS NOT ANY DRIVER FAULT!_[/COLOR]
[COLOR=Black]The problem was solved by downgrading gdm package
from:[COLOR=Red] 2.30.2.is.2.30.0-0ubuntu3
[COLOR=Black]to: gdm 2.30.0-0ubuntu5  
The last one is a good one!
[/COLOR][/COLOR]BEWARE OF gdm [COLOR=Red]2.30.2.is.2.30.0-0ubuntu3[/COLOR] !!! Ticking Time bomb!
Best regards,
Tomasz[/COLOR]

### Post by Esau on 2010-10-12
I've been following and contributing to this thread since it's inception. I've tried numerous "fixes" on 10.04 to no avail.

Recently backed-up my data and installed 10.10 RC last week. It's been a chugging along "fine" with no freezing thus far. I say "fine" because though it doesn't freeze any more it's still glitchy, i.e., compiz no longer works, my laptop monitor is not recognised, and watching video is choppy. So for me that was my "solution". I know others have tried upgrading as well but it's still a no go for them. 

With that being said, I have decided to purchase a new computer and try out a new OS. I love Ubuntu, and the effort that the devs and the community here puts into the project, but I need/want a computer to be able to be to work reliably. Period. I consider myself tech savvy, am the go-to IT for my friends and family, but the amount of time I've invested in trying to get things to just work properly in the past few releases of Ubuntu has been frustrating to say the least ](*,) . I'm definitely going to continue following its maturation but I'm not sure if it's the right OS for me at the moment. 

So good luck with you all.

Viva Ubuntu! :guitar:

### Post by mminorhsd on 2010-10-14
I seem to ( Fingers crossed ) solved my freezing/crashing problems with 10.04.

I upgraded to server version but still had problems. I then realized it was just a black screen, not a complete freeze. So I modified the bios to allocate more memory to video. That helped some. Then I turned off screen saver, as most black screen episodes happened when logging back in from a screen saver episode. That helped a little more. I then began looking at memory usage. If I had more than one browser open or a browser and a terminal session it would freeze. I found that I had only 50k memory free. I upgraded to 1gb of memory and have not had any freezes since then....I hope I am on the road to recovery with Ubuntu, because I would truly like to be able to use it daily....

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-10-15

In the process of setting up the new wifi card I used Wicd and removed Network Manager, then removed Wicid and re-installed Network Manager. (Before finding Launchpad Bug #275230 affects my new RTL8185 card, oh joy)

So far there have been no further freezes. If there are no more in the next few days I'll remove the pci=noacpi from Grub and see what happens.

Edit: It still freezes randomly, sometimes the PC works without a problem for several hours then on the next reboot after 10 minutes.

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-10-15
I've been having a freeze just about once per day this week.  Went for a few days without one before that.

I'm about convinced that VirtualBox aggravates the problem (I ran it the last few days - and the freezing was more frequent).

I've booted with a serial console and have nmi_watchdog=1 so that maybe I can catch a freeze and get something on the console, but so far (several hours) of hammering on it running youtube vids and some 3D stuff and lots of browsing and playing music, etc., and I haven't had a freeze yet.

However, there was mention that this could possibly be something related to ACPI and/or timer stuff... and the watchdog changes the situation.  /proc/interrupts used to have counts for LOC (Local timer interrupts) but now it only has NMI counts.  I'm not sure if that is confounding the test, but it would seem if it doesn't freeze when not using the Local Timer interrupts then... ???

Also, another issue is that when running the watchdog, VirtualBox won't load:

```
Oct 15 16:59:17 toaster kernel: [   31.564696] vboxdrv: NMI watchdog in IO_APIC mode active -- refused to load the kernel module!
Oct 15 16:59:17 toaster kernel: [   31.564699] vboxdrv: Please disable the NMI watchdog by specifying 'nmi_watchdog=0' at kernel
Oct 15 16:59:17 toaster kernel: [   31.564701] vboxdrv: command line.

```So that's a catch-22.

### Post by davidvandoren on 2010-10-17
I think I found the bug.
At least for my pc. Though there are many people reporting crashes and freezes, it does not mean that they are all the same cause.

In one of my Ubuntu installs I got this in the logs very 2 minutes
```
NVRM: os_raise_smp_barrier(), invalid context
``` sometimes 10 in a row.

Symptoms:
Freezes
Applications snap
Drop back to login screen
Drop onto the command line.

My hardware 
NVIDIA graphics N220GT series
Motherboard MSI H55M E33

Installed Windows XP and Ubuntu 10.10 on a 1TB sata
And ubuntu 10.04 on a 30GB hard drive
Ubuntu has the proprietary Drive via Ubuntu installed 

**[SIZE=3]How I fixed this on the Ubuntu 10.10 is as follows.[/SIZE]**

Open terminal 
```
sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/nvidia.conf
```
paste into the open file the following.
```
options nvidia NVreg_RegistryDwords="PerfLevelSrc=0x2222"
```
click on save and **reboot**

Open** NVIDIA X server settings** it's under System, Administration. 
There click on** Powermizer**
There on the Preferred mode **drop-down  menu** chose  ** Maximum Performance.**

---

### Post by Gael33 on 2010-10-17
I have Ubuntu 10.04 and Windows XP (Dual boot) and without reason my mouse started jerking all over the place, sometimes freezing completely. This went on for days until as a last resort I booted into Windows/ Control Panel/ Hardware / Mouse, and checked if the mouse was sharing an IRQ. Somehow, there were two entries; a PS2 Mouse which is fitted to the computer, and an entry for a Bluetooth Mouse which I have never had fitted to my computer. I deleted the Bluetooth entry, rebooted into Ubuntu and my Mouse has behaved since ... I don't know why it happened, it just did. My computer peripherals are now working fine.

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-10-20

Either way the freezes kept occurring.  However, one new behavior: I was watching a YouTube and the X-server crashed taking me instantly back to GDM with 3 messages to /var/adm/messages:

```

Oct 20 12:15:30 toaster kernel: [  617.810881] agpgart-nvidia 0000:00:00.0: AGP 3.0 bridge
Oct 20 12:15:30 toaster kernel: [  617.810897] agpgart-nvidia 0000:00:00.0: putting AGP V3 device into 8x mode
Oct 20 12:15:30 toaster kernel: [  617.810958] nvidia 0000:03:00.0: putting AGP V3 device into 8x mode

### Post by tc-progra-soft on 2010-10-21
I am sorry but downgrading to gdm 2.30.0-0ubuntu5 didn't resolve the problem. The error is still present.
My case most likely is following :
1. the bug occurs -> [mi] EQ overflowing. The server is probably stuck in an infinite loop.
2. overheating of nVidia Corporation G86 [GeForce 8600M GS] (rev a1)
3. artifacts are present
4. crash of X or whole Linux
The GPU usage is important - Dual screen and high resultions are the reasons of more frequent overheating and crashes.

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-10-22
As just posted on Launchpad:
I've been fighting this bug (**Seems there may be a couple of similar bugs, see my version below***)for a couple of weeks on 10.04.1 but not on 10.10.

I got fed up trying to fix it and booted to my 10.10 install, did the latest kernel update...And it started freezing my mouse!

Went back to my 10.04.1 partition and started digging through posts in this thread, and on [Launchpad](https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all).

*First I tried disabling irqbalance by editing* **/etc/default/irqbalance** and **set ENABLED=0**
***That did not work.***

[COLOR="Blue"]Then I added[/COLOR] ```
acpi_skip_timer_override nohz=off
``` [COLOR="Blue"]to my boot options, updated burg (grub) and on reboot,[/COLOR] [COLOR="Red"]NO MORE FREEZING![/COLOR]

***To clarify, my mouse pointer would freeze at random times or just after login, plugging another usb mouse in would work for a time than freeze also, my KB would continue to work for a while, but would eventually stop responding.**

My system:
MSI MB, AMD 9950BE, 4GB RAM, Ubuntu 10.04.1 and 10.10 both x86_64, Latest Ubuntu update generic kernels.
Nvidia GeForce 8200 and Driver 195.36.24 with X server version 1.7.6 (10706000)
Compiz and emerald with full plugins enabled. The 10.10 install is a fresh install, the 10.04.1 install is upgraded 9.04 > 9.10 > 10.04

Note: I lost track of who posted the kernel options acpi_skip_timer_override nohz=off but I thank you from the bottom of my heart! Hopefully this will help some people with this version of the bug, until someone upstream can find out what triggered this.

### Post by the grey side on 2010-10-23
Hi all, I'm posting this for statistical purposes, for what it's worth, in the hope that it'll help sort out all the different problems.
I had serious freezing problems with 9.10 earlier this year, as described here:

[http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1375948](http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1375948)

Installing a later kernel from [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/~kernel-ppa/mainline/]("http://kernel.ubuntu.com/%7Ekernel-ppa/mainline/") solved those problems.
I switched to 10.04 amd64 in late September (clean install, ext4 partition) and immediately installed kernel 2.6.34-020634. I never had any freezes, so I'm confident that mine was a software problem that got fixed willingly or accidentally in the meantime. I followed this thread a little (could never read all of it though) and I wonder if problems with 9.10 (mine wasn't the only one) are the same that people get with 10.04.
I could test earlier kernels to see if they work, but I need my laptop for university and I can't risk anything.

Too bad that this bug is ruining Ubuntu for so many people, because aside from my problems with 9.10 this OS has only gotten better over time for me. The audio and network problems I initially had are gone, and there have been many improvements.

---

Applications snap
Drop back to login screen
Drop onto the command line.

My hardware 
NVIDIA graphics N220GT series
Motherboard MSI H55M E33

Installed Windows XP and Ubuntu 10.10 on a 1TB sata
And ubuntu 10.04 on a 30GB hard drive
Ubuntu has the proprietary Drive via Ubuntu installed 

**[SIZE=3]How I fixed this on the Ubuntu 10.10 is as follows.[/SIZE]**

Open terminal 
```
sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/nvidia.conf
```
paste into the open file the following.
```
options nvidia NVreg_RegistryDwords="PerfLevelSrc=0x2222"
```
click on save and **reboot**

Open** NVIDIA X server settings** it's under System, Administration. 
There click on** Powermizer**
There on the Preferred mode **drop-down  menu** chose  ** Maximum Performance.**

---

### Post by Azrael3000 on 2010-10-25
Below is my original post. But it turns out I was only lucky the last few times and as if actually nothing has changed. So exchanging network-manager with wicd does not get rid of the problem. What still holds true is of course that playing CS does increase the chances of a freeze significantly. I'll once again try disabling networking to see whether this actually helps.

[edit 2] Disabling networking doesn't help. So back to the start.

[edit 3] I disabled compiz (fusion-icon) and now things appear to run smooth. However as before said, the crashes occur randomly so no guarantee.

[edit 4] As said, no guarantee and indeed crashes also occur with Metacity. In depth post below.

[COLOR="Red"]The following is not correct. And was the original post here.[/COLOR]

[COLOR="Silver"]I can now confirm a bug with the network manager. As was suggested earlier in this thread network-manager is the reason for certain freezes of the system. In general the freezes were pretty rare but when playing halflife with wine (or cs for that matter) I could be sure to get freezes more periodically. After uninstalling network-manager and using wicd instead everything seems to be fine for now. What helped too was to disable networking, but that is not really an option. 

There is nothing written in any of the logs (/var/logs) at the specific time of the crash so I guess running network-manager with debug flags on won't help a great deal. Or what do you think? I will have some time to hunt down this bug so any help is appreciated on how to do that. Maybe we can discuss this separately to not further fill this thread. Send me a PM in case you open a new thread.

Best,
Arno


Again in short.

**If you experience more or less random system freezes while your network is enabled try the following: Uninstall network-manager and install wicd instead.**
*Freeze often occurs when playing CounterStrike or HalfLife via wine.*
[/COLOR]

### Post by KernelSpace on 2010-10-27
I use Debian as my distro and have been experiancing this freeze regularly since last year while using Debian Squeeze (AKA Testing). At first I thought it was the GPU, Driver, Firefox, Flash, VLC, Hardware etc.

However this seems to be a problem with Kernel and/or Xorg. I do not experiance this issue while running a shell in Single User Mode or using a VT (Ctrl+Alt+F1 to F6) even when running Xorg on Ctrl+Alt+F7.

I have tried all I can find mentioned workarounds and suggestions across the net but still no fix. This problem has been affecting multiple distros.

See  here for more details
[http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=583186](http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=583186)
[http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=597379](http://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=597379)

### Post by davidvandoren on 2010-10-29
I completely got rid of all freezes and bugs I had so far.

First I changed the settings for the NVIDIA N220GT Graphic card as described in one of my previous posts. 
It helped a lot but since the card is then running at maximum all the time. It seemed that after hours of running I started to get other issues. Might have been of other cause though.


Then I disabled the NVIDIA card via Bios by simply changing PCI-e not to be the primary Graphic.
I now use the on-board Graphic card.

I also put a UPS before my PC. UPS = Undisturbed Power Supply not [COLOR=Magenta]united parcel service[/COLOR]
And I haven't had a single freeze, logout, nautilus crash what's ever for a week.

I have two Ubuntus installed and the other one still has the NVIDIA drive. If needed I can just enable the graphic card via bios and I am ready to go.

---

### Post by KernelSpace on 2010-11-03
Following up from my post, so far Debian Squeeze has been running ok since the weekend and I have not been experiancing as regular and lively crashes as before.

I am investigating a bit more on whats changed in the last couple of days

But the following relevant clicks:
1. I had about 4 USB devices connected including a multi-function printer, so far I have unplugged 2 of those and only left with USB Keyboard and Mouse plugged. I can't remember seeing similar message as Unable to enumerate USB... at boot time as before.
2. Reinstalled xserver-xorg*. Pay special attention to xserver-xorg-_****_input-evdev and xserver-xorg-_****_input-all
3. Installed XFCE, seems to be working fine
4. Cleaned up the PSU/CPU fans free of all clogged dust
5. Using KDM+Gnome seems to be working fine

Kernel parameters I played around with:
1. cat /proc/mtrr and checked these settings
2. added "enable_mtrr_cleanup" and/or "nopat" to the kernel command line but later removed as it did not seem to help.

PS: While testing I was able to produce  crashes by running/clicking/resizing frantically glxgears from a terminal in X.

### Post by kevinpugh on 2010-11-03
I have the same problem - my Dell C840 Latitude work fine with 9.10 Karmic, but hangs after a period of time whether it is Lucid 10.04 or Meerkat 10.10*.* Am installing straight Ubuntu so it seems it is common to all (?) Ubuntu variants.

I rebuilt the laptop assuming that was a hardware problem (usually a good indicator when the lock up is random) but got the same result. Reverted back to Karmic and I have no problems - literally the machine can be on for weeks. 

I have tried both ext3 and ext4 and this makes no difference so what are the changes in 10.xx that could be behind this issue?

Same here in Ubuntu 10.04 but adding a reboot. I am trying Fedora 14 now: same thing. 
Then I found[ http://superuser.com/questions/169705/how-to-fix-usb-mouse-freeze-in-ubuntu-10-04-or-fedora-13]("http://superuser.com/questions/169705/how-to-fix-usb-mouse-freeze-in-ubuntu-10-04-or-fedora-13") so I added ```
echo on > /sys/bus/usb/devices/usb2/power/level
``` to /etc/rc.local 
Hopefully it has some positive effect on this annoying problem...

Did not help...

### Post by eddieray7 on 2010-11-04
Okay... this is all still a theory, but so far so good for me with the hard-lock-up situation.  I have been avoiding running VirtualBox on this system since it tends to make the lock-ups occur more frequently, but I fired it up today after having about 3 days in a row without a lock-up.  We'll see...

It appears that at some point around 2.6.31, the "cpufreq" / "cpuspeed" features (to scale CPU speed automatically based on power situation, etc.) got moved into the kernel.  Right there... it was suspicious to me because many have conjectured that this whole mess got started around .31-ish.  I noticed that I had several things installed (maybe leftover from the 8.04 to 10.04) related to cpu speed control, even tho' the kernel has it built-in now.  So, I removed everything I could find related to this feature - Google for it and there are a number of pages that explain how to track down the various packages related to cpu speed that might be hanging around.  Also, there is a Gnome tool for changing the CPU frequency plan by hand... install that.  When I change the frequency by hand using this manual speed selector, my computer locks up hard and I have to power off/on.  The fact that it will let me change it by hand and causes instant lock-up tells me that I can't allow it to try to change the speed automatically.  Also, it tells me that regardless of what the kernel thinks, this kind of speed change really isn't supported on my machine/BIOS/CPU/AGP/FSB/whatever.

A while back I noticed the following in my dmesg output but didn't give it much thought:

```
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277341] cpufreq-nforce2: Detected nForce2 chipset revision C1
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277344] cpufreq-nforce2: FSB changing is maybe unstable and can lead to crashes and data loss.
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277351] cpufreq-nforce2: FSB currently at 200 MHz, FID 11.0
```Hmmmm... sounds like someone knew that this chipset has potential issues with speed-changing.

Rhetoric: Also, in-kernel speed changing is a pretty recent thing... why would they back-test this on an Athlon XP 3200+ from 6+ years ago?  Maybe 10.04 isn't doing anything wrong... but the installer just didn't clean up old cpu speed stuff from 8.04?  Did I get all the cpu speed stuff cleaned up now manually? Not sure...

Another observation: on my 10.10 machine, there's no "hald" stuff running (still no lock-up on that machine).  On my 10.04, there IS a bunch of "hald" stuff running, and a daemon "hald-addon-cpufreq" that I haven't figured out how to get rid of.  The only page I've seen that talks about disabling it mentions some config files I don't have.  Who knows... maybe "hald" stuff is another leftover from the 8.04 to 10.04 upgrade and is not even supposed to be running at all on 10.04.  More stuff to figure out?

Right off the bat, since uninstalling all auto-cpu-speed stuff, I noticed that playing a YouTube video, which has been ramping up about 50%+ CPU lately, now uses minimal CPU (<20%).

Not sure if it's gonna lock up or not...

---

### Post by sanz on 2010-11-05
After struggling with this annoying problem form months, at last I know that I'm not the only one who is suffering.

The symptom on my Dell D620 is similar, ubuntu 10.04 totally freezes suddenly and randomly, and the only way out is long-press of power button. 
However, I've a different story here. I suspected Nvidia card, mainborad, or Compriz for months, until several days ago I noticed that the WIFI indicator often kept flashing even after "freeze". I thought this must be related to my WIFI connection. 2 days ago, I turned off my WIFI switch and plugged in a cable instead, not any more freeze has been observed so far. I'm working on my laptop everyday for at least 10 hours, only in ubuntu. I used to have around 10 times of freezes everyday.

Yet I'm still doing the test. Today I disabled network-manager, pulled off the cable, turn on WIFI switch and installed Wicd network-manager. Perhaps the culprit is "network-manager". who knows? It's been working well for hours.

If someone else can repeat and prove my test, perhaps the problem will be located.

---

### Post by nixomose on 2010-11-07
As there are 120+ pages to this thread I didn't read through it all and google didn't narrow it down for me well. I run 10.10 and I got the 
out of memory thing....

Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357179] oom_kill_process: 4 callbacks suppressed
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357182] multiload-apple invoked oom-killer: gfp_mask=0x280da, order=0, oom_adj=0
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357185] multiload-apple cpuset=/ mems_allowed=0
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357188] Pid: 4068, comm: multiload-apple Tainted: G   M    W   2.6.35-22-generic #35-Ubuntu
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357190] Call Trace:
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357198]  [<ffffffff810aeddd>] ? cpuset_print_task_mems_allowed+0x9d/0xb0
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357202]  [<ffffffff81104291>] dump_header+0x81/0xc0
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357204]  [<ffffffff81104351>] oom_kill_process+0x81/0x180
Nov  7 19:47:50 io kernel: [1122368.357207]  [<ffffffff81104888>] __out_of_memory+0x58/0xd0

But whereas most people say their machine locks up, mine didn't. It spun the drive for a while and mouse and keyboard were laggy, but when it went away, I saw the system monitor showed that something allocated a ton of memory, like 4 gig. I have 8 gig on this machine of which maybe 5 was in use at the time, so basically what it was doing was something was asking for a ton of memory and the kernel was swapping out like mad which made the machine crawl.
It did this for maybe 2 minutes, and then whatever asked for all the memory returned it, because sysmon showed it all just went away.
As the machine was unusable at the time I can't tell what it was, although I'm thinking vmware might have been a culprit and I was loading a DVD at the time, maybe that's related.

Just a heads up in case this little bit of info helps anybody.
Machine is fine now. Didn't reboot or anything.

### Post by ischg on 2010-11-08
Well. After about half a year of tethered ethernet (with disabled wireless to prevent complete system freezes) I finally have a working wireless connection. As suspected, the culprit was the wireless card (Realtek) of my Lenovo T410. 
```
lspci | grep Realtek
03:00.0 Network controller: Realtek Semiconductor Co., Ltd. Device 8172 (rev 10)

```
After kicking out the kernel driver and replacing it by Realtek's driver I have not experienced any more freezes. Here are the instructions that resolved all of my issues: [http://michaelminn.com/linux/toshiba-u505/]("http://michaelminn.com/linux/toshiba-u505/")
Btw, still running 10.04 LTS with kernel 2.6.32-25

---

The fact that it will let me change it by hand and causes instant lock-up tells me that I can't allow it to try to change the speed automatically.  Also, it tells me that regardless of what the kernel thinks, this kind of speed change really isn't supported on my machine/BIOS/CPU/AGP/FSB/whatever.

A while back I noticed the following in my dmesg output but didn't give it much thought:

```
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277341] cpufreq-nforce2: Detected nForce2 chipset revision C1
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277344] cpufreq-nforce2: FSB changing is maybe unstable and can lead to crashes and data loss.
/var/log/messages.1:Oct 27 11:58:50 toaster kernel: [    0.277351] cpufreq-nforce2: FSB currently at 200 MHz, FID 11.0
```Hmmmm... sounds like someone knew that this chipset has potential issues with speed-changing.

Rhetoric: Also, in-kernel speed changing is a pretty recent thing... why would they back-test this on an Athlon XP 3200+ from 6+ years ago?  Maybe 10.04 isn't doing anything wrong... but the installer just didn't clean up old cpu speed stuff from 8.04?  Did I get all the cpu speed stuff cleaned up now manually? Not sure...

Another observation: on my 10.10 machine, there's no "hald" stuff running (still no lock-up on that machine).  On my 10.04, there IS a bunch of "hald" stuff running, and a daemon "hald-addon-cpufreq" that I haven't figured out how to get rid of.  The only page I've seen that talks about disabling it mentions some config files I don't have.  Who knows... maybe "hald" stuff is another leftover from the 8.04 to 10.04 upgrade and is not even supposed to be running at all on 10.04.  More stuff to figure out?

Right off the bat, since uninstalling all auto-cpu-speed stuff, I noticed that playing a YouTube video, which has been ramping up about 50%+ CPU lately, now uses minimal CPU (<20%).

Not sure if it's gonna lock up or not...

My original post to this thread:
 [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=9583443#post9583443](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=9583443#post9583443)

So, for me it started on July 10. I'm not sure if that relates to the kernel version you mentioned. I'd run ubuntu FOR YEARS before that with no lock up problem. Same hardware. I tried 10.10 on a separate hard drive and the lock up problem is still there.

Here is a list of July 8th and 9th updates. I think that one of these software changes is causing the problem. I did start using some ppa's. Maybe those started the lock ups?

### Post by tk189 on 2010-11-12
I've been enduring sudden hang ups and freezes since I began using ubuntu 10.04 and since upgrading to 10.10 (not dual booted with Wincrap) when it was released. Alongside grub errors causing boot failures my ubuntu experience so far has been pretty crappy on the whole.

One thing I have recently noticed (just prior to crashing) is that as I have been editing some html or even just text files using gedit the file in question has suddenly gone read-only just prior to the freeze and crash. If there's enough time before the thing freezes up I have tried to edit other files, even just renaming them and been denied. I am guessing the read-only thing affects everything then causes stuff that needs to be executable etc to crash or stop responding/working.

launchers stop working although the cairo dock eye candy continues to pop and fizz and look great nothing starts when clicked on. The same is true of the top and bottom launcher panels... all remain clickable, all the effects work but not a single app will open, including the shut down or reboot option.

A hard reset is always required to reboot and 99% of the time the damn thing doesn't reboot, instead I get a Try passing init= bootarg error some busybox thing or other and am forced to purge and reinstall grub via liveCD.

I thought things had settled down when I upgraded to 10.10 but after 3 weeks of running fine it all starts again.

New hard drive too BTW. I am really getting fed up spending 30% to 50% of my working day Googling for ubuntu errors and fixes... not good, seriously not good.

I really like the system and very much want to remain a user of it (I despise Windows) but it's seriously unstable and I just can't trust it.

---

### Post by wwuster on 2010-11-12
I've become more aware of what seems to cause the lockup for me. It is when I'm scrolling web pages with the mouse middle button, or resizing a window with the mouse, or moving a window with the mouse. **And only if I do these things too fast**. If I am careful and do these mouse things slowly there is no lockup. Something about gnome / kernel / xorg / video driver. I have an nvidia card but am not running any proprietary driver. It seemed to lock up worse with the recommended nvidia driver. I have a core 2 duo processor, so speed shouldn't be a problem.

William

### Post by ivarn on 2010-11-12
The newest kernel (36 I think) works just fine.
You should also install gconf-cleaner to clean up in your gconf preferences.
And install bleachbit to get rid of useless files and free up your memory.
Also if you use compiz and have less than 2gb ram on your pc it could be an idea to disable the Blur effect. You should also turn down the swappiness from 40 to 10 to use more of your internal memory instead of the hdd memory since your hard drive goes slower ```
sudo sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10
```
And you can do a few things in Ubuntu Tweak to increase your ram.
Also, if you don't use a server you can turn off your firewall ```
sudo ufw disable
```

---

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-11-14
My impression is that people are reporting at least two types of freezes. One type primarily involves the mouse malfunctioning or freezing and is often temporary and recoverable. Mine presents with the screen dimming, one or two drop down menus can be opened but that's it, soon everything below the surface is frozen, the hard drive light remains lit. The only solution is a hard reset. Nothing has been recorded in the logs. I suspect an I/O issue.

Some time ago, I'd found that booting with pci=noacpi noticeably reduced my freezes but did not eliminate them. Additionally in the past couple of weeks I downgraded from using SataII to the slower Sata port and since then there have been no freezes.

Yesterday I removed the pci=noacpi from Grub2 but kept using the slower Sata port and so far, no freezes. It may be a false dawn and there'll be another freeze tomorrow. I'll report if there is.

---

### Post by nunrgguy on 2010-11-15
I'd not seen this thread so started another, add another person having random freezes to the list. Acer 1502lmi fresh install of 10.10. Have Virtual box installed but was freezing before that. Nothing really pertinent in the logs. Have tried diabling ACPI which I can't do and have the system work properly. Have tried switching off speed stepping to no avail and a few other grub changes back and forth.
Now the system doesn't completely lock up and hang but rather shuts down and reboots...progress maybe? Not sure what caused the change as I've changed so much.
Some info which MAY help - this acer used to have problems under windows due to a crappy BIOS w.r.t ACPI and used to suffer audio dropouts and system hangs. There was a windows hack which involved hacking the registry to stop acpi checking smart battery.

Anyway, have also upgraded two other boxes to 10.10 on a mythtv system so hope they aren't going to be the same? Although will have to completely rebiuld the server from scratch along with th elvm etc as the upgrade also killed mysql.

Now I remember why I had a written note saying 'NEVER UPGRADE'

DOH:guitar:

### Post by nunrgguy on 2010-11-16
Right, 
have changed to 
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="ACPI=acpi_pm quiet splash nohz=off"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="" 

in grub

and has run today without crashing or rebooting, will have to test for longer to see if this really has fixed it as ran up to 10 hours before before freezing. We shall see.

### Post by yuki-nagato on 2010-11-17
> **hoover67 said:**
> I've also had the freezes on Linux Mint, so I hope YMMV. At the moment, I'm trying LXDE on Ubuntu 10.10, only one freeze so far.

Since LinuxMint is very much based off of Ubuntu that isn't terribly surprising.  Something happened between 10.04 and 9.10 that seems to be what is causing the freezing but it doesn't look like there's much agreement as to what it is yet unfortunately...  I really wish there was... it would help prevent the freezing that I've been seeing since the upgrade every few hours in Firefox...


A few things that I've determined for sure about the freezing at least on my install:

1) It seems to be specific to Lucent and Maverick since this never happened on Karmic
2) So far it has only occurred in Firefox when visiting certain web pages with Noscript enabled.
3) Xorg is the problem.  Everything else in the system is ok, Xorg is what is freezing up and preventing any input from the peripherals.
4) It is repeatable as evidenced by the fact that if Firefox is on a website that happens to be during a freeze, revisiting said page after a reboot using the session restore feature causes another freeze 100% of the time.
5) The freeze usually happens when Firefox is working on a lot of stuff at once but this isn't a for sure cause of a freeze.
6) In addition there is another Xorg based bug that causes some images to render as a completely black rectangle at the default zoom which turns into pixelation upon zooming in or out too much.
7) The freezing does not seem to be occurring on any other browser which is very odd.

I just remembered that I had not removed any of the older kernel versions that I'd used with Karmic => *boots with different kernel*
we shall see how this turns out...

---

Did not yet tried plugging usb mouse, but plugging ps2 definetly don't work. Shortcuts don't work, altough I had a case, when keyboard worked, so I was able to reboot. Ubuntu 10.04

### Post by cottfcfan on 2010-11-18
Could this be a xorg-xserver problem?
I'm very limited technically on these things, but many have said that pclinuxos runs fine, when other Distros don't.
So I checked the difference. Xorg-xserver.
Pclinuxos still uses version 1.6.4, while other Distros are way past that version. 1.6.4 was last used in Ubuntu 9.10. The last one before the freezes started.
All the newer distros use versions 1.7-1.9 of xorg-xserver, and all appear to freeze. 
All the Distros using 1.6 and prior appear to have no problems.
Just a thought.

### Post by winggndm on 2010-11-20
Ok, I also had this issue with my system, what will be common to this issue is if you have an Athlon 64, X2, or any of the first generation AMD processors which uses cool and quite aka powernow-k8.  

The random hard lockups could only be fixed by hard resetting the system.  After a month of research and bisect I found the issue in my system.  My system was stable from 2.6.12 all the way up to 2.6.28.  When 2.6.29 was introduced, my system started to experience this same issue.  It still persists today.  After a month of bisect I found the cause of my random lockups.  It is a patch which modified the powernow-k8.

Now to test if you are affected by this issue, first disable cool and quite in the bios.  If you notice that the lockups stop then follow the bug report on bugzilla.kernel.org.

[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=21082](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=21082)

Hope this helps.

---

### Post by edward1978 on 2010-11-20
> **winggndm said:**
> Ok, I also had this issue with my system, what will be common to this issue is if you have an Athlon 64, X2, or any of the first generation AMD processors which uses cool and quite aka powernow-k8.  

The random hard lockups could only be fixed by hard resetting the system.  After a month of research and bisect I found the issue in my system.  My system was stable from 2.6.12 all the way up to 2.6.28.  When 2.6.29 was introduced, my system started to experience this same issue.  It still persists today.  After a month of bisect I found the cause of my random lockups.  It is a patch which modified the powernow-k8.

Now to test if you are affected by this issue, first disable cool and quite in the bios.  If you notice that the lockups stop then follow the bug report on bugzilla.kernel.org.

[https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=21082](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=21082)

Hope this helps.
