---

### Post by winggndm on 2010-11-20
Yes, only AMD Processors with Cool and Quiet can be affected.  This will most likely only affect the first gen k8 cool and quiet processors where the motherboard vendor did not put in the correct values for the transition latency in the _PSS table.

### Post by edward1978 on 2010-11-21
Should I stop the ubuntuone process or unistall? What does UO do anyway?? I have seen another freeze thread marked solved & had this. For some reason, I don't trust it.

             Add this to your /etc/rc.local file (sudo gedit /etc/rc.local)
Code:
mkdir -p /dev/cgroup/cpu
mount -t cgroup cgroup /dev/cgroup/cpu -o cpu
mkdir -m 0777 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user
echo "/usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/release_agent

and make it executable
Code:
sudo chmod +x /etc/rc.local

And then add the following to your ~/.bashrc file (to open it: gedit ~/.bashrc):
Code:
if [ "$PS1" ] ; then
mkdir -p -m 0700 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$ > /dev/null 2>&1
echo $$ > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/tasks
echo "1" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/notify_on_release
fi

Run the following command:
Code:
sudo gedit /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And paste this:
Code:
#!/bin/sh
rmdir /dev/cgroup/cpu/$*

then save the file and make it executable:
Code:
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And finally, restart the computer or manually run the /etc/rc.local file ("sudo /etc/rc.local").

### Post by edward1978 on 2010-11-22
Well I have tried 

Add this to your /etc/rc.local file (sudo gedit /etc/rc.local)
Code:
mkdir -p /dev/cgroup/cpu
mount -t cgroup cgroup /dev/cgroup/cpu -o cpu
mkdir -m 0777 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user
echo "/usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/release_agent

and make it executable
Code:
sudo chmod +x /etc/rc.local

And then add the following to your ~/.bashrc file (to open it: gedit ~/.bashrc):
Code:
if [ "$PS1" ] ; then
mkdir -p -m 0700 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$ > /dev/null 2>&1
echo $$ > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/tasks
echo "1" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/notify_on_release
fi

Run the following command:
Code:
sudo gedit /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And paste this:
Code:
#!/bin/sh
rmdir /dev/cgroup/cpu/$*

then save the file and make it executable:
Code:
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And finally, restart the computer or manually run the /etc/rc.local file ("sudo /etc/rc.local").

hope it fixes it, no freezes yet, but I did this yesterday so to soon to tell really. That does the same as the new unreleased kernel patch, hope I did it right.

### Post by cd-r80 on 2010-11-22
Terminal. I used nano, not gedit.


zeus@lightning:~$ sudo nano /etc/rc.local
[sudo] password for zeus: 
zeus@lightning:~$ sudo chmod +x /etc/rc.local
zeus@lightning:~$ nano ~/.bashrc
zeus@lightning:~$ sudo nano /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean
zeus@lightning:~$ sudo chmod +x /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean
zeus@lightning:~$ sudo /etc/rc.local

End of /home/zeus/.bashrc was:


if [ "$PS1" ] ; then  
   mkdir -p -m 0700 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$ > /dev/null 2>&1
   echo $$ > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/tasks
   echo "1" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/notify_on_release
fi


/etc/rc.local

#!/bin/sh -e
#
# rc.local
#
# This script is executed at the end of each multiuser runlevel.
# Make sure that the script will "exit 0" on success or any other
# value on error.
#
# In order to enable or disable this script just change the execution
# bits.
#
# By default this script does nothing.
mkdir -p /dev/cgroup/cpu
mount -t cgroup cgroup /dev/cgroup/cpu -o cpu
mkdir -m 0777 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user
echo "/usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/release_agent
exit 0

/usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

#!/bin/sh
rmdir /dev/cgroup/cpu/$*

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-11-22
> **bgiannes said:**
> sorry i don't understand, can you explain

I made up a new Desktop PC with a spare Asrock Dual-Sata motherboard with the intention of replacing my wife's old PC. It was a fresh install on 13th August with 10.04 (then subsequently upgraded to 10.10). Throughout the first few weeks I experienced frequent freezes. Sometimes almost immediately after login, sometimes after a an hour. Hardly a day went by without a freeze and the need for a hard re-set.

From the Forum it looked like it was perhaps a ACPI / power saving issue which was being suspected. I found that if I added pci=noacpi to the boot line in Grub2 my freezes were far fewer but still occasionally happened. If I booted with the noacpi option my PC wouldn't switch off properly.

During various freezes I'd see my hard drive LED lit and see some messages suggesting an I/O problem. Whilst I suspected my hard drive might have been faulty, I found that by connecting my sata hard drive to the slower Sata port on the motherboard I didn't have any more freezes.

About 10 days ago from memory, I removed the pci=noacpi from Grub2 but to my surprise, the freezes didn't return. Today I returned the PC to it's original configuration and plugged the sata hard drive into the faster Sata11 port. Hey presto I get a freeze within about an hour.

I've experienced no freezes at all with my own PC, that's an Asrock Dual-Vsta with a slow IDE hard drive and ethernet not wifi. It's not a fresh install, has Grub and ext3.

Edit: With this last freeze the led showing hard drive being accessed may not have been lit, typically during a freezee the red led light would be on. I was able to move the mouse and open a couple of windows but I got an error messages when attempting to look at the log files or open a terminal. If I'd not rebooted I expect that soon the mouse would have stopped working too.
I'm not applying any patch until enough people report this as a solution.

---

### Post by tepr on 2010-11-23
Another report for you... I bought a new computer last week and installed 10.10 (64 bit) on it, and have been experiencing freezes right from day one.

Machine is a i5 760, 4 GB RAM, Nvidia GT 210.

The only software I'm running that's proprietary/not in the repos/otherwise unusual is Dropbox, Spotify (via Wine) and the Nvidia graphics drivers.

The timing between the freezes is very variable; the soonest was after about 25 minutes, the longest (and most recent) after over two days of uptime.

I'm inclined to suspect it's not a hardware problem: the processor is running at about 40 C under load (think the highest I've seen is 42), memtest86+ passes with no problems, and I've got plenty of memory so I doubt it's running out (enough that I've never seen it use my swap partition).

Whenever the machine freezes, the keyboard and mouse stop responding, the screen stops updating, and the audio output basically repeats a short amount (I assume it's the contents of some buffer) over and over until I do a hard reset. A very large amount of noise suddenly appears on the left audio channel when this happens. I've also tried connecting via ssh from another box without any success, so I'm inclined to suspect a kernel-level problem (and this thread so far would appear to bear that out).

I can't see any connection between the freezes - in none of the cases was I trying to do anything unusual at the time (ie, nothing that I don't do regularly without the machine freezing), although in each case I was actively using the machine. When I've been away from the machine it always seems to have been fine (I'm assuming that it doesn't eventually recover from its freezes - unlikely if it's a kernel problem).

Nothing appears in any of the log files at the time of any of the freezes. (But if it's a kernel issue is it possible that it tries to write to a log but it doesn't appear on disk because the file buffers don't get flushed?)

This is a showstopper for me, as the machine is intended to eventually host email and therefore will need to be up at all times.

I also have an older machine (a laptop) with a completely different hardware configuration (Core Duo processor, 2 GB RAM, ATI graphics of some sort), on which I've recently installed 10.10 32-bit and haven't had any problems with it, though admittedly it doesn't see as much use as my main computer.

I've tried the following measures to attempt to fix the problem:
- updating kernel to 2.6.36-020636-generic from the kernel PPA
- updating graphics drivers to the latest version (260.19.21) from the X Updates PPA
- removing Ubuntu One

(and I think there might be something else I've missed but can't remember right now...)

I will confess to not having read the entire thread (just the oldest and newest ten pages or so), so if there's anything worth trying that I've missed please point me to it! Thanks!

---

### Post by mgmechanics on 2010-11-23
> **edward1978 said:**
> I have 4 G of ram, so how big should swap be?

If you run a 32 bit operating system, (Windows 32 bit, Ubuntu 32 bit ...) your operating system is not able to use more than about 3.5 GB RAM. With 4 GB RAM you doesn't need a swap space. 

You need swap space to compensate having not enough RAM. I.E. you may have 1 GB RAM but you need 1.5 GB for some application then you need 0.5 GB swap.

Earlier i run my Ubuntu 32 bit without swap (but virtual machines consuming up to 1 GB RAM).

### Post by oraslaci on 2010-11-24
> **edward1978 said:**
> I have 10.04, so I think it is 2.6.32-26 with yesterdays update, but it is the generic-pae version. What is SATA11, I read some posts saying that caused freezes & going to normal SATA worked for some. I don't think I have it SATA11 is not in my bios.

SATA is a not so old interface for hard disks. It's fast but 
Windoz XP didn't support it by default,
you needed a driver slipstreamed into the xp setup disks, otherwise the installer would not see the HDD.

Probably this is why some manufacturers decided to put a switch into the BIOS to disable this feature. Since XP is quite oldfashioned by know, maybe they decided not to include this option anymore.

Just a guess.

---

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-11-24
edward1978, I have two similar motherboards here-

Freezes: Asrock Dual-Sata2 motherboard circa 2005 has three serial ports, the manual says-
Serial ATA: Supports up to 2 SATA devices at 1.5Gb/s data transfer rate
Serial ATA II: 1 SATA II device at 3Gb/s data transfer rate
I boot 10.10 from a SATA drive, either it, this motherboard or Ubuntu 10.10 doesn't like being connected to the faster SATA II port. It freezes.
I use Asrock's term for SATA II rather than calling it SATA 2 to distinguish between the two slower motherboard SATA ports which are named 1 and 2. The memory and CPU both worked without any hitch previously in other PCs. The motherboard was one I bought new some time ago.

No freezes: Asrock Dual-VSTA motherboard circa 2006 has three serial ports but also supports "Hot Plug" functions which I have not used. I boot 10.10 from an IDE drive and there have been no freezes. Incidentally I have an accessory AM2 CPU board fitted. 

If installing 2000/XP/XP 64 you were required to first make a SATA driver diskette before installing one of these systems but not apparently if in non-Raid mode neither if you chose to operate SATA II in IDE mode. (I will not be answering questions to explain this, as I've not needed this myself).

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-11-25
**[SIZE="3"]Success![/SIZE]**

**For my version of this problem:**
> My version ONLY freezes the mouse pointer and then stops responding to any subsequent USB mice randomly after I plug them in, also in a few cases, after having 3 mice stop responding in succession, the USB KB also stopped responding. In all cases the System is still stable and responds to remote axx. I NEVER experience high CPU usage, Video driver corruption problems or app or X crashes with this version of the freeze bug
**Note: using the 2.6.31-11-rt kernel eliminates the freeze.**

On my Lucid X86_64 install with the 2.6.32-25 Kernel, adding ```
acpi=force irqpoll
``` stops the freeze problem (3 days uptime so far). 

After another couple of days of uptime to be sure, I will try ```
acpi=force irqpoll
``` on my 10.10 x86_64 install and report on the result.

My Hardware is the MSI K9N2GM-FIH in my sig with the latest v.3.8 BIOS. My version of the freeze seems to be USB related. Follow my posts in the bug report [Here](https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all)

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-11-25
@nunrgguy, As stated in this thread and else ware, **there are several different types of freezing problems (and causes)**. In my case sometimes the mouse pointer will freeze right after booting, but more often it will go for a few hours to a day or two before it freezes. So to be sure in my case I must keep the computer booted for several days just to be sure (Although I do believe my problem is cured) My next step is to upgrade to the newer kernel that has been released since I started this test "2.6.32-26"

@edward1978, I am also using 10.04 with the next to newest kernel "2.6.32-25" as I stated above "2.6.32-26" came available since I started testing these kernel options, and I will test that kernel next. My version of the freeze bug also affects my 10.10 install so I will also be trying the option combination on it.

As noted [Here](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BootOptions#Common%20Boot%20Options) 

**acpi=force** Activates the ACPI system even if your computer BIOS date is older than 2000. This parameter overrides acpi=off and can also be used with current hardware if the ACPI support is not activated despite apm=off. (Even though my MB and BIOS are not nearly that old, the combo of this and irqpoll fixes this error for me)

**irqpoll** Reduces driver initialization failures due to shared interrupts.

**I had previously tried both of these options separately and they did not work, however using them together (And I believe in this order) DOES work on my computer / version of the freeze bug, which I believe is related to my particular USB bus, Nvidia Southbridge Chipset and Logitech wireless KB and Mouse combination**, as two other computers in my home do not suffer from this freeze problem (See my sig)

**Again I must stress, one of the reasons that this bug is so hard to diagnose is that there seem to be several different causes for freezing in Linux that seem similar**, Please read my posts in launchpad [Here](https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all) for more info on MY version. **If your symptoms are different, this fix probably will not work for you.**

Good luck all, and happy Thanksgiving!

### Post by ivarn on 2010-11-26
[SIZE=1]> **mbreman said:**
> Does anyone know what the status on this issue is right now with the developers. I mean are people working on it and is progress being made?

Thanks,[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]

It has been fixed but I am not sure if the new kernel has the fix or not since I installed the patch 2 days before the new kernel came. I think it came yesterday or something.

If you have already installed the new kernel but everything is still jerky, here's the fix.

[/SIZE][SIZE=1]Add this to your /etc/rc.local file (sudo gedit /etc/rc.local)
Code:
mkdir -p /dev/cgroup/cpu
mount -t cgroup cgroup /dev/cgroup/cpu -o cpu
mkdir -m 0777 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user
echo "/usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/release_agent

and make it executable[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
Code:
sudo chmod +x /etc/rc.local

And then add the following to your ~/.bashrc file (to open it: gedit ~/.bashrc):[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
Code:
if [ "$PS1" ] ; then  
   mkdir -p -m 0700 /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$ > /dev/null 2>&1
   echo $$ > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/tasks
   echo "1" > /dev/cgroup/cpu/user/$$/notify_on_release
fi

Run the following command:[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
Code:
sudo gedit /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And paste this:[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
Code:
#!/bin/sh
rmdir /dev/cgroup/cpu/$*

then save the file and make it executable:[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
Code:
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/sbin/cgroup_clean

And finally, restart the computer or manually run the /etc/rc.local file ("sudo /etc/rc.local").[/SIZE] [SIZE=1]
[/SIZE] 
[SIZE=1]
[/SIZE][SIZE=1]
[/SIZE]

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-11-27
**OK, I can now say with certainty** ```
acpi=force irqpoll
``` **has cured my version of the pointer freeze bug on my 10.04 install (Kernel 2.6.32-25)** I have not tried the -26 Kernel yet, but I have had it up for over 3 days and have restarted it several times today, while trying everything I could to get it to freeze (Play MP3's while hot-plugging a USB thumb drive and copying a 4+GB file to it), either of those seemed to trigger a freeze in the past.

I'm now using my 10.10 install with the new 2.6.35-22 kernel, playing a MP3 in the background and typing this in FF. Uptime is 9 hours so far including the kernel upgrade and subsequent reboot.

The MP3 reference pertains to my prior thought that pulse audio had something to do with the freeze, for a complete explanation of this thought and the fact that I had completely removed pulse audio is documented in my posts (Last 3 posts) in the launchpad bug report [Here](https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all), Short version...Removing Pulse Audio did NOT help.

**My version of the freeze bug is explained in post #1361 in this thread, and more detail on my troubleshooting steps are also in the above mentioned launchpad thread.** [COLOR="Blue"]It may help if any new posters to this thread explain their **specific symptoms** and system specs.[/COLOR]

[B]So bottom line for MY freeze version:
In 10.04- The real-time Kernel "2.6.31-11rt" works perfect,  = > 2.6.32-xx freezes, adding[/B] ```
acpi=force irqpoll
``` [B]to these Kernels fixes the freeze, Kernel 2.6.32-26 is untested by me at this point (I'll get to it next).

In 10.10- Any Kernel prior to the newest 2.6.35-22 freezes, and I am now into testing that Kernel without any Kernel Options.[/B]

> acpi=force irqpoll

### Post by hoover67 on 2010-11-27
you can add those options (as root) to /etc/default/grub, then run "update-grub" (also as root). 

Cheers, Uwe

### Post by hoover67 on 2010-11-27
here's a copy of mine (Linux Mint 10, based on Ubuntu 10.10), I hope it helps: 


# If you change this file, run 'update-grub' afterwards to update
# /boot/grub/grub.cfg.

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
#GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=true
GRUB_TIMEOUT=10
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="acpi=force irqpoll"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""

# Uncomment to enable BadRAM filtering, modify to suit your needs
# This works with Linux (no patch required) and with any kernel that obtains
# the memory map information from GRUB (GNU Mach, kernel of FreeBSD ...)
#GRUB_BADRAM="0x01234567,0xfefefefe,0x89abcdef,0xefefefef"

# Uncomment to disable graphical terminal (grub-pc only)
#GRUB_TERMINAL=console

# The resolution used on graphical terminal
# note that you can use only modes which your graphic card supports via VBE
# you can see them in real GRUB with the command `vbeinfo'
#GRUB_GFXMODE=640x480

# Uncomment if you don't want GRUB to pass "root=UUID=xxx" parameter to Linux
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true

# Uncomment to disable generation of recovery mode menu entries
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_RECOVERY="true"

# Uncomment to get a beep at grub start
#GRUB_INIT_TUNE="480 440 1"

Note: this will also show plenty of ugly boot messages and remove the splash screen.

---

### Post by edward1978 on 2010-11-27
# If you change this file, run 'update-grub' afterwards to update
# /boot/grub/grub.cfg.

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
#GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=true
GRUB_TIMEOUT=10
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="ipv6.disable=1 acpi=force irqpoll"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""

# Uncomment to disable graphical terminal (grub-pc only)
#GRUB_TERMINAL=console

# The resolution used on graphical terminal
# note that you can use only modes which your graphic card supports via VBE
# you can see them in real GRUB with the command `vbeinfo'
#GRUB_GFXMODE=640x480

# Uncomment if you don't want GRUB to pass "root=UUID=xxx" parameter to Linux
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_UUID=true

# Uncomment to disable generation of recovery mode menu entries
#GRUB_DISABLE_LINUX_RECOVERY="true"

# Uncomment to get a beep at grub start
#GRUB_INIT_TUNE="480 440 1"

Is it right?

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-11-27
> **rodkerbs said:**
> when I run

# sudo gedit /boot/grub/grub.cfg

I get a file that does not have any of the lines mentioned in this post. I think I am running grub2 under ubuntu 10.10. I still have the same freezing that I had in 10.04 before updating. I think that this IRQ fix would really help my in 10.10 but I don't know how to implement it with grub2. Please advise!

The file you need to modify is **/etc/default/grub** so do:
```
gksu gedit /etc/default/grub
```, add **acpi=force irqpoll** then save and do: ```
sudo update-grub
``` then reboot.

**Update: I've been using my 10.10 install with the latest 2.6.35-22 (2.6.35-22-generic #35-Ubuntu SMP Sat Oct 16 20:45:36 UTC 2010 x86_64 GNU/Linux) for 27 hours now including 3 reboots (Just to make sure), WITHOUT the kernel options and no freeze yet.** 

@hoover67, On my 10.04 install WITH ```
acpi=force irqpoll
``` I get no bad boot messages nor loose my splash screen. Can you post your boot messages please?

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-11-28
> **Braged said:**
> Thanks, put that into the grub 2... let you know how it goes... and edit this in a sec to make sure it's all good.

(edit) - Reboot went good

This is how I inserted the code above....

GRUB_DEFAULT=0
#GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT=0
GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT_QUIET=true
GRUB_TIMEOUT=10
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR=`lsb_release -i -s 2> /dev/null || echo Debian`

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""

Btw, with this latest install, (knock on wood) I've not had a lock up... but figured for kicks I'd slap that in there because I'm sure it would have come sooner or later.

This is how the grub file needs to look (With only one GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT line, not 2 as you had), then you must do ```
sudo update-grub
``` in a terminal, then reboot.

If that does not work, please describe the type of lockup that you have. This fix is for when the mouse pointer freezes but you can still fully use your system with the KB (At least for a while). If your whole system locks up and you must reset the computer by powering off, this fix may not work for you.

### Post by madurax86 on 2010-11-28
I've had this problem with my Asus k42jc notebook, I kept on upgrading the kernel and at 2.6.36 stable it just went ok for like a whole week or so _NO_freezes_ then I kind of upgraded the intel libdrm and a few libs from xorg-edgers ppa(don't blame me :P I have this thing towards being on the edge) Well for the first time after about a week I got a new kind of freeze,

[drm:i915_hangcheck_elapsed] *ERROR* Hangcheck timer elapsed... GPU idle, missed IRQ.

THAT! And I see the kernel is active since it writes stuff to the screen, BUT it never responded to my request to reboot(SysRq+Alt+B) the funny thing is that xorg-edgers have updated the libs again on 27th nov I had the libs from 22nd nov when it froze now I've updated the system and waiting for the freeze :P if it freezes I'd just go back to ubuntu-updates version of the libs.

Hope you get some help from what I've posted :)
PS: irqpoll in the kernel options makes the xserver hang up soon after compiz loads(most of the time, sometimes it doesn't)

### Post by davidvandoren on 2010-11-28
Hi 

I had these freezes with ubuntu 10.10 and 10.04

After disabling my NVIDIA pci-e video-card and using the on-board video-card all my freezes had disappeared. That's over a month ago.
I think it has to do with PCI-E 
Ubuntu 10.10 which is on a 1TB sata drive has some application snap and things like that. 
Ubuntu 10.04 which is on old Hard-drive never ever crashes or closes application.
Dual monitor, wacom tablet, wbcam, compiz, everything works.

### Post by ttguy on 2010-11-29
Of course with thread title like Random Freeze/Hangup we are going to get a bunch of disparate issues all lumped together. 

And I too have recently been having Random Freezes. And so I tried a lot of things including going way back to a Jaunty install. Nothing fixed my freezes. And then I read a suggestion indicating that the fedora distro was good with respect to random freezes. So I built myself a Fedora Live USB stick. And played with it. But it too random freezed on me. But then I noticed this Live USB has a memtest on it. memtest86. So I decide to run that. And lo and behold I get massive failures of my memory reported by memtest86. 

So I pull one of my memory sticks out of the motherboard and run the memtest again - and now - no errors. So right now I am running on 1G of ram instead of 2. But I would appear (for the last hour at least) that my random freeze and continual firefox crashing is over.

So bottom line - one other thing to check is -  run a memory test on the PC if you are getting the random freeze.

---

### Post by Braged on 2010-11-29
Edward just built the 37 kernel...
Thought I'd make a few things more clear.


First it wasn't mkinitrd it was in fact it was... 
update-initramfs -k 2.6.37-rc3 -c  (the newest version I decided to get from [http://kernel.org/](http://kernel.org/))
and then you run update-grub when that is finished.

Also, when you reboot it seems to break the xserver so you have to goto /etc/X11 and move or delete the xorg.conf file. Then startx and I had to uninstall/reinstall the Nvidia driver. I'm sure someone here has a better method of doing that but that's what I did lol.

Bit of a pain in the butt... but oh well something to do :P

Also learned that make -j4 would use all 4 processors yay speed.

Another decent link: [http://www.linuxtopia.org/online_books/linux_kernel/kernel_configuration/index.html](http://www.linuxtopia.org/online_books/linux_kernel/kernel_configuration/index.html)


(edit)

So far so good.
$ uptime 14:23:31 up  2:10,  2 users,  load average: 0.73, 0.59, 0.55

/knock on wood.
2.6.37-rc3 kernel adjusted for core 2 and took out some of the stuff I didn't need.
Also using the crack groups most up to date downloads.

---

### Post by Kir_B on 2010-11-29
> **McLowry said:**
> On my 64bit system, everything had been fine up to kernel 2.6.32-23.

With 2.6.32-24, the system started to freeze several times a day.
The 2.6.32-25 update did not change this behaviour.

Going back to 2.6.32-23 made my system stable again. No worries anymore.


I hope that I don't report success too early, but here we go: Since I updated to 2.6.32-26, it looks like the problems which caused the freezes with the -24 and the -25 kernel have been fixed (or reverted -- the changelog of 2.6.32-26 looks interesting).


I'll report it here if the freezes come back. But currently i am happy with Lucid again.

Okay, now I just have to weigh in on this discussion.

My main hard drive (250GB) has been unable to boot for at least three months now. That HD contains my original install on it, which was updated regularly and upgraded from 9.04 to 9.10 and then to 10.04. During it's 10.04 lifetime, it was regularly updated via synaptic, during which, I cringed every time I saw a Kernel update (mostly due to issues involving grub2 in the past). About 4 months in, I rebooted my system, only to discover that none of the kernels would boot to the log in screen. Selecting any of the recovery modes would result in a black screen!

I'm currently running from my back up of that system, which was performed about two months after the upgrade. I've since uninstalled the back up HD from the USB case and installed it into the PC's case. I have only allowed security updates and that's it! It's run flawlessly for the past few months and I'll continue to run it this way until I see some progress on the current bugs.

Now, the reason for this post: I couldn't help but notice the discussion of different Kernel versions. And especially, I noticed that the working version of my system is using Kernel: "_**Linux 2.6.32-22 Generic**_" (according to my conky display on my desktop)

I'm not sure if that is helpful or not, but there it is, for what it's worth.

Good luck all. Kirby

### Post by antoport on 2010-11-30
I'm on Ubuntu 10.10 and had no freezes while I was on 10.04.
I updated to Maverick last week and few minutes after login the system started to freeze, the only thing that could be done was hard reset / shutdown.
After changing from ATI to open source drivers, I experienced no shutdowns but screen would dim and system returns to login screen.
I could continue to work on my laptop with no problems if there was no program opened that required Internet connection. But as soon as I started firefox (or Chrome), the system would freeze.
Sometimes if I removed / reinstaled broadcom STA driver, system would act normally, but after restart, the problems would reappear...

The solution for me was to move to new kernel (2.6.36.1) via kernelcheck ([kcheck.sourceforge.net]("http://kcheck.sourceforge.net")) - this went pretty smooth, and after reinstalling drivers for both GPU and WiFi problems seem to have stopped.

I have Dell Studio 1558 and had problems with wifi kernel modules before, so I suppose, in my case they were causing the troubles..

### Post by Ophidion on 2010-12-01
A long thread... I had the same problem you experienced. I made aplan to know what is the reason. I formatted and reinstalled ubuntu 10.04 LTS for more than 15 times within the last month to come out that it is nvidea driver that freezes my computer (both the one on nvidia site: NVIDIA-Linux-x86-260.19.21.run and the one in the repository: nvidia-current). Freezes never happened before installing nvidia drivers. it only freezes after nvidia driver installation either on:

[LIST]
[*] * multimedia playing.
[*] * USB procedures like plugging unplugging USB disks.
[*] * screensaver.
[*] * Internet surfing by any means (opera, firefox, download managers... etc).
[*] * heavy tasking.
[*] * prolonged processor usage.
[*] * some times colors turned to gray then back again before complete freezing occurs.
[*]* Alt+PrtSc + REISUB, never worked.
[/LIST]
 **I really defined some events that caused a freeze, then,**

[LIST=1]
[*]      I simulated an event without the driver being installed, of course no freeze happened.
[*]      I simulated the same event again after installing the driver, then I have that stupid freeze.
[/LIST]
    I suggest we must start a thread here and in nvidia site against nvidia graphics cards, until they are going to provide us with a stable driver. What a shame!:evil:

---

### Post by CMaziero on 2010-12-01
> **Ophidion said:**
> 
...
I suggest we must start a thread here and in nvidia site against nvidia graphics cards, until they are going to provide us with a stable driver. What a shame!:evil:

Hi Ophidion, please don't go so fast...

I'm having the same kind of problems in my laptop, but it does not have NVidia hardware at all. My graphics engine is an Intel 945GM, using an i915 driver. According to the log files, the problem seems to reside somewhere between the Xorg server and the kernel itself, and it does not seem to be driver-specific.

IMHO, people blaming some specific driver, or pulseaudio, or wifi, or compiz, or whatever, are just attacking side-effects and not the root cause.

Regards,

```Such log messages are being produced about each 120 seconds. I also noticed that the USB subsystem (for my external USB mouse and keyboard) is being continuously restarted in-between. 

```
[25600.454580] usb 4-2.3: USB disconnect, address 16
[25601.919603] usb 4-2.3: new low speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 17
[25602.061776] usb 4-2.3: configuration #1 chosen from 1 choice
[25602.088135] input: Microsoft  Microsoft Basic Optical Mouse v2.0  as /devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:1d.2/usb4/4-2/4-2.3/4-2.3:1.0/input/input22
[25602.089396] generic-usb 0003:045E:00CB.000D: input,hidraw0: USB HID v1.11 Mouse [Microsoft  Microsoft Basic Optical Mouse v2.0 ] on usb-0000:00:1d.2-2.3/input0
[25650.119211] usb 4-2.3: USB disconnect, address 17
[25651.591238] usb 4-2.3: new low speed USB device using uhci_hcd and address 18
[25651.737424] usb 4-2.3: configuration #1 chosen from 1 choice
[25651.763803] input: Microsoft  Microsoft Basic Optical Mouse v2.0  as /devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:1d.2/usb4/4-2/4-2.3/4-2.3:1.0/input/input23
[25651.763989] generic-usb 0003:045E:00CB.000E: input,hidraw0: USB HID v1.11 Mouse [Microsoft  Microsoft Basic Optical Mouse v2.0 ] on usb-0000:00:1d.2-2.3/input0
```According to the logs, it seems that the graphics driver is not being scheduled anymore, leading to a freeze. This does not seem to be driver-specific, but something in the real-time scheduling code of the kernel itself.

My system is an Acer 6291 laptop, core2duo processor, Intel 945GM graphics engine, running Ubuntu Lucid 32 bits with the default kernel (2.6.32-something). Since the freezes I moved back to the 2.6.31-11-RT (real-time) kernel and my freezes have (almost) gone since then.

Hope this may help someone...

### Post by pok1yoyo on 2010-12-01
Hello again!
I have made some progress and solved my problem, and I would like to share it with you. Perhaps we don't have the same problems, but it might help someone.
**My issues: **My Ubuntu Lucid would randomly freeze (tried few kernels, happens to all) and no other option than hard reboot (again, RSEIB wouldn't work, not even CAPS led would respond :P)
**Solution:** After purging over ALSA, OSS, nearly anything that I could think of, including my radeon driver and reinstalling ubuntu-desktop (it seems that lucid don't work over that), I made some progress by executing ***metacity --replace**. *I run into it somewhere on forums and tried it. It stopped Ubuntu from crashing, however, I couldn't see the title bar and I was only able to use the top window (unless I alt-tabbed to other). Alt+F2 had some problems too, it hanged on my password field and that was it, so it wasn't very useful. After few days of work, I think I managed to find solution:
First thing that I did, was to disable metacity compositing (appearently it was interfering with compiz)
*$ gconftool-2 -s '/apps/metacity/general/compositing_manager' --type bool false*
([http://tombuntu.com/index.php/2008/03/31/enable-metacity-compositing-in-gnome-222](http://tombuntu.com/index.php/2008/03/31/enable-metacity-compositing-in-gnome-222))
That and gdm restart made my graphic ok again (I was able to see the menu bar etc...) but it still has frozen sometimes. I tried to get rid of metacity, but that made my graphic look bad again. I tried with default theme and it was ok, but still didn't do. So I tried to get rid of compiz and run only metacity (with and without compositing), but that didn't work either.
    So what I did was, I made xorg.conf (recovery/root shell $X -configure) and after that, I did **compiz --replace.** It seems to be running flawlessly for now (I also had problems with metacity showing true transparency instead of pseudo on my terminal).
That's it. I hope it helps someone.

Long live Open Source :P

---

### Post by Ophidion on 2010-12-01
Hi [U][CMaziero,]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1197932")
   [/U]You just asked me not to go fast. you may be right. Now I have been running my laptop for 3 days without a bit of a hang because I didn't install the graphics card driver. Since you have the same problem. we must find out if it happens to all of us after installing any proprietary graphics driver. Then we can tell it is a bug in ubuntu with proprietary graphics drivers or a bug in all proprietary graphics driver dealing with ubuntu?
   Note: I have another computer which is running ubuntu since 2 months. I didn't install any proprietary graphics driver to it and it never had a freeze at all.
   Another thing which is vague to me, why my cpu frequently and suddenly running so high (80% - 100%) only after I install my proprietary graphics driver and this didn't happen at all through out the past three days without it?

This might give someone here a clue about it
Once I have a 100% cpu usage of no reason, I ran the system monitor, it showed no specific process that high!! I tried to open a terminal, toke me a 30 sec to see it. I pressed Ctr+Alt+F2 --> tty. wrote top.
I found all my open application are consuming my cpu like: -firefox-bin --> 50%, geany -->30%, I don't remember exactly the numbers but all open applications were participating in the 100% consumption.
Another time of the 100% cpu consumption shared be all open applications, unplugging my USB modem solved the problem but for only 10 minutes then it froze!

** for me : Only after installing my proprietary graphics driver either current in the repository or the one on the site, I have these multiple freezes.

every one can tell if he is so or not, plz. so we can have a clue to start with.

### Post by Braged on 2010-12-01
> **edward1978 said:**
> sudo install NVIDIA-Linux-x86-260.19.21.run
install: missing destination file operand after `NVIDIA-Linux-x86-260.19.21.run'
Try `install --help' for more informatio?? Can I just run it through the GUI as root, like in synaptic?

uh, sh instead of install.

Also, are you closing down the X-server and installing from tty1?
Cntr+alt+F1

Keep in mind you have to run the command  sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop to stop x before installing.

It's pretty easy after that point.

GL

### Post by freak.show on 2010-12-01
had the same problem, except freezing was permanent. I solved it for myself. don't know if it will help for others but you can give it a try
right click on desktop didn't work, couldn't change wallpaper.
(I have lenovo g560, intel hd graphics card, Ubuntu 10.04)

found post which suggested to reboot. did that, new wallpaper was on the desktop but right click still didn't work. and again - couldn't change wallpaper. one of the posts said that there was problems with icons on desktop, too. I don't use icons on my desktop, so I did install ubuntu tweak before and at Desktop Icon Settings took out unchecked Show desktop icons. so now I thought that I should try to get some icons on desktop just to check if on my desktop it shows any icons. I checked the Show desktop icons and under that checked Show "Computer" icon on desktop. I didn't believe in what I saw - computer icon was on the desktop, right click works, can change wallpaper. 

so, basically what you have to do is:
- install Ubuntu Tweak ([http://www.ubuntugeek.com/how-to-install-ubuntu-tweak-in-ubuntu-10-04lucid-lynx.html](http://www.ubuntugeek.com/how-to-install-ubuntu-tweak-in-ubuntu-10-04lucid-lynx.html))
- go to Desktop Icons Settings on the left and check Show desktop icons. If you want icons on your desktop then check which ones do you want, if you don't want then uncheck everything, except Show desktop icons

I'm not a pro ubuntu user and don't know if this will help but it solved the problem for me, so I suggest to give it a try

### Post by mbreman on 2010-12-02
Yes I think you have to reinstall the NVIDIA driver after a kernel update. That is if you reboot after the kernel upgrade and X won't start, reinstalling the driver solves it for me (on ubuntu 10.04)

I think technically you just need the kernel HEADERS (if the NVIDIA binary is build against a different kernel), but the installer will prompt you if that's the case.

### Post by CMaziero on 2010-12-02
> **Ophidion said:**
> Hi [U][CMaziero,]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1197932")
   [/U]You just asked me not to go fast. you may be right. Now I have been running my laptop for 3 days without a bit of a hang because I didn't install the graphics card driver. Since you have the same problem. we must find out if it happens to all of us after installing any proprietary graphics driver. Then we can tell it is a bug in ubuntu with proprietary graphics drivers or a bug in all proprietary graphics driver dealing with ubuntu?
   ...


Hi Ophidion,

I never used nor installed any proprietary graphics/network/whatever driver in my system. I'm using the standard package "xserver-xorg-video-intel" provided by the Ubuntu official repos, which provides the i915 driver for the Intel 945GM chip. The freezes I experienced were similar to other reported here: just the mouse and graphical interface & mouse freeze, but the system can be reached through SSH. No abnormal CPU usage or other symptoms, just the i915 kernel thread (the graphics driver) and the Xorg process both appear with a D status ("uninterruptible sleep") in the "top" list, meaning that they are waiting in a kernel request. Unplugging/re-plugging the external USB devices (as some people suggested here) does nothing.

I tried disabling services like wifi, Compiz, PulseAudio, and other subsystems, but  such measures only reduced the frequency of freezes and did not solve them. I also tried the most recent Ubuntu kernels like 2.6.32-25.45 with no success. The only effective solution for me was to downgrade my kernel back to 2.6.31-11-rt, which is available through Synaptic. I had no more freezes since then. 

Now I'll investigate the kernel changes between 2.6.31 and 2.6.32 related to (real-time) scheduling. Sadly I cannot participate in your list because my free time now is quite exiguous...

Regards!

---

### Post by KernelSpace on 2010-12-06
I am running Debian Squeeze/Testing with the latest packages in that repo.
 
I don't think Evolution is a problem here. The freeze happens even when I am not running Evolution and/or kill all Evolution related processes.
 
For me, booting into single user mode (recovery mode) VT seems to be stable except that when copying large amount of files to my USB disk it freezes.
 
In normal boot VT 1-6 also seems to work fine most of the time but freezes under high load.
 
And sooner or later when using Xorg (Gnome/KDE/TWM/XFCE) it freezes.

### Post by Achim Abeling on 2010-12-06
Hi folks,

I just came to page 25 until now, so there might be a similar post on the other 125 pages. But I just wanted to share my workaround.

I am able to fix my freeze problem by calling
# rmmod psmouse
# modprobe psmouse
per ssh from another machine as mentioned in one of the very early posts.
Then I switched to call this every minute in a cronjob which made my life a bit easier. I just had to wait at most 60 seconds until it was working again.
But then I found out how to bind this script to the power button. Now when the freeze occurs I just press the power button and the mouse and keyboard are available again.

Have a look at [http://www.banapple.de/display/BANAPPLE/2010/11/04/Mouse+and+keyboard+freeze+on+ubuntu+10.10](http://www.banapple.de/display/BANAPPLE/2010/11/04/Mouse+and+keyboard+freeze+on+ubuntu+10.10) for the complete story.

---

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-12-06
I'll just repeat, there are probably different causes of the freezes reported in this thread.
Type 1. It appears that people are reporting a freeze issue that isn't primarily  a mouse issue.
Type 2. It appears that people are reporting primarily a mouse issue.
Type 3. It appears that others may have a problem finding an appropriate driver for their graphics card.
Type 4. Some say it's a USB issue
Type 5.??

I repeat too. If I connect the Sata drive to the slower 1.5Gb/s port on the motherboard, there are no freezes here. I've another almost identical PC with a slow IDE drive which has never experienced a freeze though occasionally I've notices a temporary lock up which soon clears if I stop inputting commands. I have chosen no desktop visual effects on both PCs.

What sort of hard drive do you have if you are experiencing Type 2 freezes? Is it a Sata 3Gb/s, 1.5Gb/s, IDE or something else?

---

### Post by caixamagica on 2010-12-06
I have an ACER TravelMate 2702WLMi, Intel Pentium 4 processor 3Ghz, ATI MOBILITY RADEON 9000 IGP.
I'm using Ubuntu 10.10, fresh install, and I've tried a billion solutions but my system kept freezing everyday: 3D settings, xorg.conf, pulseaudio, grub.cfg settings, flash player, boot options, uninstall all kind of programs, and so on, and so on.
Nothing worked.

I think I've finally found something that WORKS for me:
Disable **Hyper Threading** in BIOS options.

I'll come here later to confirm.

### Post by JeremyA on 2010-12-08
my athlon 64 X2 5400+ running on a biostar A740G M2+ is experiencing random hangs.
I have tried booting with 'noacpi' option, but this does not seem to help.
kernel 2.6.32-26-server

This is a headless configuration w/o X.

nothing is logged in dmesg, /var/log/messages, /var/log/syslog, or any other logfile I can find.

I am not plugging in USB devices or doing anything special when this happens -- just using it as a gateway/router

I have deactivated the motherboard's sound system (since I don't use it), and the onboard serial port, since that is also unused.  BIOS are now set to use ACPI v3 (I figured I have nothing to lose)

when the system becomes non responsive, the keyboard and screen are dead, and I cannot access the various text consoles.  If I fix it, or it stops happening, I will post that, too.

all 8gb of DDR2 checks out in memtest86+ (3 complete passes)
cpuburn does not report cooling issues.
None of the six harddrives are reporting errors via smart
the newegg PSU calculator ensures me that my 500 watt powersupply (FSP brand) has sufficient power to run everything in the case, with 100w to spare.

The hangs do not resolve if the system is left to itself (the longest I've waited is 7 hours)

when this happens, it's like flipping a switch - - the machine goes from fully reponsive and processing network packets, to entirely non-responsive.  pings timeout, ssh sessions fail immediately, etc.

### Post by caixamagica on 2010-12-09
I can confirm that in my case freezes were caused by **Hyper Threading Technology**.
No more random freezes since I've disabled that option in BIOS.
So, even if you don't have this option, try to disable some features in BIOS.
You might find the solution over there. 
Good luck to all!

---

### Post by JBAlaska on 2010-12-15
@Ophidion, there are many bug reports on launchpad with very similar symptoms, up till today the closest one to my version was this one: [https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all](https://bugs.edge.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/585765?comments=all)

Today I found this also similar bug report here: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/627862](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/627862)  **that exactly describes my version of this bug**, [COLOR="Red"]I urge people to read the symptoms of **that bug report** and subscribe to it only if it exactly fits your version of the mouse pointer freeze problem.[/COLOR] **Otherwise try to find one that exactly matches YOUR symptoms**, As it stands there are at least 3 different mouse freeze problems that although all "Freeze the system" they do so in different ways. These problems are unrelated and each have different causes and cures, until we start to separate these logically I fear we will never have a answer to them.

I will still check in with this thread, but my diagnostic energy and fix reports are now moving to [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/627862](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/627862)

Thank you all, and good luck.

### Post by hoover67 on 2010-12-16
Hi folks,

I just decided to run memtest for a couple of hours... surprise surprise, bad RAM detected in a couple of locations! I think it's time to apologize to the devs and to the community for my wasting of their precious time and resources ;-) 

I'll try to isolate the bad stick (3GB in total) and then report back *if* memtest completes successfully *and* Ubuntu (or one of its variants) still crashes. 

I suggest to everyone to run memtest for half a day or so, I was all "oh, it didn't crash before, so it must be the distro's fault" before too, and will now have a very large piece of humble pie with cream on top ;) RAM can go bad after a couple of years of continuous use. 

All the best, Uwe

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-12-18
Just some simple thoughts, more like guesses really.
There are probably different reasons people are experiencing these freezes but probably for most folk there's one single common cause. Unfortunately these freezes manifest themselves on a variety of hardware set-ups and the software being run at the time, though it seems that this all started from 10.04 onwards. Agree?

Aside from things like faulty memory, the finger of suspicion has pointed in the directions of- power management, ACPI, Compiz, AMD's Quiet and Cool, Firefox, mouse function, Sata at 3Gb/s, proprietary video drivers, USB implementation etc.

As the PC doesn't crash and reboot by itself the kernel is the doing some things appropriately, it seems like the cpu is waiting for something to happen or complete before it can move on to the next task.

Currently my suggested causes are-
A problem with the kernel's handling of interrupt requests (irqs)?
or slow memory for the cpu in use?
or?

What were changes to the kernel in 10.04 which might suggest a cause?

### Post by dumitru on 2010-12-20
I totally agree with malv83's post dated on September 10.
And the problem is not solved till today, and it lasts for 8 months!

At home, on the laptop, I'm using Linux Mint 10, but I decided to try Ubuntu 10.10 as the most popular distribution. I installed it on my desktop. But I faced the same problem as thousands of other Ubuntu users. In 5-10 minutes of running, my system freezes (both keyboard and mouse).

I use to watch OSGUI shows on Youtube. In one of their movies Tim tells why he uses Ubuntu: "because it's stable". But I think it's not the case. Yet, for me, Windows is more stable.

I will stay on Mint.


> **malv83 said:**
> I used to recommend Linux to novice computers users like my parents. After having to solve too many broken updates with Ubuntu I had to finally concede that Windows is the most appropriate environment for a user that doesn't want to have to fight with their OS. There are just far too many obvious and critical bugs in Ubuntu to recommend it to an inexperienced user without a strong hacker ethic. While I still prefer using Linux over Windows as a software developer, I find myself beginning to lose more and more patience with Ubuntu with every new release. When will we get to a point of having a solid release of all the core functionality? Why are more features being added when the current features remain in such an unstable state? I wish the Ubuntu devs would spend more time on stability and polish and less time on features. This bug should have never reached release, and the fact that it did makes me question the whole entire Ubuntu development process and the feasibility of a community-developed OS as a contender to commercial OS for the casual desktop user. It's been months and this bug affecting so many users has not been resolved. Why not?

### Post by davidvandoren on 2010-12-20
> **Handssolow said:**
> It's probably irrelevant but this thread has 10.4 in it's title yet freezes still occur with 10.10.

On the other hand what's not been explained is why some PCs aren't affected and others are. I've had no freezes today as the hard drive has been connected to a Sata 1.5Gb/s port on the motherboard.

I have three hard-dives with 3 three ubuntus now.
two old IDE harddrive  and one 1TB sata.

The ubuntu on the sata is 10.10 and freezes from time to time.
The two ubuntu installations on the IDE are ubuntu 10.04 and never freeze.
Not in months so far. 

At least for my configuration I'm quiet sure that it has to do with sata.
If you guys are lucky enough and still have an IDE connect just get yourself a second hand 120G or whatever IDE hard-drive, disconnect the sata and install ubuntu then reconnect the Sata and use F11 or F12 to boot either system.

### Post by Handssolow on 2010-12-20
Another possible approach to try if you've got a newer desktop motherboard, Sata drives often can be downgraded to Sata 1.5Gb/s with a jumper. 

[http://www.seagate.com/ww/v/index.jsp?locale=en-US&name=Serial_ATA_Jumpers_and_Cabling&vgnextoid=4a02242cb043e010VgnVCM100000dd04090aRCRD](http://www.seagate.com/ww/v/index.jsp?locale=en-US&name=Serial_ATA_Jumpers_and_Cabling&vgnextoid=4a02242cb043e010VgnVCM100000dd04090aRCRD)

### Post by NoVista on 2010-12-21
Any time I hear someone saying things freeze up on SATA drives, the first thing I say to them is:
If you are not using a quality SATA cable, go put one on it, because with the cheap SATA cables you also get very cheap connectors on them, that, even when appearing to make good contact, do not.

I got problems with fglrx since 9.10 /Ubuntu/ and trough 10.04 to 10.10. If I try to cut off the A/C adapter and go to battery the CPU usage goes straight to not less than 50% /100% of one of the cores/ and I got no more than 10 seconds to total freeze, then I got no other option except the power button or disaster reboot /alt+prtsc.../. ### Post by antoport on 2010-12-24
I had problems with freezing few minutes after login. Every time I started some graphic intense app system would completely stop responding (REISUB had no effect). It seemed that only graphic intense apps trying to connect to network would cause the problem (in my case loading big webpage in Firefox/Chromium or starting Eclipse).
First I thought the problem was in wireless drivers, then in Ati/Amd drivers or compiz and than I compiled custom kernel...without any luck...
When using Ati proprietary drivers system would completely freeze, but on open source Ati drivers the X11 would crash and throw me back to login...
I noticed when something like that happened the fan would start spining faster...

In process of trying to fix the problem I accidentally changed the ubuntu theme and...the freezing problem dissapeared \\:D/ .
It had something to do with the custom theme I installed few months ago...
Somebody could have faced the same problem...

I'm probably using a sata cable which came with my Asrock mobo.

Some use a technology called 'pinch connector.'
If your MB supports locking SATA cables, those are another option.

What I have learned to do before plugging SATA cables in, is to get a small bottle of DeOxit, and spray it on the cable connectors before use.

### Post by MiztaW on 2010-12-26
Hello guys,
I just registered for this forum because of this thread.
I read it for many weeks now, and during the last pages, i felt there are others whose bug is very similar to mine. So i want to share my version of the bug, maybe it might help.

In my case I strongly believe it has something to do with my SATA-Drives, or with my SATA-Controller.

My bug is not as evil as yours. It occurs rarely, but is reconstructable by playing the game Sauerbraten for some minutes (always); when playing Hedgewars (but more randomly); when doing many things at the same time, including flashvideos in firefox (very very rare). One time even while programming in vim!

Mostly the system freezes for approx. 20 seconds and will go on displaying the following syslog:
```
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024020] ata4: lost interrupt (Status 0x50)
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024040] ata4.00: exception Emask 0x0 SAct 0x0
 SErr 0x0 action 0x6 frozen
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024046] ata4.00: failed command: READ DMA
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024054] ata4.00: cmd c8/00:00:78:72:87/00:00:
00:00:00/ef tag 0 dma 131072 in
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024056]          res 40/00:00:00:4f:c2/00:00:
00:00:00/00 Emask 0x4 (timeout)
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024060] ata4.00: status: { DRDY }
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.024071] ata4: soft resetting link
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.208362] ata4.00: configured for UDMA/133
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.208371] ata4.00: device reported invalid CHS 
sector 0
Dec 26 13:35:03 xxx kernel: [  909.208384] ata4: EH complete
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040024] ata4: lost interrupt (Status 0x50)
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040043] ata4.00: exception Emask 0x0 SAct 0x0
 SErr 0x0 action 0x6 frozen
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040050] ata4.00: failed command: WRITE DMA EXT
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040058] ata4.00: cmd 35/00:08:98:38:29/00:00:
27:00:00/e0 tag 0 dma 4096 out
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040060]          res 40/00:00:00:4f:c2/00:00:
00:00:00/00 Emask 0x4 (timeout)
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040064] ata4.00: status: { DRDY }
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.040075] ata4: soft resetting link
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.744740] ata4.00: configured for UDMA/133
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.744748] ata4.00: device reported invalid CHS 
sector 0
Dec 26 13:39:57 xxx kernel: [ 1203.744762] ata4: EH complete

```

But sometimes, if I get impatient and try to use the computer while it's stuck, it will crash, only Alt-SysRq-REISUB will help.
(When i once did a hardware reboot while this, my filesystem was also destroyed, fsck helped then)

Things that are possible sources of error:
-> **CPU**: Hyper-Threading
-> **Graphics**: nvidia proprietary driver
-> **HDD**: SATA-Drive
-> **Mainboard**: SATA-Controller + driver?
-> **SATA-Cable**
-> **OS**: Kernel-Version?

Things I tried:
-> **CPU**: Disabling Hyper-Threading in BIOS: failed
-> **Graphics**: nothing tested...
-> **HDD**: 3 smartctl tests (1 long, 2 short): no errors.
-> **HDD**: badblocks: no badblocks
-> **HDD**: Setting Jumper for SATA-I (which is required, because my Mainboard can only handle SATA-I :D) freezes will occur later.
-> **Mainboard**: nothing tested...
-> **SATA-Cable**: Changed ports on mainboard: failed
-> **SATA-Cable**: tried an other SATA-I cable: failed.
-> **SATA-Cable**: tried an SATA-III cable: failed.
-> **OS**: Moving my Swap-Partition to my second drive (I thought, maybe this problem has to do with the swap partition, since it occurs only when doing intensive stuff, my RAM isn't tooo big at all)

The history of my bug:
It worked semi-fine in the beginning with 10.04, there were two big freezes where I did the hardware reboot as mentioned above and had to revover my filesystem. But I thought it was nothing to worry about (2 times in approximately 5 months).
With upgrading to 10.10 it occured more often. Sometimes i even lost the harddisk, leading to Buffer I/O errors, and the System slowly crashing (the desktop started to disappear, programs failed to start, work became unsaveable).
This got better with some kernel patches of ubuntu, but I don't know which.
After this point the system froze often while doing many things at the same time or playing graphical games.
When I played Sauerbraten the error would occur after half a game.
After haveing set the jumper at my HDD for SATA-I mode the freezes occur after **one and a half game**. Definitely later! 
But this is still my current state.

**My System**
**CPU**: Pentium 4 3,00 GHz ~ Hyper-Threading enabled.
**Mainboard**: Intel I875PBZ
**Graphics**: nVidia Geforce 7600 GT
**RAM**: 1536 MB DDR-RAM (2x 512 MB, 2x 256 MB) ~ Dual-Channel enabled.
**HDD**: /dev/sdb: Western Digital Caviar Green 1,0 TB - WDC WD10EARS-00Y5B1 ~ SATA-I Jumper Set.
**OS**: Ubuntu 10.10 Maverick Meercat Kernel 2.6.35-24-generic.

**lspci**:
```

00:00.0 Host bridge: Intel Corporation 82875P/E7210 Memory Controller Hub (rev 02)
00:01.0 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82875P Processor to AGP Controller (rev 02)
00:03.0 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82875P/E7210 Processor to PCI to CSA Bridge (rev 02)

### Post by Vlad40 on 2011-01-01
OK after a week or so I believe I can say the SATA cables and drives are part of the problem. Changing cables between drives definitely changes whether the machine crashes or not or begins slowing down.
My findings so far
- shorter cables improved the situation
- slowing drives to SATA 1 speeds improved the situation
- A known good cable improved any drive and stability and will run at SATA 2 speeds/settings
- A new cable is not necessarily a good cable
- testing cables showed huge quality variance with no correlation in price or source as yet
- from ubuntu 9 to 10 there is an improvement in drive read/write speed but lower stability so next will be to look at the changelog regarding SATA

---

### Post by Dark Oak on 2011-01-09
Greetings.

I'm new to Ubuntu (for several days now) and yes I've been navigating for days for this annoying, almost arrogant problem people say they're having in this thread. I clean installed my desktop PC entirely with Ubuntu because I believe in the OS.

Things went pretty smooth for the first day, and then, it happened: I could not close any of my windows neither move them, as some may have experienced, the keyboard seems to do the work.

BUT, I discovered a trick that might help you. If you let your "[Window List]" open, the bug is magically neutralized. I don't know why it's like that but for now, I can do things no problems until I found the right answer for this problem.

So that's my share of advice. If someone more experimented can tell how and where to open [Window List], it would help. In my case, when Ubuntu has just been loaded, [About Window List] is minimized and invisible.

Good luck.

### Post by GvO on 2011-01-15
Using Ubuntu myself for over 3 years (since Gutsy) I'm now on 10.10. It has always functioned flawlessly. I'm helping a friend who's computer worked fine on 8.04 but suffered freezes since 09.10. He's now on 10.10 and still had freezes until now. Following this thread from the beginning in may last year, the one thing that puzzles me the most is the randomness of the phenomenon. Electric currents in circuits don't change randomly, there's always a physiological change needed. Last week my friends computer died entirely, no POST no video whatsoever. Thinking the Mo Bo had left  this world, I decided to take the thing apart. It occurred that the 3V BIOS battery cell was faulty and I changed it for another one. And voila The BIOS came up again and the video also. But there was another thing that caught my attention: the CPU cooler was in another circuit than it should be. That is, it wasn't connected to the Mo Bo in the usual way but ran via a fan control unit. Leaving the CPU fan connector on the Mo Bo empty.  Having read the various posts on disabling or modifying ACPI which helped some users, it occurred to me that it could be a hardware problem overheating the CPU while the system can't control CPU temperature anymore, or gets faulty feedback from the Mo Bo. Turning ACPI off could seemingly solve the problem but leaves you with an uncontrolled CPU fan.  
 So after a thorough inspection of the CPU cooler I placed it back to its proper position and now the computer is running without any freezes. Maybe it's to soon to celebrate but the system seems to be stable with a massive CPU load for two days now.
 GVO

---

### Post by hoover67 on 2011-01-15
> **GvO said:**
> Maybe it's to soon to celebrate but the system seems to be stable with a massive CPU load for two days now.
 GVO

Overheating isn't the problem at least in my case. The cpu has a massive cooler on top (amd x64x2 4200) which doesn't even strictly require a fan, CPU temp is around 40C most of the time (ondemand governor) and the freezes still occur no matter how high or low the cpu activity. 

Things have become a bit better in my case with uptimes in the couple of days, but there's still the occasional mouse click that freezes the entire system into a hard-reset state, it sometimes even freezes watching a relatively std-res movie (mpg or avi) in vlc.

### Post by GvO on 2011-01-15
> **hoover67 said:**
> Overheating isn't the problem at least in my case. 

I didn't mean overheating itself causes the problem but with ACPI on the Kernel wants to influence the cooling system whatever cooling device is used. I'm not quite sure but I think with ACPI off the BIOS tries to do the same thing. In either case some kind of connection between the software and the cooling system must be established.

### Post by ockelz on 2011-01-17
Running ubuntu since 7.04 in different flavours (KDE/Gnome/XFCE), i'm now also stuck with this 'time-bomb-freeze-bug' in Lucid, this is my share:

The freezing occurs in KDE as wel as in Gnome here on Lucid 10.04 with the latest updates. On KDE it seems to occur less. Two things i've noticed:

1.Oddly randomly when trying to shutdown the shutdown message won't show up and de CPU fan is going at 100% leaving a hard shutdown as an only option (KDE).

2. In Firefox i have also random freezes with an alert box (a javascript popup, that always displays the same message in an internal web app). The alert window does shows up whith NO text BUT with a close button. The CPU-fan also increases immediately to 100% leaving the keyboard and desktop unusable (waiting doesn't help either). The mouse is responding only. My PC is now in freeze-state. Here comes the weird part: I can only click on the close button and the system becomes response again immediately. The cpu fan slows down also and i can continue my work.

Reading this thread i do not think it's related to HW, because my same machine was running perfectly stable 9.04, 9.10 before.

Maybe this sounds stupid; Because some people are reporting freezes by clicking or keypresses that invoke  window action, "maybe", just maybe it has something to do with a window caller or whatever function that should draw a window or display a message, but simply fails and turns the desktop in an endless loop.

### Post by tk189 on 2011-01-18
I'm getting totally fed up with this problem. It's a massive issue and nothing seems to be getting done about it.

The problem began for me using 10.04 it got to the point it was unusable for anything other than messing about on. I work from home and despise Windows. I have posted further back in this thread too.

Quite often my ubuntu 10.10 boots fine, the GUI loads and everything is pretty however none of the launchers in my dock work.

The mouse cursor moves and I can click on icons, their animations all play etc

I can hover at the top of my screen, the panel lowers into view but again nothing on there will work, I can't restart, shutdown nothing!!!

All i can do is either press the reset button on my tower or CTRL SysRq B to reboot.

Other times I am using the system (I am a freelance web designer) using gedit for html editing, maybe have an ftp client open and firefox, sometimes Chrome too at the same time. Not a great deal of CPU load.

Suddenly when I come to save my progress I can see that gedit has become Read Only ... in fact it then appears that the whole filesystem has gone Read Only and then I notice it has crashed, again nothing responds although the mouse continues to work fine.

Reboot either works or I have to load LiveCd and purge and re-install grub to enable the damn thing to boot again.

I replaced my RAM with two new 2Gb sticks and for 3 or 4 weeks I didn't experience a single crash or freeze. I thought the problem was solved. Then the other day, BANG! suddenly lock up and off we went again. It is again happening several times a day amnd once again I am seriously having to consider ditching ubuntu totally and going back to using shi**y Windows - it might be a bulky, buggy turd of an OS but I have never had to fart about as much as I do with ubuntu.

This really sucks, it's not just me this is a massive issue!!! Why is it not being fixed?

I hate to use the "You get what you pay for" saying but this is not good pr for ubuntu.

My system is:

AMD Phenom 9950 Quad Core
GF8200 M2++ mother board
4GB RAM

Maverick 10.10
2.6.35-23 or 2.6.35-24 (the latter crashes worst of all)
GNOME 2.32.0

I have nVidia drivers installed but have tried without them installed, with the older one and the newer one that ubuntu makes available from system > admin > additional drivers installed and this problem doesn't appear to alter - it may be more or less frequent but it's still there.

I am interested in this FAN issue that has been mentioned, during boot up a brief message is flashed up about "unreliable CPU temp" but it's not up long enough to read fully.

I do hope a fix is discovered as I really do like the usability and features of ubuntu generally. I am keen to spread the word and would like to install it across all of the pcs (7) in our home network. I am loathe to do so at present.

---

### Post by Rounin on 2011-01-18
I'm getting the issue where the system freezes and disk usage peaks as well. One incident happened as the KDE file manager was producing thumbnails of a large number of images. All this from userspace, with no special privileges.

At least I'm getting some studying done while I'm waiting for the console to pop up...

### Post by Rounin on 2011-01-19
Just now, I was listening to a web radio in VLC and was about to open a Word document, when the bug occurred again, disk usage went through the roof, and the system took 19 minutes just to get to the console, after which X crashed and KDM restarted anyway.

Either it was just chance or it's happening on a daily basis now. Could it be a cronjob causing it? It's hard to explain the extremely high disk usage considering the disk's not in use.

Either way, I'm starting to agree about this being a serious problem. It's kind of a dealbreaker to have the OS stall for 15 minutes at random intervals.

Also, the bug report on Launchpad is here: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/582264](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/582264) But it's for Lucid Lynx only (I'm having the problem on Maverick), and it's been closed for a few months due to incomplete information.

### Post by Rounin on 2011-01-19
There's at least bug report on Launchpad, by the way: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/582264](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/582264)

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-01-19
There's nothing much new to report from my earlier posting, except that not one of our PCs running Ubuntu freeze.

1. Dual Athlon FX-62 had Ubuntu installed in October 2008 and now runs Maverick 10.10 from a slow IDE drive. I've very occasionally noticed a brief lockup lasting about 5 seconds but never any freeze that's needing a reboot. Currently the overall speed of the PC is throttled back by having such a slow IDE hard drive.

2. Single core Athlon runs Lucid 10.04 from a SATA drive with a data transfer rate of 150MBs. No freeze experienced, ever.

3. I made up a dual core Athlon based PC to replace our slowest machine. I installed 10.04 last autumn then later upgraded to 10.10. Freezes were frequent with both versions. Removing special effects and booting with pci=noacpi reduced but didn't fully eliminate the freezes. However, changing the Sata drive over from the Sata2 mobo socket to the slower Sata1 socket on the mobo has eliminated the freezes and I no longer add pci=noacpi in the boot line. Not had a freeze for weeks.

---

### Post by forestgril on 2011-01-19
Ok - I HAVE FOUND THE SOLUTION (for me). My system works without a hang for hours already (and wasn't stable for longer than 15 minutes previously).

The solution is in this forum in post nr XXX of user XXX.... EDIT: well now I can't find it :D (in the range of previous 10 pages)

But listen: it seems to be a fan/cpu-temp problem. I have simply disabled in BIOS the INTEL Fan-speed-step option (from Automatic to Disabled) and now I see no problems. Could anyone check it too for INTEL processors and search for similar AMD options?

EDIT_2: I have switched back to Maverick and the issue returned ;( But now it is connected to Firefox (not random anymore)- freezes in a matter of seconds.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2011-01-20
> **Handssolow said:**
> There's nothing much new to report from my earlier posting, except that not one of our PCs running Ubuntu freeze.

1. Dual Athlon FX-62 had Ubuntu installed in October 2008 and now runs Maverick 10.10 from a slow IDE drive. I've very occasionally noticed a brief lockup lasting about 5 seconds but never any freeze that's needing a reboot. Currently the overall speed of the PC is throttled back by having such a slow IDE hard drive.

2. Single core Athlon runs Lucid 10.04 from a SATA drive with a data transfer rate of 150MBs. No freeze experienced, ever.

3. I made up a dual core Athlon based PC to replace our slowest machine. I installed 10.04 last autumn then later upgraded to 10.10. Freezes were frequent with both versions. Removing special effects and booting with pci=noacpi reduced but didn't fully eliminate the freezes. However, changing the Sata drive over from 300Mb/s data transfer to 150MB/s by plugging the Sata lead into the slower Sata1 socket on the mobo has eliminated the freezes and I no longer add pci=noacpi in the boot line. Not had a freeze for weeks.

Many of the freezes happen when the computer is at idle so it is not solely due to the type of hard disks (SATA or IDE).

---

### Post by forestgril on 2011-01-20
It also seems to be related to Compiz. 

SET System->Preferences->Appearance->Visual effects-> NONE

When I use no visual effects I don't have a freeze in Firefox since several minutes already. When I set them to normal I immediately (within a minute) had a hang - the same-old-story.

I will let You know within hours if that helped for a longer time... 

IMPORTANT NOTE: 
keep in mind that I have also - as mentioned a few posts before - changed the setting in BIOS: The INTEL Fan-Speed-Step to "Disabled" which seemed to have done the trick for FEDORA 14 (it had a similar issue), but did not help alone in Ubuntu. 

CONCLUSIONS for now: it may be (at least) 2 separate problems leading to the same system behaviour (one with computer fan, one with GPUs) - check it people!

---

### Post by C87 on 2011-01-20

thats the solution to our problems... once i did it.. since yesterday no freezes!!!!

### Post by tk189 on 2011-01-20
> **forestgril said:**
> Ok - I HAVE FOUND THE SOLUTION (for me). My system works without a hang for hours already (and wasn't stable for longer than 15 minutes previously).

The solution is in this forum in post nr XXX of user XXX.... EDIT: well now I can't find it :D (in the range of previous 10 pages)

But listen: it seems to be a fan/cpu-temp problem. I have simply disabled in BIOS the INTEL Fan-speed-step option (from Automatic to Disabled) and now I see no problems. Could anyone check it too for INTEL processors and search for similar AMD options?

EDIT_2: I have switched back to Maverick and the issue returned ;( But now it is connected to Firefox (not random anymore)- freezes in a matter of seconds.

This is spooky, I was just reading through posts since my post yesterday with what may be a good news update (fingers crossed)...

It is also cpu fan related.

I went into my BIOS and found under Advanced the CPU Fan intelli-settings (forgive me if the wording is wrong but without shutting down and going back into the BIOS settings I can't be 100% exact) and found mine was disabled (it must have been that way since leaving the factory) - beneath the option to swap it was another option to test the fan. The recommended setting was 4Pin so I changed the setting from Disabled to 4Pin and (fingers crossed) my system has been running for about 26 hours now without a glitch...

I've done a tonne of work today, multiple browsers open, text editor, gimp an FTP client and Thunderbird and there hasn't been a single issue, no sudden READ ONLY, no freezes ... could this be the answer???

---

### Post by forestgril on 2011-01-20
>>>>>SOLUTION SOLUTION SOLUTION<<<<< (probably)

Ok guys, we already have 3 confirmations for the 2-fold-problem solution:

1. Disable advanced configuration options in BIOS for CPU fan.
2. Set Compiz effects to none in System->Preferences->Appearance->Visual effects.

>>>>>>>> I KINDLY ASK YOU ALL: <<<<<<<
Because the issue seemed to affect so many people for such a long time (because it is a 2-fold-problem - two different problems with the same effect, probably and it was hard to accidentally solve them both at once) - let them all read this solution. It would be best not to add anny comments for some time now and if You do (to confirm or to neglect), please give a reference to POST nr 1625 and below, so the solution does not dissapear in the flood of the following posts. >>>>>>>> I KINDLY ASK YOU ALL: <<<<<<<

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-01-20
Incidentally, why are so many people getting multiple posting in this thread, it's not happened before? Could the mods tidy things up?

My guess is that a while back something was changed in the kernel which has had repercussions not appreciated by the developers. Here I think that our having PCs which boot successfully without freezes from either a slow IDE drive or from a 150MB/s Sata suggest an I/O or IRQ issue. We don't get any freezes here because we have slow data transfer with our hard drives.

Throttle back your system, limit visual effects, limit your Sata drive's I/O transfer rate and my guess is that you'll have fewer freezes... but something, somewhere in the kernel still needs fixing.

### Post by hoover67 on 2011-01-21
Just for the record, I also have a problem with 10.04 and 10.10 both hanging after about 20GB or so during largish rsync jobs from an internal sata disk to an external usb drive (xfs formatted). 

During an rsync -av (I've tried both the latest version compiled from source and the rsync provided by the distro) from one disk to another, the job would eat 100% cpu after transferring about 20gb of data, then just hang and sit there blocking the shell session. Typing Ctrl-C doesn't help, nor does kill(1)ing the job with -9 in another session, the only thing that gets rid of it is a reboot or shutdown -r now, for that matter. 

For laffs, I booted pclinuxos 2010.12 off an usb stick yesterday as I haven't been able to securely back up my internal drives for a while now, reformatted my 750gb ext. usb drive with xfs from there, and happily transferred about 600gb of data from the internal drives to the ext. drive without a single hang, freeze, blinking keyboard light or whatever. 

pclinuxos uses kernel 2.6.32.something, so I'm guessing (as others have before in this thread)it's some change in the kernel that killed ubuntu's (and mint's, for that matter) stability on my system. (See my earlier posts for hardware specs). 

All the best, Uwe 

EDIT: Sorry, I just noticed my post from last night has made it into the thread even though chrome just sat there waiting for hours when I tried submitting it...
