### Post by hoover67 on 2011-01-23
I'm approaching a full day of uptime too now in spite of heavy desktop usage over the weekend. The activities that usually brought the machine down sooner or later with mouse / keyboard freeze, X crash, blinking keyboard lights, cpu pegged at 100% requiring a hard reset now seem to hum along just fine (transmission, remuxing videos with projectx, vdr recording some mpeg streams off the sat card, copying data over to a USB stick / drive). 

dmesg output has also remained clean, no spurious segfaults in chrome or whatever, so things are looking good for now. I'm running on mint10 (based on 10.10 maverick), using kernel Linux  2.6.32-27-generic #49-Ubuntu SMP Wed Dec 1 which I installed from the 10.04 LTS archives, nvidia drivers 260-beta ("nvidia-current").

In order toget xvideo support back (tv hardware overlays), I had to manually create a generic xorg.conf ("sudo Xorg -configure"), install the kernel development headers and then apt-get install nvidia current. 

Before rebooting, I had to manually enter the nvidia driver in the device section of the newly created xorg.conf, otherwise I'd be sitting at the console after a reboot with the nouveau modules having been blacklisted by the nvidia installation process.

I'll report back if the crashes re-occur... havind said that, I can hardly remember how often I have used that phrase in this thread before, always hopeful that things might have taken a turn for the better :D

Well, "hope is all we have" to use a famous quote...:)

All the best, 

Uwe
:EDIT: Scratch that, another "rm -rf" of about 4GB of data hung itself up it seems... strangely enough, I was able to remove the directories in question without a problem in a 2nd terminal session.

---

### Post by KernelSpace on 2011-01-28
My random freezing problems have been **solved**.

Here is what I did to fix the problem
1. Ran memtest86+ through GRUB and it froze on tests for 1st pass.
2. In the BIOS I changed the DRAM frequency from the default "Auto" to 400MHz.
3. The PC and Debian Squeeze has been rock solid stable ever since.

In other news the guys at OpenSUSE are having similar freezes and fixed it by modifying the USB autosuspend settings. Here is the link.

[http://forums.opensuse.org/english/get-help-here/hardware/445907-keyboard-mouse-freezes-12.html](http://forums.opensuse.org/english/get-help-here/hardware/445907-keyboard-mouse-freezes-12.html)

---

### Post by ste_bran on 2011-01-29
Even tho the title of this thread is 10.04, it seems a lot of people are using 10.10, as am I. version 2.6.35-24. So I will take a chance and post in this thread.

Not sure if my freeze is the same as what you guys are experiencing, but my experience is as follows. I have had the freeze both with the Live CD and now with my fresh install.

My symptoms are the the keyboard and mouse are no longer recognized, as if the computer had gone to sleep. However, the screen is still there, and the cpu still is on -- if the fan spinning is any indication. After a few minutes (or one minute?) the screen goes dark because the computer goes into sleep mode with no inputs received. However, if I press the power button, the computer wakes up and I get a log-in screen asking for my password, and giving me the option to Leave Message, Switch User, Cancel or Unlock. Of course, I can't respond. Is this what others mean when they say "freeze?" I saw some people mentioned getting a black screen, which is not what I have.

I let it sit a few hours in case the refusal to accept input was due to the computer being tied up doing something else, but no joy. I unplugged and plugged the keybrd & mouse, and hot swapped them, but the computer just ignores me. :mad:

These are not usb inputs, they are PS/2.

After reading this thread, I went into BIOS to look for something to set on the cpu fan. I didn't find the 4-wire setting some have mentioned, but I do have Asus Q-fan, so I disabled that. For good measure, I turned off usb 3.0 and some controllers I'm not using.

But still freezes. By the way, with a fresh install, I'm not running anything heavy. I have run memtest86+

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-01-30
I've no idea what causes these freezes.

I've posted before that one of our 3 Desktop PCs freezes frequently if the Sata hard drive is connected to the Sata2 port on the motherboard but is stable if using the Sata1 mobo socket. Incidentally the freezes started in September 2010 with a fresh install of 10.04 and continued after the upgrade to 10.10 and stopping when I change to using the Sata1 socket.

Our PC with a slow IDE hard drive has a similar but not identical Asrock mobo, it's never locked up. Over the past few days I've installed 10.10 on a new Sata drive connected to the Sata2 mobo socket. So far I've not had a freeze despite all the intensive work migrating data across from the old IDE drive and configuring the new OS. The only issue is the red led remains permanently lit on the Samsung Sata and I've not found a solution.

Regarding the freeze issue, has something been fixed recently?

### Post by Qbeta on 2011-01-30
One thing I see reading these replies is that we're all seemingly looking for "the cause" when there are probably a myriad of different things leading to the same effect - a hard freeze.  Perhaps some kernel expert can talk about what what really goes on during a freeze. What does the CPU do?  Does it halt or just go into a tight loop?  What is the low level mechanism that might be common to all these freezes even if the specific causes are diverse.

### Post by jlh68 on 2011-01-31
Well after 3-months of good operation on my laptop, it froze up this morning (2011.01.31).  I have had the problem on my desktop for a long time.  

My freeze was in that the keyboard and touchpad and the touchpad buttons had no effect on the computer.  I am not a programmer, but it seems to me that there is an internal communications problem between the input devices and the operating system.

As others have reported this problem is hardware independent as it occurs on both Intel and AMD CPU's.  It occurs on desktop computers and laptops. Both Ubuntu 10.04 and 10.10 users have reported this problem.  [U]This clearly places it in the Operating System (Ubuntu)
[/U]
I wonder why the developers have not gotten involved in finding a solution?

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-01-31
Whilst I agree that there might be several different causes of these freezes, it did seem that several people were reporting freezes with characteristics similar to mine. Unlike freezes I've know with windoze where one moment your PC is working then the next everything is locked up, here the freeze had it's own signature. It developed over about 5 seconds before the full lockup and reboot, like a crash in slow motion.

1.The first indication I get here is the screen dimming slightly
2.The mouse pointer can still be moved around the screen
3.For about three seconds, 1 or 2 dialogue boxes can be opened but soon that's all folks.
4.The freeze has progressed and you're stuck with the open dialogue box
5.Finally the mouse pointer is frozen too

On one occasion I had a terminal open during a freeze and hoped to get some useful information. It's not essential reading unless you're enthusiastic.

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1600750](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1600750)

I get no freezes if I use the Sata1 port on the motherboard with this hard drive. On an almost identical Asrock mobo I'm using Sata2 without any freezes. I've no explanation for this difference.

---

### Post by Qbeta on 2011-01-31
> **XEtedBear said:**
> [bitch-&-moan]
I share the view, previously posited in this thread, that there are probably multiple causes for these sudden 'BRS' (Big Red Switch) jobs, so I would have expected Canonical to have published some PSI procedure by now, the issue having been raised by so many people.

[/bitch-&-moan]

One PSI that comes to mind is trying to ssh or telnet (anybody still use that?) into the box from another machine.  If you don't another computer but own a smart phone there are even ssh apps for them (my Android has one).  This will at least tell you if the problem is limited to XWindows and the keyboard/mouse or if the system itself is indeed frozen.  In my case the system is totally frozen but I've seen (with other, earlier machines and verions of Linux) various Xwin crashes where the OS was actually just fine and I could ssh in, kill and restart X and get back to work.

### Post by leviathan8 on 2011-02-02
Hello. I am having the same problem on a Dell N5010 laptop with 10.04 ubuntu version. 
Maybe this will sound a bit weird, but I guess that it is a ram overheat problem? 

The memtotal is alright, but you see, the data buffers, as far as I  know, use abnormally too much RAM even if no program is running.

```
mono@mono-laptop:~$ cat /proc/meminfo
MemTotal:        1923168 kB
MemFree:         1310680 kB
Buffers:           89424 kB
Cached:           287312 kB
SwapCached:            0 kB
Active:           224628 kB
Inactive:         322732 kB
Active(anon):     180316 kB
Inactive(anon):    71864 kB
Active(file):      44312 kB
Inactive(file):   250868 kB
Unevictable:           0 kB
Mlocked:               0 kB
HighTotal:       1047640 kB
HighFree:         575608 kB
LowTotal:         875528 kB
LowFree:          735072 kB
SwapTotal:       1952760 kB
SwapFree:        1952760 kB
Dirty:                 8 kB
Writeback:             0 kB
AnonPages:        170632 kB
Mapped:            64228 kB
Shmem:             81556 kB
Slab:              21932 kB
SReclaimable:      11332 kB
SUnreclaim:        10600 kB
KernelStack:        2184 kB
PageTables:         4468 kB
NFS_Unstable:          0 kB
Bounce:                0 kB
WritebackTmp:          0 kB
CommitLimit:     2914344 kB
Committed_AS:     755148 kB
VmallocTotal:     122880 kB
VmallocUsed:       34748 kB
VmallocChunk:      37884 kB
HardwareCorrupted:     0 kB
HugePages_Total:       0
HugePages_Free:        0
HugePages_Rsvd:        0
HugePages_Surp:        0
Hugepagesize:       4096 kB
DirectMap4k:       16376 kB
DirectMap4M:      892928 kB

```Same with free:

```
mono@mono-laptop:~$ free -m
             total       used       free     shared    buffers     cached
Mem:          1878        628       1249          0         87        312
-/+ buffers/cache:        228       1649
Swap:         1906          0       1906

```Is it normal that the data buffers use up to 87 mb ram right  after boot? Before this happened, my total memory was 1950 or so, and  now it decreased to 1870. Also to note that on desktop the data buffers  after boot up only use 12 MB, not 87...

EDIT: I also have plugged in a USB mouse, which keeps freezing time to time, though the OS continues working. But not no more, both keyboard and mouse die. I will try running ubuntu without mouse to see if it will still freeze.

EDIT2: I will try uninstalling stuff like acpi, powertop, java, disabling bluetooth services, saned and cups and setting gdm to log in automatically. Maybe it will help, I will return with an answer later.

EDIT3: So, apparently *crosses fingers* ubuntu worked fine in the past 1 hour. Disabled saned, cups and bluetooth, purged powertop and acpi, and I no longer use GDM to login. Let's hope it will continue working. What I doubt, is that when the data buffers on RAM reaches values higher than 100, the laptop will halt. 
*to note is that the laptop is now running with AC power. Didn't tried on battery yet.

EDIT4; Good news. It's all fine. Works perfectly under battery and AC power.

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-02-03
One of our PCs froze for the first time today. On 28th January instead of booting Ubuntus from a slow but reliable IDE hard drive, I installed 10.10 on a new Sata drive connecting it to the Sata2 port on the mobo. I've been working on my Garmin satnav plugging in and out of the usb port transferring files. The first indication of the freeze was that this time the satnav didn't show up on Desktop. I soon lost the top toolbar but surprisingly I could still access the Places window though each time I attempted to look at files, a new window would open with this text appeared-

could not display /home/handssolow/Desktop
Error: Error starting file /home/handssolow/Desktop
input/output error

Eventually I did Ctrl+Alt+Del but not much happened initially then this-

Ext4-fs error (device sda1):ext4_find_entry:inode #27531741: (comm gdm-binary) reading directory 1block0

After a reset it's been ok for the past few hours.
One vote for it being a USB issue. One vote for it being an input/output issue.

### Post by moodog on 2011-02-03
I have an athlon X2 wtih an nVidia GeForce 6200 in it, which has been running mythbuntu fine for a few years now. In the last two days I've started having similar problems to those reported here. I initially thought it was because I'd installed mythbuntu repos, and updated to the latest myth release as I'd most often witness the crash while running mythfrontend and watching livetv. However after digging around a bit, I witnessed the same problem simply while using the desktop (without mythfrontend running). Each time I've ssh'd from another machine, and found that X is taking 100% of the CPU - sometimes the monitor and 42" plasma that are connected actually go blank, as X is obviously struggling to drive them. I am running Ubuntu 10.10 with all the latest updates, and the latest official nvidia driver. Not sure why this has just started happening 2 days ago.

If I run mythfrontend on another machine (the problem machine is also the backend) then everything runs fine. Would love to solve this problem. Am planning to upgrade the graphics card to a GT430 this weekend, but don't want to do that upgrade and still find I've got this problem.

If that is all you have to post and you're unhappy with people not listening, stop posting it.

*do you still use candles?* Yes.

*ride a horse to work?* Wish that I could...would save a lot in gas.

*Systems should not freeze.*  Ahh, but it does freeze.

*there is usually a way to have updates and not have problems.*  Please tell us what that way is.  Many users have not found a safe way to do an update.

*"Don't update your system" is not a useful answer.*  Unfortunately or fortunately by not doing an update give many users a "WORKING" computer so, in a way, it is a useful suggestion.  You do not have to listen to the suggestion.

---

### Post by bulazeem on 2011-02-07
Maybe choosing not to update is a way to avoid problems.  However, for those of us that do want to update, a solution is needed.  Please try to keep this constructive.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2011-02-07
> **bulazeem said:**
> Maybe choosing not to update is a way to avoid problems.  However, for those of us that do want to update, a solution is needed.  Please try to keep this constructive.

Yes, update would be beneficial if they work.  What is the point of doing update if all we get is "random freezes"?  I wish that I could do an update and still have a working computer.  And, yes I am avoiding the problem - a big random freeze problem!  I am sorry, but this reminds me too much of Windows...constant security/bug fix updates and at the end...a complete Windows re-install.  Maybe, I am wrong in this approach, but my most reliable computer is still running 8.04 LTS w/o a single update.

---

### Post by bulazeem on 2011-02-07
We appreciate your advice and I'm sure you've gotten someone's computer back on track with it.  However, you've already conveyed to us how you have no trouble with your computer by placing undue emphasis on the fact that you haven't updated.  We completely understand where you're coming from and your decision to avoid updates for freezes.  You're not moving this thread forward though.  Part of making things better is trial and error instead of living by the "if it aint broke dont fix it" philosophy.

---

### Post by Qbeta on 2011-02-07
The problem is that if you've been updating and start to notice random freezes it's hard to know what caused it.  The tempting thing to do is just keep updating and hope that maybe the next update will fix things.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2011-02-08
> **Qbeta said:**
> The problem is that if you've been updating and start to notice random freezes it's hard to know what caused it.  The tempting thing to do is just keep updating and hope that maybe the next update will fix things.

Yes, I have done probably more than 10 updates with 10.04 LTS on my play computer with the same hope that the "next update" would fix the problem.  I do not modify my working computer (with 8.04 LTS) because I need it.  My advice was given to users who posted panic requests for help because they do not have a working computer due to these random freezes.  It seems logical to me that, unless we have clear solutions to these problems, the best thing to do is to leave the computer alone.  The risk is minimal because there just are not too many viruses for Linux.  Keep in mind that there are many users who would not know what to do if their computers lock up.

---

### Post by mbreman on 2011-02-08
Well, after several weeks of running without any freezes now, even with a lot of sleep/resume cycles, i do really get the impression that the freeze problem is solved for me by setting Visual Effects to NONE.

I can recommend that setting to everyone with this freeze problem.

---

### Post by Qbeta on 2011-02-08
> **mbreman said:**
> Well, after several weeks of running without any freezes now, even with a lot of sleep/resume cycles, i do really get the impression that the freeze problem is solved for me by setting Visual Effects to NONE.

I can recommend that setting to everyone with this freeze problem.

You mean in CompizConfig?  I don't see a generic setting for Visual Effects unless you mean just turning off everything under "Effects".

---

### Post by TheTank on 2011-02-09
My system really started acting up a few days ago. A couple of hangs and 1 reboot on one day.
After doing the disable CPU-Fan in bios it still had a problem, but so far it seems ok.

As I do not even have Compiz enabled, that should not be the problem. ;)

One thing I did notice though, it really likes to happen when I have FF running.

---

### Post by psychok9 on 2011-02-09
I've solved my lockup/freeze of 5/10s adding "libata.force=noncq" without quote on grub2 kernel boot configuration, file /etc/default/grub and update-grub, before the words "splash quiet".

---

### Post by jrusidoff on 2011-02-10
Well, I have been suddenly having this same problem after a good solid year of Ubuntu working like a charm.  I have to think it was because of one of the many (and there have been MANY) updates that have come along recently.  As much as I like to have up-to-date stuff, I have to say that the guy who suggested that "if it works, leave it alone" is on to something.

---

### Post by mbreman on 2011-02-10
> **Qbeta said:**
> You mean in CompizConfig?  I don't see a generic setting for Visual Effects unless you mean just turning off everything under "Effects".

System -> Preferences -> Appearance -> Tab: Visual Effects -> None.

---

### Post by dnguyen1963 on 2011-02-10
> **mbreman said:**
> System -> Preferences -> Appearance -> Tab: Visual Effects -> None.

I suggested this fix awhile back along with turning the power management off.  Ubuntu worked fine for a few weeks then random freezes reappeared without anything  being done to the computer.  The system looked fine until I moved the mouse.  X-org crashed, but no error message could be found anywhere in the log.:confused: This approach minimized the freezing problem.  As long as I was doing something on the computer I had no freezes.  It was only when I leave the computer idle for awhile then I would experience the freeze.

---

### Post by wyoming on 2011-02-15
Just a small grain of salt in this huge soup:
I came to believe that the problem starts when the CPU gets quickly strained regardless of the application that does it (firefox, sound, whatever). Often the freezes happen after I click too fast on 2 different things or such. Gray out, freeze, icons disappearing, hard stop and a few failed reboots before the system is back up again for an unpredictable period of time...

### Post by MiztaW on 2011-02-16
Hello guys, I solved my issue, as described in post [#1582](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10282450&postcount=1582).

I just added some new PCI-SATA-Controller (this cheap one: [by Digitus](http://www.digitus.info/en/products/accessories/interface-cards/pc-interface-cards/sata-ide/digitus-sata-150-raid-pci-card-2-port)), now my symptoms have vanished, no random freezes since then...

But my freezes were slightly different from the others, so its only interesting for those whose errors are similar to mine.

Greez
MiztaW

### Post by vinkic on 2011-02-20
I have the similiar problem,
process /*usr*/*bin*/*X* :*0* -br -audit 0 -auth /var/lib/gdm/ :0.Xauth -nolisten tcp vt7 uses 30%-40% all the time, I think that OpenGL is the problem - reinstall it, I reinstall NVidia display driver and nothing then when I try to logout I notice that cpu ussage whent to normal. Then I save all the running process

sudo ps axf > workingsys.log

and restart. 
When the system restarted I run htop and see that process *usr/bin/X :0 -br -verbose -auth"* usess 30%-40%

then I save process list 

sudo ps axf > badsys.log

and analyse two logs. Than I saw diffrence in openbox process. When I remove it  sudo apt-get autoremove openbox all back to normall 

I'v install compiz, metacity and openbox maybe something is happened when all that managers are installed and that makes xorg to load cpu all the time. 

I'm a newbee to ubuntu because this post is little funny to someone but I think I could help

### Post by pbakker on 2011-03-02
I came to this thread because I was experiencing random freezes on my Ubuntu 10.04 workstation running 2.6.32 kernels. I started from a clean install on this computer which had previously been running openSuSE 9. I have run Ubuntu on other computers succesfully for a number of years, including 8.04 LTS and 9.10.

Symptoms of the freezes:

[LIST]
[*]Firefox crashes regularly
[*]Clicking on links in Chrome does not go anywhere
[*]The Gnome desktop gradually becomes unresponsive, windows do not repaint, the task bar responds but programs cannot be launched or closed.
[*]After a period of inactivity the system does not come back from a screen lock. On the black screen I can still move the mouse pointer but the system does not respond to any keyboard input.
[*]Typically I can still ping the machine over the network but cannot ssh into the machine.
[*]There are is no crash-related info in /var/log/messages
[*]System would freeze randomly without apparent relationship to activity or number or combination of programs running, uptime anywhere from minutes to several hours. Typically several hard resets and reboots required per day.
[*]Large file copies onto the machine using either NFS or SMB seem to lock up
[/LIST]
I had tried a number of things without success, including

[LIST]
[*]Make sure system is updated (maybe the next update fixes things)
[*]Switch to the 2.6.31-11-rt kernel
[*]Run memtest86 to see if there is a RAM issue
[*]Set visual effects to "None" (System > Preferences > Appearance > Visual Effects)
[/LIST]
Video card from lspci gives
```
01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation G98 [GeForce 8400 GS] (rev a1)
```Currently the system has an uptime of a week without freezes after updating the kernel to a 2.6.34 mainline kernel. Reference is [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes). As advertised, this does leave me with some degredation in video capability, but on the upside I am able to get useful work done on this machine without the frustration of multiple daily reboots.

---

### Post by Andrew.Stevenson on 2011-03-03
I believe many different issues of freezing are being discussed here (hence all the mixed replies producing a neverending thread).
For a summary of my *specific* problem, please read one of my previous post at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9820011&postcount=988](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9820011&postcount=988).

I'm having approximately between 1 and 5 freeze a day (depending on the weather? :p).
I've been trying for a long time to find out the cause of this bug but to no avail, then I lost hope and just gave up.
I'm actually using a dirty fix by running a program that will "rmmod psmouse" and "modprobe psmouse" 
every 30 seconds (system should unfreeze after 30sec, you can decrease sleep time if needed). 
Ugly for sure, but works here, which is fine by me as long that I don't have to reboot all day long.


```
/*
 * unfreeze: do a "rmmod psmouse; modprobe psmouse" every 30 seconds (must run as root)
 *
 * gcc -o /usr/bin/unfreeze unfreeze.c
 *
 * add /usr/bin/unfreeze to /etc/rc.local
 */

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>


int main()
{
        if (fork () != 0)
        {
                exit (0);
        }

        if (fork () != 0)
        {
                exit (0);
        }

        setsid ();

        while (1)
        {
                if (fork () == 0)
                {
                        execl ("/sbin/rmmod", "/sbin/rmmod", "psmouse", 0);
                }
                else
                {
                        wait (NULL);
                }

                if (fork () == 0)
                {
                        execl ("/sbin/modprobe", "/sbin/modprobe", "psmouse", 0);
                }
                else
                {
                        wait (NULL);
                }

                sleep (30);
        }

        return 0;
}
```

---

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-03-04
I agree that many people report their freeze problem under the umbrella of this thread, though earlier more people appeared to report freezes which had similar characteristics to mine. They were-

The screen dims/greys slightly, you know immediately that a freeze is about to happen in a few seconds time.
For about 5 seconds you might have time to open a window then the mouse buttons freeze.
The mouse pointer can be moved around the screen but that's all
Soon screen/mouse/keyboard are all locked up
There's nothing recorded in the logs
Only a hard reset restores the system  

I was getting frequent freezes with Lucid and Maverick on just one PC here. Booting with pci=noacpi, not using power manager and compiz effects all seemed to help. However moving to use the Sata socket rather than Sata2 eliminated the freezes for us. We haven't had any freezes this year.

### Post by chkneater on 2011-03-08
Changing the sata socket would be an easy fix since some earlier ones only supported ONE sata device anyways.  The thing with random freezes IS : (read this REAL closely now) YOU CAN"T POST THOSE QUESTIONS HERE!!!  

Do you realize how soon that Cononical would run out of servers if EVERY single person flooded them with their craash problems?

My point: Launchpad.net

Look there before posting here, every single bug goes throught launchpad and you can submit YOUR information and they will even ask you to perform tests to help diagnose your problem.  

Example: OP's problem had to do with mislabled metacity directory, last poster had to switch SATA ports, one person just wrote a script to run every 30 seconds and refresh.

Most likely this IS a hibernation issue, so try turning off hibernation mode if you haven't found an answer yet, but got to Launchpad.net and look there first!!

In case you don't know Launchpad.net fixes all your bugs anyways, you just may not know it.

---

### Post by TheTank on 2011-03-09
@chkneater:
Ok, then report this thread to the Mods and if they think we should move to Launchpad.net, they can close it and we all can decide what to do.

Otherwise this is IMHO a nice place to just dump freeze problems. Currently everyone in this thread are looking for similarities and if we find some, we can probably distill it then create a report.
Yet if everyone that has an issue would immediately report it, Lauchpad.net would be flooded instead and we would be taking developer time away for issues that are completely unclear.
At the moment we are not taking a single minute of developer time.

### Post by Handssolow on 2011-03-09
Essentially I'm restating here views that I've posted about before.

Though one of our PCs stopped freezing when I changed from Sata2 to Sata 1, it was obvious that this wasn't the main cause. Initially the frequency of freezes were reduced from about hourly to one per session booting with pci=noacpi. Possibly the implementation of Sata2 on my now dated Asrock motherboard may be important. During one of our freezes I happened to have an open terminal and could enter commands, I guess the OS was just running in RAM. I could not interrogate my hard drive, mostly getting I/O error messages in the terminal.

Freezes in my view is/was perhaps caused by an IRQ or I/O problem and there were several fixes which helped people overcome this kernel bug. Slowing things down by not using compiz effects or power management worked for some. This did not mean that compiz, ACPI, power management or altering Sata were the cause. Some people were reporting freezes only after they updated their fresh install. Those who reported freezes stopping when they replaced faulty memory didn't shed any fresh light.

Anyone an idea how many Ubuntu users have systems which have freezes today?

---

### Post by Ophidion on 2011-05-05
I am downloading that new driver now but I find a little inconvenience in installing the driver that way
Searching around to find the PPA repository and here it is

edit the next file as a root:
> /etc/apt/sources.list

add that line:
```
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/ubuntu-x-swat/x-updates/ubuntu lucid main #NVIDIA drivers
```
Save and exit.

then follow that link below:
[http://keyserver.ubuntu.com/pks/lookup?op=get&search=0xAF1CDFA9](http://keyserver.ubuntu.com/pks/lookup?op=get&search=0xAF1CDFA9)

This will generate a public key, copy the page contents starting from "-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----", create an empty file on your disk, name it **x-swat.key**, open it with a text editor (gedit or geany or ...), paste into the file as plain text. Then save and exit.

open "Synaptic Package Manager"->Settings->Repositories->Authentication->Import Key File, then browse to where you saved it. Close. Reload.

Now you will be some months behind the latest version through synaptic.
OR
Through Terminal
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-current nvidia-current-modaliases nvidia-settings

```

Hope that helps.

### Post by chargersfan420 on 2011-05-06
I haven't read the entire thread (176 pages?  Wow!) but instead just the first and last two pages.  Has anyone considered that it might be your wireless causing the problem?

I have a Broadcom 43224:
```
$ sudo lshw -C network
  *-network
       description: Wireless interface
       product: BCM43224 802.11a/b/g/n
       vendor: Broadcom Corporation
       physical id: 0
       bus info: pci@0000:05:00.0
       logical name: wlan0
       version: 01
       serial:  xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx
       width: 64 bits
       clock: 33MHz
       capabilities: pm msi pciexpress bus_master cap_list ethernet physical wireless
       configuration: broadcast=yes driver=brcm80211 driverversion=2.6.38-8-generic firmware=N/A ip=192.168.1.152 latency=0 link=yes multicast=yes wireless=IEEE 802.11abgn
       resources: irq:17 memory:f0800000-f0803fff

```
In 10.10, I had issues with the Broadcom STA driver (module name: wl) because it was generic, meant to cover a wide range of cards but had issues with the 43224 specifically (and in some cases the 43225, 4313 & related chipsets).  I have been waiting patiently for 11.04 to come out so I can make use of new drivers for this series (module name: brcm80211).

Anyway, both the STA driver and the new brcm driver cause random freezes for me.  They can happen at any time, and for no apparent reason (although I think it might have something to do with automatic scanning of wireless frequencies in range).

My apologies if I don't have any links to support my claims, but I thought it might help to source the heart of your problem.  Check out what type of wireless card you have.  

Also, google is your friend.

---

### Post by mcrsro on 2011-05-07
> **Dutch70 said:**
> There are several reasons for the system to freeze. Anyone with an Intel card & some others should try the method in my sig.

I want to share my experience about how to fix the random gnome freezing in Ubuntu 10.04.1. I checked this on two notebooks, Asus UL20A and Dell Latitude-13, both having Intel 4500HD video card.

The essential symptoms were:

- completely random freezing of Gnome
- after several "suspend" instances, a message announcing that Ubuntu is running with low screen resolution

After several trials (system update, installing backported kernels and so on) I found the following solution:

1) I upgraded to kernel 2.6.38-8.42~lucid1 from the kernel-ppa:

[http://www.ubuntuupdates.org/ppa/kernel-ppa?dist=lucid](http://www.ubuntuupdates.org/ppa/kernel-ppa?dist=lucid)

( After the introducing of this repository, I used Synaptics to install:
linux-image-2.6.38-8-generic-pae, linux-headers-2.6.38-8-generic-pae and linux-headers-2.6.38-8)

2) I upgraded the Intel video driver (2.15) from the Stefan Glasenhardt launchpad:

Install the updated -intel driver

[https://launchpad.net/~glasen/+archive/intel-driver](https://launchpad.net/~glasen/+archive/intel-driver)

sudo add-apt-repository ppa:glasen/intel-driver 
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade

I rebooted and noticed the following effects:

- no more freezes: I squeezed the Asus UL20A by putting it frequently on "suspend" and "wake-up". Now I already have 5 days and 17 hours from the last booting and I didn't experience one freeze.

Checked the with System Log Viewer the Xorg.0.log file. The significant difference is that now the UXA feature is enabled (this was not activated by the old 2.9.1 video driver, as was shown in the Xorg.0.log file).

After one week without freeze, I will check for another week using hibernate. My actual impression is I will not have freezes and the system is now stable.

### Post by The Chef on 2011-05-13
Big thread, but I will add my 2 cents. I had this problem too, with a 64 bit i5 Intel processor on a Shuttle box.  Flash seemed to set it off, but not consistently.

I think it's fixed.  In Bios, I set IGD Graphics Mode to 64 MB (the lowest) and DVMT Memory to 128 MB (the lowest).  I have had hundreds of hours of processing since with no crash.  I can still edit HD video and have not noticed any performance effects -- but I am not a gamer.

I wonder whether some combination of the Ubuntu Lucid graphics driver, the i5 onboard HD graphics processing and the memory allocation by the Shuttle Bios (AMIBIOS v104 build 7/27/10) is the cause.  The very same machine never displayed this problem with Windows 7 (64 bit) using the higher IGD/DVMI settings.  So, I do not believe the issue is just Bios vs. Processor thing.

### Post by beaucoder on 2011-06-02
No freezes or lock-ups for 3 days!!!  Lucid 10.04 Nvidia 8400GS video card, 8 gb RAM, AMD Athlon II dual core processor. AMI bios. 

Was having daily multiple lockups: 1 to 3 a day. For over 6 months. Once in a while more lockups than 3. The cursor would continue to work but the mouse & keyboard otherwise dead. Only a hard (power off) reset would work. Once in a while the Alt +SysReq + REISUB and then a Ctl + Alt + Del  would re-boot the system.

The History: New install of Lucid 10.04 worked just fine for a few monthis, then either an update changed something or I did something that started the miseries. Logs did not point to any one error. 

Over time I noticed most of the freezes happened while using FireFox 3.6. I also used Chromium but had fewer lockups there. Recently I had even more freezes, mainly in FireFox3.6. 

Time to bite the bullet. I backed everything up and made a drive image via Clonezilla. Then....

   1. Installed a fresh clean Lucid 10.04 LTS with the latest ISO. Ran the updates as usual.
   2. Even tho I allowed the FireFox 3.6 install, I decided to also install FireFox 4.0.1 manually from the Mozilla site. 
   3. Extracted the 4.0.1 FF and made it my default. (Kept 3.6 for comparison & testing, if needed.)
   4. Did not add back in all my other installed packages. I have about 24 that I've added in over the years.
   5. Kept everything simple. No Chromium yet, it will come later. No extensions to FF (Tab Mix Plus, Ad Block Plus, Better Privacy, No Script, to name a few.)
   6. Half-expected a freeze or lockup the very first day. Nada, zippo, zilch. Third day now, no freezes, no lockups. 

I did use the F6 AMI BIOS "Optimized Defaults" setting in the BIOS and set the ACPI to "S3". Otherwise nothing unusual. 

I think there is an incompatibility between FF3.6.x and either the video processing or the FF-to-mouse synchronization. Most often, the freezes happened right after I clicked on something either at the top of the FF screen (bookmarks, tabs or menu command selection) or far less often when clicking on a button or link.

Out of curiousity I made a backup image of my fresh, new, stable Ubuntu Lucid install and ran FF3.6 in my OLD install again. Froze up within the first 40 minutes. Ran it twice and it locked up each time. 

Restored my fresh, new Ubuntu Lucid image with FireFox 4.0.1 ( and the 3.6 which I don't use except for testing.) Bingo!!  NO MORE FREEZES. So, so nice as long as I stay away from FireFox 3.6.

My Visual Effects = None in Preferences > Appearance. I have 2 monitors hooked up to the video card using standard Ubuntu setup. Using no other or proprietary drivers.

Using max screen resolutions on my Acer 19" & 20" LCD monitors. 

Gnome Desktop with usual 2 panels. Using 6 WorkSpaces. 

I tested FireFox 3.6 twice more in this new setup for short but busy sessions. Sure enough: freezes. 

As long as I now stick to FireFox 4.0.1 -- Shazzam!!!!   NO MORE FREEZES!!!  Glory!!!   :grin:

I hope this helps you in your quest to have solid, reliable, predictable Ubuntu Lucid 10.04 LTS usage. 

I am just plain delighted thus far. Sure missed my steady, reliable, trustworthy Ubuntu. 

Best wishes for success to all of you,

beaucoder in Sugar Creek

P.S. Any further freezes or lockups, I will post details here. My gratitude to all who posted here. Helped me narrow the selections in my new setup.

### Post by beaucoder on 2011-06-05
Hi [diegoviola]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=1286683")

No the freezes & lockups on my system never happened on shutting down. It did not appear to be a driver issue because I am using the Lucid drivers, that is, no Nvidia or enhanced Visual Effect (set to none). 

They almost always happened on the desktop while using FireFox 3.6.x. I would click on something and there would be a slight hesitation then the freeze. After a few months of this I could almost always tell immediately when it was going to lock up. It seemed like Lucid made some sort of brief, valiant attempt to handle the click (almost always on a bookmark, menu command or a link), then a micro-pause, then nothing.

I have the system monitor windows on my Gnome Panel so I could just catch the last few CPU cycles or I/O spikes. Then zilch. But the mouse cursor would always work but nothing else would function: keyboard, reboot button, Ctl +Alt + Del or the power button on my keyboard.

Still working flawlessly since May 30th. It's the 5th today. Keeping my fingers crossed.

My box has an MSI motherboard with AMD Phenom II dual core processor. Nvidia 8400s video card without using any Nvidia drivers. 8GB RAM, "pae" kernel 2.6.32-32-generic-pae. I misstated my processor above it's "AMD Phenom(tm) II X2 560 Processor". 

If more detailed system info is needed I will be glad to supply it. 

Smooth sailing so far. Hooray!!! I hope you achieve the same, too.

beaucoder

Do you use the nouveau driver?

I know there are/were some problems with the proprietary NVIDIA driver recently, where resizing a window would lock the entire OS. I'm not sure if this has been fixed, since I switched to xf86-video-nouveau.

But nouveau also has some issues, and it would freeze the entire OS, by only giving me the chance to hard reboot/shutdown the computer. However, a latest update to nouveau apparently has solved this issue for me and I no longer get this "hang/freeze" problem with nouveau.

I don't use Ubuntu, I run Arch Linux, but drivers/software tend to be the same across distros, maybe if you are using nouveau and this is indeed the problem, maybe getting a nouveau update on Ubuntu would solve that issue for you. As the last nouveau update has helped me to solve this problem.

Good luck.

### Post by Baasha on 2011-06-11
Just thought I would throw my 2 cents in to see if it helps.  I have had this random freezing problem (total lock-up of mouse and keyboard, no way out but a hard re-set) for many, many months.  I finally gave up and went back to Karmic.  But then I got a notice that Karmic was no longer being supported so I was forced to upgrade again.  The problem came right back, so I started a weeks long search, hoping after all this time there had been a fix.  I tried everything I came across, but no success.

I came across a post in this thread, way back in the first 5-6 pages that I must have missed previously.  The poster had an almost identical system as mine: Asus P7P55D mobo, Nvidia 195 driver, same amount of RAM, 64 bit system, etc.  He happened to mention in his hardware description that he had a usb keyboard and mouse, connected to his ps2 ports via a usb/ps2 adapter.  I had never even thought about that that might be a problem, because I had just assumed it was just a straight wire to wire transfer.  But perhaps there is some adjustment in signal levels between the two signals, I don't really know.  All I can say is that I plugged my mouse directly into a usb port.  I haven't had a freeze for about ten days now.  Before this change I was getting freezes every few minutes.  I was spending more time rebooting than working.  Now all I have to do is to get some usb extender cables so I don't have wires cluttering up my desk.  This may not work for everyone but I bet there are a lot people out there that have adapters on their mouse and keyboard cables, so it is worth checking.  I had completely forgotten that I even had a usb mouse because of the ps2 connections on the computer.  It also might explain the wide variety of hardware configurations that are being affected by this problem.

One thing I noticed when having a close look at the usb cable is that it has a choke around the cable (ferrite core?) just before the connector.  Even though it only represents a few cents in extra cost, it makes a huge difference when you are producing hundreds of thousands of cables.  Manufacturers don't spend money unless they have to.  So perhaps (I'm just guessing) the usb system creates some kind noise on the line that is re-introduced by the extra length of ps2 cable.  After all the time and frustration if that is all it was, it will be a cheap fix.

I hope this helps at least a few people.

### Post by Baasha on 2011-06-23
This is an extremely frustrating problem to track down.  I have been trying to find a solution from the time 10.04 was first introduced.  The worst part of it all is the logs don't give any useful information.  People are just trying random guesses as to what the problem is; what works for one doesn't work for another.  The developers want concrete evidence before they will jump in (don't blame them for that) but in this case there is none to give them.  The problem is widespread across multiple hardware configurations so I tend to think it is operating system based but then some people have had success with newer or changed hardware.  In my case it was simply a case of going from a PS2 mouse (analog signal) to a USB mouse (digital signal).  So I am surmising that perhaps my PS2 cables were picking up high frequency interface from my compact fluorescent desk lamp, maybe the phone, or even from the monitor which were all near the cables.  I know it isn't much help but keep experimenting.  What we need is some one who knows and understands Ubuntu and also is an expert in how electronic devices interact.  Any electrical engineers/programmers out there?  I was almost to the point where this problem was a deal breaker and was considering switching to a different brand of Linux.  Any new user of Ubuntu who runs into this problem won't be back for a long time thinking that "of course it doesn't work because it's free."  I love Ubuntu but if someone doesn't get a handle on this the future doesn't look very optimistic.

---

### Post by StuartN on 2011-06-23
> **Baasha said:**
> The worst part of it all is the logs don't give any useful information.

You give the key right here. The root partition has been marked read-only following some unknown error state, and therefore no trace of that error state can be retrieved from the logs because they can not be written to.

There are several different causes in this thread, including mouse and graphics driver issues, in addition to some fundamental timing issue that causes the filesystem to lock read-only. A driver issue alone would leave a trace in the logs for inspection on reboot.

One repeatable factor seems to be that wireless is disabled on the first reboot after the lock.

(I have mainline kernel 2.6.34 and that has resolved the issue on my machines).

---

### Post by StuartN on 2011-06-24
> **Baasha said:**
> OK, but how do I check to see if the / partition is marked read only and how do I change that?

You need to prepare before the system locks by a) Having a terminal open; b) Setting up SSH for network access from another machine; c) Use Ctrl-Alt-F1 to open and log in to a text login. Sometimes you will find that the read-only mode even prevents a new log-in, so you need to have your text-mode login prepared and just wait.

**mount** (or **mount | grep " / "**) will list the current state of all mounted filesystems, for instance **/dev/sda4 on / type ext4 (rw,relatime,errors=remount-ro,data=writeback)** in a healthy system - the "rw" will become "ro" after the lockup.

You should not reset the filesystem to write access, because it has locked for the purpose of protecting the root filesystem. You will, however, be able to run any debugging commands that you can think up, until the system attempts a disc write and locks your text terminal. Other filesystems (e.g. a USB drive, or your home partition) should be available for saving output.

---

### Post by DawieS on 2011-06-25
Short story:
Imminent disk failure caused random freezing.
Long story:
I have a fairly old desktop PC on which I have used Ubuntu for the last three years. It ran very stable without any hick-ups, except for some sound problems in the early stages of 9.04.

Over the last about three weeks, I started getting random freezes, at first once/day, but building up to about three times/day. The symptoms varied:
- A solid frozen state most of the times, where the only way out was hard boot or power shut-down.
- A semi frozen state, where the mouse was still active. However when trying to open an application, displayed an input/output error. Attempting a soft restart or shut-down gave no reaction, and the only way out was hard boot.
- The freeze was sometimes preceded by a single click sound from the internal disk, followed by the HDD LED full on (not flickering as when the disk is working normally).

To me the last two symptoms was a dead giveaway that the freezing was caused by the disk. I then backed up the data on it, and cloned the Ubuntu 10.04 partition onto one of my external disks. The internal disk was an old 200GB IDE disk, and according to Disk Utility was powered on for 2.6 years in total, with about 4200 power cycles.

Interestingly, all diagnostics which I ran on the disk (even some from the disk vendor), showed no problems, and that the disk was good. However, when I switched on the PC yesterday morning, the disk was dead, and the BIOS informed me that I should "INSERT SYSTEM DISK AND TRY AGAIN". I then dug up an even older 40GB IDE disk that I installed internally, mainly because I need all four Sata ports on the motherboard for my external disks. On these I store my data and backups, and they are switched off most of the time.

The boot-up is now slower than it used to be, but I am back in business, and Ubuntu is again as rock solid as ever.:grin:

---

I experienced freeze problems on a Lenovo T410, which were caused by the wireless drivers that are shipped with Ubuntu. My T410 uses this controller:

$ lspci | grep RTL
Realtek Semiconductor Co., Ltd. RTL8191SEvB Wireless LAN Controller

The freezes can be fixed by installing the latest drivers from the manufacturer.

### Post by Ophidion on 2011-07-26
Hi, everybody, my problem now is **[COLOR=Green][solved][/COLOR]**. I mentioned before that it is the nvidia-driver*xserver mix doesn't match. A new driver called **libgl1-mesa-dri-experimental** enables my 3d without hangs. Now I can use unity, compiz, emerald, etc. without a freeze!!!
Hope it works for those with nvidia graphics cards who used to have that freeze after installing nvidia-current or nvidia.....run.
to find it you need to get the ppa: **xorg-edgers**. I use **[COLOR=Red]ppa manager[/COLOR]** to find and install ppa's for me.

### Post by david.medine on 2011-08-26

I cannot boot in standard mode (I presume because the wireless drivers are somehow corrupted and Lucid is trying to boot them). 

I figured that I would simply do a backup and wipe the hard drive and start over, but I can't backup remotely to my backup server because I don't have the internet and I can't back up to my LaCie external hard drive because I don't have the necessary nfts software installed, and I can't install that because I don't have internet access. I am hoping there is a way to resolve this without having to reformat my system (I am running Lucid on a cheapo Lenovo netbook which worked perfectly until a few days ago) but I can get my files backed up one way or another if necessary.

The lesson here is to backup often and in an organized fashion. I have been lazy about this and now I am painted into a corner.

Also, I was doing a lot of Java development right before this issue arose. Perhaps this is related.

### Post by DawieS on 2011-09-30
In my case, the problem was caused by the Power Manager, which had some weird interaction with my Epson 915 printer, connected on USB. It seems as if the Power Manager wants to spin down disks, and then run into problems when "spinning down" the SD card slot on the printer. Whenever the machine was left on for a prolonged period (+10 hours), I sometimes had the following symptoms:
- Random freeze/hang-up.
- File Splicing error (Input/Output error) when reading from disks, then mark all partitions as bad, and off-line the disk.
- Some charge build-up on USB ports, causing a hard reset when plugging in a (grounded) external disk.

Three months ago, I removed the Swap partition, and disabled the Power Manager from starting up. The machine is running rock-solid ever since, with none of the above-mentioned symptoms. The only disadvantage is that there is no hibernating ability.

I will now file a bug report, including the log files (pointing to the Power Manager). Since I had several updates since disabling the Power Manager, I will first re-enable it, and check for the re-occurrence of the symptoms, before filing the bug report.

---

### Post by TinEllus on 2011-10-13
Hi all,

 I don’t normally post on forums like this one, but I’m also a ‘victim’ of this illustrous freeze bug and maybe my experiences might add something to the understanding of it.

 First, I switched from windows XP to ubuntu lucid in april 2010, (coincidentally) at the time of the release of 10.04 LTS. Have been using it ever since, without the slightest problem other than beginners mishaps, and  to my greatest satisfaction; have been installing loads of great software and using the machine for ripping, downloading, surfing, text editing, playing music, … Also ran all the updates. Soon, i had running Lucid on three machines in the house: a new laptop, an Asrock HTx00 media center and an old laptop for watching and recording TV. Again, without the slightest problem.

 August 2011 I decided to upgrade ubuntu on my main machine (laptop) to maverick, things didn’t go smoothly (don’t remember exactly anymore what the problem was, but it weren’t the freezes), upgraded further to natty, wasn’t quite happy with Unity and then decided to revert back to lucid.

 And that’s when the trouble started.

 First of all, I erased the entire HD and freshly reinstalled _using the same Live CD I used in april 2010_(!). Immediately updated after the install and the machine started freezing. Generally the samen things happen as generally described in this thread. In fact in my case:
 - freezes several times a day
- seconds before the actual freeze, you see things happening, it runs slower, you know the freeze is coming much like poster beaucoder #1775
- screen is ok, nothing moves (no keyboard, Mouse, mouspad, …) conky freezes completely
- not responsive to on/off switch presses on the laptop, only unplugging the current and removing the battery work
- nothing abnormal in .xsessions-org
- very noticeable: while working: evolution email takes an abnormal long time to start
- sometimes there’s another ‘type of freeze’: Mousearrow moves, i can click and select etc… but programs don’t start(!) log off/turn off/restart not possible, not responsive

 So, what next?

 At times, the machine wouldn’t boot (probably due to frequent hardstops?), I had to rescue it using the live CD (the same, remember, as in 2010) and run fsck. Several times, fixes were necessary and succesfull. 
At one time the machine _froze while running the live CD_!! 
I think this might be essential, and it’s why I believe there’s a hardware problem, that originated sometime while i was using maverick or natty.
 Any comments on this??
 My two other machines have continued running flawlessly up until this very moment, but are not regularly updated.

 Situation right now: a few days ago, i upgraded to kernel 2.6.34-020634-generic. Freezes are much less frequent now (once every couple hours, sometimes days). 
For me it’s manageable, nevertheless, they’re still there! I can live wit it, I’m happy, but still this is unacceptable…
 I think this is very, very sad, ‘ve been using lucid for 16 months to my greatest satisfaction. Now i have to either put up with random freezes or look for something else and start all over again…
 I hope this adds to the understanding of the problem.

 Many thanks to all other posters and good luck!

### Post by eddieray7 on 2011-11-18
My situation turned out to be a hardware problem.  I ran Lucid for a LONG time on the machine and it was coincidental that when I upgraded to Maverick, I started seeing the random freezes.

It's a rather old computer:

ASUS A7N8X-E Deluxe
AMD XP 3200+ (Barton)
2x512MB Corsair TwinX RAMs at 400MHz
etc.

This combination was stable for 6+ years with a number of different Linux versions.  This is one of our office desktops... we built 5 of them with the exact same hardware.  When I started seeing the Linux freezes (complete system lock-up/hang) I took a step back and tried installing a number of different things and at some point tried WinXP.  As it turns out, the WinXP SP1, SP2, and SP3 installer CDs would all blue-screen right before going into the graphical phase.  The error that was presented hinted at possible "memory compatibility" issues but that was kinda sketchy at best.  Eventually, after trying different types of RAMs, different memory timings/voltages/settings, different amounts of RAM, taking out the sound card, swapping in a different video card, etc., etc., I tried changing the RAM to run at 333MHz instead of 400MHz (which unfortunately for this motherboard/CPU combo that also means the CPU runs at a lower MHz than normal due to the particular model of Barton and it's locked CPU multiplier).  Since I backed off the memory speed, the system has been rock-solid... and has been running Maverick for a solid year with absolutely no hangs.

So... this may not be a great solution, but if your motherboard/RAM/CPU combo happens to be "on the bleeding edge" for its architecture cycle (mine was at the top end of the "Athlon XP" architecture for it's era), or is just old in general, it might be a way to alleviate the freezes/hangs.  It seems that my system, over time, has become unable to maintain the full memory speed that it used to.  This is not so surprising since we had a bear of a time getting the right settings when we built these systems - some of the RAMs we got (all the exact same TwinX model) would fail Memtest86 if you let it run all night, some of the RAMs went bad in the first few months, the BIOS incorrectly configured the RAM voltage and timing settings, etc.  It took months to get that all worked out and then the machines ran perfectly for many years.

---

### Post by Hooker Jay on 2011-11-19
Yup, that's old but good hardware! :D 

As for Memtest86, I don't entirely trust it as I've seen cases where a system will be rock solid and stable on every OS thrown at it, but fail Memtest86 within 20 minutes because of dirty power, EFI interference, astral moon phases ... :P 

A buddy of mine saw Memtest86 consistently fail on every system he ever built ... until he started using a UPS. Sure sign that his garage-turned-computer room (if not his entire house) needed to be re-wired. When he had the cash to spare, he went on a binge at Lowes to renovate it proper. Months later, Memtest86 passed every single time with or without the UPS. He couldn't believe it. Refused to believe it until he carried his rig into the kitchen, and Memtest failed, and failed again in the living room, and failed in every room still wired to the old breaker box. He couldn't help shake his head and mutter, "No wonder this house was eating my cell phone chargers and power supplies like PEZ!" ... :lol:

---

### Post by hans1967 on 2011-12-09
Hi Community,

Thank you for consolation. Have not found a german thread about this topic.

My first Linux was Debian 4 stable. As Debian 5 stable was out, I switched over to Xubuntu.

I experience freezes in Xubuntu Lucid Lynx for about half a year. I have installed the proprietary graphic driver.

Hardware: Intel Core Duo 2GHz-32-bit mobile cpu, MSI Intel 945GT MS-9632 workstation mainboard,  4GB DDR2-RAM-memory on mainboard, Sapphire-AMD-Radeon HD5450 PCI-express graphic-card.

Not even wellknown linux three-finger salute  ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_SysRq_key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_SysRq_key)) can save the filesystems  in these cases. Cold boot is then required. Those crashes happen about three times a week. That's a  bad situation.

I think, X-server or X-server-related software have grown too fat again.  Developers have betrayed small-is-beautyful-paradigm once more leaving  the netbook-versions-line. My assumption is well-funded:

I suggest an experiment, everyone here can easily replicate. Burn two  Live-CDs, Puppy-Linux 5.2.5 and 5.2.8, both ubuntu-lucid-based. Play the  fattest high-definition-wmv-file you can find in your collection under  both live-CDs. On my machine, the difference is obvious.

This might be one reason Puppy-Linux's newest release (5.3), surprisingly enough, resorts to Slackware. Puppy-Linux-maintainers are always sensitive to performance, compactness and usability.

Compromise-solution: get complete conserved karmic-mirrors and  karmic-ISOs before financial crisis will shutdown internet  infrastructure for the next 10 years ;-). Just leave the party when it's  most beautiful ;-). Could be that there remain just a few weeks to do  this ;-).

useful link: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=352460](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=352460)

comfortable, resource-sparing, Debian-Squeeze based alternative: [http://crunchbanglinux.org/](http://crunchbanglinux.org/)

Crunchbang is another project which has recently abandoned the Ubuntu-branch.

I am just testing Puppy-Linux 5.2.5 Lucid and Crunchbang Statler Squeeze Live-CDs. Both without proprietary graphic driver. Even Puppy 5.2.5 Lucid is crashing when running under load. X-server and Firefox stress Debian 6 engine too. Also Debian 6 graphics can get very slow. But Debian 6 stays stable. No X-server- or system-crashes under Crunchbang Statler so far. I came from Debian - it seems, I return to Debian.

brainwaves of the crackbrained: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NzkJj7tn41E](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NzkJj7tn41E)

best regards,

hans1967

### Post by wonderfulboy on 2011-12-28
Hardware:
CPU: Pentium4 3.0GHz
Mobo: ASUS P4P800-E Deluxe
RAM: 3GB in Single Channel (2x512MB, 2x1GB Kingston DDR400 changed to 320MHz)
Gfx: ASUS N7600 GS Silent (256MB)
HDD: WDC Caviar Blue (1TB)
Power Supply: Corsair HX520W

Kernel:
2.6.32-35-generic

Drivers:
Gfx: Nvidia Current (195)

For few months I have tried many different options to fix this random freeze for example:
- changing power supply,
- changing graphic card,
- changing nvidia driver,
- completly remove ubuntuone,
- scanning HDD and RAM,
- combining with RAM compositions,
- installing all possible updates,
And many others but nothing helps to fix this. After my system or hardware changes once time I don't have freeze for week. Another time for two weeks, but this freezes always returns. I have downloaded new ubuntu 10.11 but after meeting with this new sweet Unity I have decided to try again with bitter 10.04. Last time freezes was very annoying sometimes few times for one day. Freezes happens always very fast when I turn on System Monitor->Resources.

I don't have freeze only for 3 days but I feel that could be final solution for many people. What I did?

1. I have decreased speed my DDR400 to DDR320 and no freeze!
2. When I try return to DDR400 and increase RAM voltage for a while from "auto" to 2.65 and 2.75 it doesn't help or even get worse.

Now I have DDR320 and I hope so this freezes this time disappears for ever. However If freezes would return or dissapear for longer time (month) I will write about this.

### Post by TinEllus on 2011-12-30
Hi,

An update on my situation:

As I said (Oct 13th): updating to kernel 2.6.34 made it better, but freezes still occured.

For some reason or another, last couple of days the system froze more frequently, yesterday i had 4 freezes and had to perform several file system checks, every time there were errors.

I also noticed the following errors in /var/log/Xorg.0.log:

> (EE) Failed to initialize GLX extension (Compatible NVIDIA X driver not found)> (EE) NVIDIA: Failed to load the NVIDIA kernel module. Please check your
(EE) NVIDIA:     system's kernel log for additional error messages.
(II) UnloadModule: "nvidia"
(II) Unloading /usr/lib/xorg/extra-modules/nvidia_drv.so
(EE) Failed to load module "nvidia" (module-specific error, 0)
(EE) No drivers available.Then found this:

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/X/Bugs/Lucidi8xxFreezes)

Did the 'install an upgraded kernel' (natty backport 2.6.38) et voila...

no more hickups today (for the time being)...

error messages dissappeared from Xorg.0.log

System runs smoothly or now; will keep fingers crossed and everybody informed...

Hope this helped somebody

### Post by wonderfulboy on 2012-02-19
Now I'm pretty sure that in my case it's not Ubuntu10.04 but hardware issue. The reason of my problem is dying motherboard. After few days after my last post (#1820) what I have got:

1) After I have clicked "turn off" my computer and monitor. I heard that HDD turned off but fans was still working. I turned on monitor and I saw that Ubuntu still "thinking" (screen with appearing and disappearing dots). Computer completely turned off after about 30 seconds after that.

2) I think that was strange but once time issue, but second time was the same.

3) Next thing that I tested was settings where always freezes occurs. And freezes returned.

4) After changing memory frequency to 266 Mhz freezes still appears but not so fast like by 320 Mhz.

5) I have tried back to nominal frequency - 400 Mhz and this time monitor screen was black and POWER ON button had orange light like in power saving mode. There wasn't blip sound like always at start and HDD didn't start any loading. Computer resets and even BIOS reset didn't help.

6) After long fighting with all hardware monitor again starts dispaly view (It's not HDD and Ubuntu issue because my HDD was unplugged). What helps? Left one memory chip in motherboard and after few resets display view again appears. After that I have changed memory frequency to 266 Mhz and again place into motherboard all memory chips.


I think it's matter of time that my motherboard will completely crash. In my opinion better be buy old similar or the same motherboard than new, because present hardware is very poor quality. It's often situation that new hardware even high-end after period of warranty time completely crashes.

### Post by TinEllus on 2012-02-19
Hi all,

Sorry for not posting earlier, but reading the previous post, I wanted to let you know that the problem I had was also hardware related.

Lockups/freezes started happening again after a while; what convinces me it's a hardware issue is the fact that freezes also occured while running liveCDs, both ubuntu 10.04, 9.04 and Linux Mint...

Maybe also worth mentioning is the complete 'irrationality' of the freezes: sometimes several times a day, not workable at all, sometimes no problems for weeks. A period of high-frequency freezes would stop after leaving the laptop for 1 or 2 days (if possible); then could run again for days, until the freezes happened again...

Finally turned to buying a new laptop; still a happy Ubuntu user though (Oneiric), although I had been thinking of using an alternative.

Just thought I'd let you know,

Greetz,

### Post by Qbeta on 2012-02-19
> **wonderfulboy said:**
> Now I'm pretty sure that in my case it's not Ubuntu10.04 but hardware issue. The reason of my problem is dying motherboard...

It's been my experience that memory chips are more likely to be bad than motherboards and you mention that memory speed seems to affect your problem.  My current machine is a homebuilt that seems to experience random flake-outs every 10th boot or so.  Running memtest usually finds nothing but about 1/10 times finds bad memory blocks in various places.  So first try memtest a number of times(it's an Ubuntu boot option).  Then see if you can borrow some memory sticks from someone.  Also, get a **good** surge suppressor (not one of those old 6 slot stick types that everyone steals from the office).  Household current can be remarkably unsteady.

Surge Suppressor:
I think I will check this for some time for peace of mind.

My FREEZE TEST that always works:
Turn on: System Monitor with graphical display (memory, cpu use etc.), after some time - once shorter, once longer - It freezes.

### Post by georgezenios on 2012-03-16
ive had the same freeze problems, and after a lot of elimination work i think ive got this down to a specific problem. thought it was opera as this was giving segmentation faults in the logs. removed it but crashed again. wsuspected other programs including libreoffice, shutter etc etc. another suspect gnome keyring so deleted this from gvfs in the home directory. no compiz or anything fancy like that although i do use Xplane flight sim running at 5040x1050 which uses the nvidia 290 driver without any problems - nouveau blacklisted as well. these problems come when im doing normal 2d work.

in the end, i noticed that it crashed a lot of the time if i used the window panel at the bottom of the screen. so ive removed the panel completely and just use alt/tab to go between windows - so far 24 hours without a crash. so well see ill post again in a few days to let you know. 

2.6.32-38-generic 10.04 lts ubuntu
16 cores nehalem 3.7ghz
16gb 1333 triple channel ram
triple sli gtx 260 in AFR configuration
classified sr2 mobo
ocz high I/o ssd
1200w goldAX corsair

### Post by Andrew.Stevenson on 2012-03-19
TO ALL THE PEOPLE HAVING THESE RANDOM FREEZES, WHO HAVE BEEN SUFFERING FOR YEARS NOW, 
AND WHO THINK THAT PEOPLE WRITING IN CAPS-LOCK ARE ANNOYING.

Listen.

Just do this, please. Throw your **PS/2** mouse and keyboard away in a garbage can,
and use a **USB** mouse and keyboard instead. It's a bug in the **KERNEL** code handling the **PS/2** stack, 
and it has been around **2.6.32**. Strangely enough, that's about the same time that I started to experience mental instability in my life.

Now I'm heading back to my backyard to finish burning to ashes my computer.

Bye

I haven't had any problems with 10.04 freezing myself. I think they've fixed some of the issues that were causing it for other people though... is your system updated? You could also try re-installing the gnome components just in case that solves the problem.

### Post by dave2001 on 2012-03-23
> **georgezenios said:**
> thank you dave, hmm im really tempted to try it, but its working so well right now i fear to touch it - actually getting some work done without having to pull my teeth out. 8 days now no crash.....

From your comments here I'm guessing you don't do OS backups. I find this a handy habit to get into with Linux. You never know when an update, modification, etc, is going to mess your system up beyond your ability to recover. There are a lot of free utilities out there that will make a disk image of your OS partition with a liveCD. I prefer to use Paragon Backup and Recovery... but you need a windows system to run the initial Paragon program to burn the liveCD.

### Post by georgezenios on 2012-04-04
hi dave just done one using the script posted by another user earlier really easy to do

#!/bin/bash
S1='b'
S2='y'
echo "Please enter b for backup or r for restore:"
read action

if [ $action = $S1 ]; then 
    
    echo "These Directories will be excluded:"
    echo -e "\033[1m\033[32m /proc /lost+found */backup.tgz /mnt /sys /dev /media"
    echo -e "\033[0m"
    echo "To chance these values edit this shell"
    echo "continue (y/n):"
        
    read action
    if [ $action = $S2 ]; then 
        echo "Backing up PC ~ timestamp " ;date
        tar cpzf backup.tgz --exclude=/proc --exclude=/lost+found --exclude=*/backup.tgz --exclude=/mnt --exclude=/sys --exclude=/media  /
    fi
else
    echo -e "\033[1m\033[31mWARNING: this will overwrite every single file on your partition with the one in the archive!"    
    echo -e "\033[0m"    
    echo "Please enter name and location of the archive to restore:"        
    read fileName
    echo "You are about to restore $fileName do you want to continue?(y/n)"
    read action
    echo -e "\033[1m\033[47mJust sit back and watch the fireworks.This might take a while. When it is done, you have a fully restored Ubuntu system!"
    sleep 5
    echo "start"
    if [ $action = $S2 ]; then 
        tar xvpfz $fileName -C /
        echo "Creating excluded directories"    
        #Just to make sure that all excluded directories are re-created
        mkdir /proc
        mkdir /lost+found
        mkdir /mnt
        mkdir /sys
	mkdir /media
        
    fi    
fi
echo -e "\033[0m"

### Post by kn2013 on 2013-01-19
had a similar issue on a Dell Inspiron 1501 notebook using the ATI RS482 Radeon Xpress 200M graphics driver, where the gnome desktop - well, both the bottom and top panel - would stop responding - and you could only shutdown or restart using the top panel on the top right. This would bring dialog boxes to allow to save all open gedit, openoffice files.

The error logs had many XID collision errors. The issue would occur especially when the notebook had been on for 2-3 days, then when switching between windows - using alt-tab - or by clicking on the bottom panel. However, it occured more often depending on the applications running. 

To resolve - I changed the setting in 'system' --> ''preferences' -->'Appearance' from 'Normal' to 'none' - simple desktop. No random freeze - hang-up since then and the notebook works with many applications running and switching between applications is really smooth and quick, even when the notebook has been 'on' constantly.

this fix may be specific to this particular model of notebook - graphics driver - using ubuntu  10.04 LTS, but worth a try on other hardware that has similar behaviour. Went through all the power management fixes before making this simple tweak and [I]Voilà. 
[/I]

---
